services.name: GST Registration
services.shortDescription: Mandatory and voluntary GST registration support with ongoing compliance options.
services.content:
# GST registration

## Overview

GST registration is how a person or firm in India, or a foreign business where the law allows, gets a **GSTIN** to charge **IGST, CGST,** and **SGST/UTGST,** and to file **GSTR-1, GSTR-3B,** and other returns on **https://www.gst.gov.in.** The usual form is **GST REG-01** in **Part A** and **Part B.** Many applicants complete **Aadhaar-** **based** authentication. When **taxable** turnover crosses the threshold or a **compulsory** case applies, you file within the period the **CGST** Act and **CBIC** updates give, or face late fees and **DRC-** class exposure in serious cases.

## Key features

- One **PAN** can have one **GSTIN** per state where a **place** of **business** exists.
- **Principal** place, **constitution** documents, **PAN,** **bank** account, and **address** proof are the usual uploads.
- You pick **HSN** and **SAC** for your goods and services in line with your business.

## Benefits

- Lawful issue of **tax** invoices, **B2B**-style **ITC** for customers, and access to the **GSTR-1**-**2B**-**3B** stack.
- **E-** way bill and the rest of the **GST** compliance menu where your facts match.

## Process

1. Start **Form GST REG-01,** **Part** **A,** to get a **TRN;** gather **constitution, PAN,** address, and signatory details.
2. Complete **Part** **B** with business details, **bank** account, **HSN** and **SAC** choices, and premises proof.
3. Finish **Aadhaar-** **based** authentication or other checks the portal requests.
4. Reply to any **query** or **SCN-** style notice on the application with fresh evidence.
5. After **GSTIN** is issued, plan **GSTR-1, GSTR-3B,** and **e-** way bill use for your trade.

## Documents

| Item | Use |
|------|-----|
| **PAN** and **constitution** proof | Identity of the entity |
| **Address** for **principal** place and any **additional** places | Jurisdiction |
| **Bank** account | **REG-01** table |
| **Photo** and **ID** of **promoter** / **signatory** | **Aadhaar-** path |
| **Rent** deed or **NOC** for premises | **Address** support |

## Fees (indicative, professional, excl. GST 18%)

| Service | Band (rupees) |
|---------|-----------------|
| Simple **SME** new **GSTIN** | 2,000 to 6,000 |
| **Multi-** state or complex group | 5,000 to 15,000 plus |
| First-time **NRI-** or **NRT-** class hand-holding | 8,000 to 25,000 plus |
| **Government** | **Nil** in the usual online path |

## Applicability

Any person or firm that meets the **turnover** or **nature** of **supply** test, **voluntary** **registration** under the Act, or other **compulsory** class such as some **TDS,** **OIDAR,** and **E**-**commerce**-**operator**-**style** **cases,** in line with the **CBIC** list in force.

## Common mistakes (short)

- Wrong state jurisdiction for your principal place of business.
- HSN and SAC that do not match the goods and services you really supply.

## Frequently asked questions

**Can we have two GSTINs in one state?** Usually one GSTIN per state; extra places in the same state are often on the same registration with additional places, unless the law and portal allow a different path for your case.

**Registration came after the threshold date. Is there a penalty?** A late fee on tax for the time you were liable to be registered and full compliance from the effective date are the main themes; a CA-led note on your facts is best.

**E commerce operator, first time. Any extra forms?** Follow the e commerce and OIDAR class rules and the portal rows for the year, on advice of your professional.

*All service content is for informational purposes. Specific GST advice should be obtained from qualified practitioners based on your business facts, transactions, and the law in force.*

services.name: Professional Tax Registration
services.shortDescription: State‑specific Professional Tax registration including PTEC and PTRC where applicable.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

Professional Tax Registration is a state-level tax registration required for individuals earning income through salary, profession, trade, or business. It is levied by State Governments and Union Territories under their respective Professional Tax Acts. Unlike income tax (central), professional tax is a local tax, and its applicability, rates, and slabs vary by state. It applies to:

- Salaried employees (deducted by employer)
- Self-employed professionals (doctors, CAs, lawyers, freelancers, etc.)
- Businesses and firms employing staff
- Companies operating in applicable states

The tax is either deducted monthly (for employees) or paid periodically by professionals/businesses depending on state rules.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Mandatory Legal Compliance

Ensures lawful operation of business or employment.

### Avoids Penalties

Prevents fines for non-registration or non-payment.

### Required for Business Licensing

Often needed for GST, trade license, and other registrations.

### Systematic Employee Compliance

Helps employers manage payroll deductions legally.

### Simple State-Level Structure

Easy periodic payment system in most states.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### State-Wise Variation

Rules differ significantly across states.

### Recurring Payment Requirement

Must be paid monthly, quarterly, or annually.

### Additional Compliance Burden for Employers

Requires payroll deductions and filings.

### Penalties for Delay

Late payment attracts fines and interest.

### Not Applicable in All States

Some states do not levy professional tax.

## 4) Applicability Across India

Professional Tax is levied only in certain states and union territories.

### Applicable States

- Maharashtra
- Karnataka
- West Bengal
- Tamil Nadu
- Telangana
- Andhra Pradesh
- Gujarat
- Madhya Pradesh
- Assam (limited applicability in certain cases)
- Kerala (varies by local rules)

### Not Applicable in Most Northern States

- Delhi, Uttar Pradesh, Haryana, Punjab, Rajasthan, etc. (generally no professional tax)

### Regulatory Authorities

- State Commercial Tax Department
- Municipal Authorities (in some cases)
- State Revenue Department

## 5) Key Features of Professional Tax Registration

### State-Level Tax System

Governed by individual state laws.

### Employee + Professional Coverage

Applies to both salaried and self-employed individuals.

### Monthly Deduction Mechanism

Employers deduct from employee salaries.

### Slab-Based Tax Structure

Tax amount depends on income level.

### Mandatory Employer Compliance

Companies must register and deduct PT for employees.

### Periodic Filing Requirement

Monthly/quarterly/annual returns depending on state.

## 6) Documents / Information Required

- PAN card of applicant/business
- Aadhaar or identity proof
- Business registration certificate (Company/LLP/Firm)
- GST registration certificate (if applicable)
- Address proof of business premises
- Employee details (for employer registration)
- Salary structure (for payroll registration)
- Bank account details
- Application form as per state authority

## 7) Process / Procedure

### Step 1 — Check State Applicability

Confirm whether professional tax is applicable in the state.

### Step 2 — Select Registration Type

Employer registration or individual professional registration.

### Step 3 — Submit Application

Apply to State Professional Tax Department.

### Step 4 — Document Verification

Authorities verify identity and business details.

### Step 5 — Obtain Registration Certificate

Professional Tax Enrollment/Registration Number issued.

### Step 6 — Monthly Deduction Setup (if employer)

Configure payroll system for PT deduction.

### Step 7 — Periodic Payment & Filing

Pay tax and file returns as per schedule.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### Tax Amount (Varies by State)

- Salaried employees: ₹200 to ₹2,500 per month (approx slabs)
- Professionals: annual slabs from ₹2,500 to ₹10,000+

### Registration Fees

- Usually minimal or nominal (₹0 to ₹500 in many states)

### Late Payment Penalty

- Interest and penalty vary by state laws
- Can include fixed penalty + percentage interest on due amount

### Employer Compliance Cost

- Payroll system setup
- Filing and administrative cost

## Conclusion

Professional Tax Registration in India is a mandatory state-level compliance requirement for individuals and businesses engaged in income-generating activities. It ensures structured tax collection at the state level, payroll compliance for employers, and legal recognition for professionals, making it an essential component of India's indirect tax and employment compliance system.

services.name: TAN Registration
services.shortDescription: TAN registration for TDS/TCS compliance on salaries, professional fees, rent and other payments.
services.content:
# TAN registration

## Overview

TAN (Tax Deduction and Collection Account Number) is a unique 10-character alphanumeric code issued by the Income Tax Department of India to every person or entity that is required to deduct tax at source (TDS, Tax Deducted at Source) or collect tax at source (TCS, Tax Collected at Source) under the provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961. TAN is issued under Section 203A of the Income Tax Act and is mandatory for all tax deductors.

Like PAN, TAN is issued through the authorised agencies NSDL and UTIITSL. The TAN format is: AAAA12345A, 4 letters (first indicates city code, next 3 are random), 5 numbers (sequential), and 1 check digit.

Every employer deducting TDS from employees' salaries, every business making payments to contractors (TDS under Section 194C), every company paying rent above ₹50,000/month (TDS under Section 194-I), and every entity collecting TCS on specified goods and services must obtain a TAN and use it for all TDS/TCS compliance, including TDS payments, TDS returns (24Q, 26Q, 27Q, 27EQ), and TDS certificates (Form 16, 16A, 27D).

Quoting an incorrect or false TAN, or making TDS payments without a TAN, attracts a penalty of ₹10,000 under Section 272BB of the Income Tax Act.

## Applicability

**TAN is mandatory for any person/entity who:**

| TDS/TCS obligation | Who needs TAN |
| --- | --- |
| Deducts TDS on salary | All employers |
| Deducts TDS on contractor payments | All businesses making payments to contractors |
| Deducts TDS on rent | Individuals/HUFs paying rent above ₹50,000/month |
| Deducts TDS on professional/technical fees | Businesses paying professional fees |
| Deducts TDS on interest, dividends | Banks, NBFCs, companies |
| Deducts TDS on property purchase | Individual buyers of property above ₹50 lakh |
| Collects TCS on sale of goods | Sellers of specified goods (Section 206C) |
| Deducts TDS on foreign remittances | Businesses making payments to non-residents |

**Not required for:**

- Individuals deducting TDS on property purchase, they use PAN (Form 26QB) without TAN
- Individuals deducting TDS on rent payment under Section 194-IB, Form 26QC, no TAN needed

## Key features

- Unique 10-digit TAN: Separate from PAN, used exclusively for TDS/TCS compliance
- City-specific first character: The first letter of TAN indicates the city/region of the assessing officer's jurisdiction
- Mandatory quoting: TAN must be quoted on all TDS payment challans (Challan 281), TDS returns, and TDS certificates, failure attracts ₹10,000 penalty
- One TAN per PAN: Generally, one TAN is issued per entity. Large organizations with multiple branches can obtain a separate TAN for each branch, but all TDS returns for a PAN can be filed under a single TAN
- Online application: TAN application (Form 49B) can be filed online through NSDL or UTIITSL portals
- No Aadhaar linkage required: Unlike PAN, TAN does not require Aadhaar linking
- TAN verification: TAN details can be verified on the Income Tax portal, valid TANs show the entity name and jurisdiction
- Traces portal: TDSCPC.gov.in (Tax Deduction and Collection System) is the portal where TDS returns are filed, TDS certificates are generated, and TAN-based compliance is managed

## Benefits

- **Legal TDS compliance:** TAN is the legal identifier for all TDS/TCS activities, without TAN, TDS payments cannot be made through the banking system (banks require TAN for Challan 281)
- **TDS certificate issuance:** Form 16, Form 16A, Form 27D (TCS certificates) can only be generated by entities with a valid TAN, deductees need these certificates to claim TDS credit
- **TRACES portal access:** TAN registration enables access to the TRACES portal for downloading TDS certificates, reconciling Form 26AS, and managing TDS compliance
- **Avoid higher TDS:** Deductees receiving payments without TAN-backed TDS deduction face higher TDS rates, proper TAN compliance benefits both deductor and deductee
- **Payroll compliance:** Employers must have TAN for monthly salary TDS (Section 192), Form 24Q filing, and Form 16 issuance, TAN is the backbone of payroll tax compliance
- **Bank mandate:** Banks process TDS payment through Challan 281 only against a valid TAN, a TAN is operationally essential for any business making significant vendor or employee payments

## Documents

| Document | Individual / HUF | Company / firm |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Form 49B (TAN application) | ✓ | ✓ |
| PAN of the applicant | ✓ | ✓ |
| Name and address of deductor | ✓ | ✓ |
| Aadhaar (for individual applicants) | ✓ | Not required |
| Certificate of Incorporation | Not applicable | ✓ |
| Office address proof | ✓ | ✓ |
| Authorised signatory details | Not required | ✓ |
| Contact details | ✓ | ✓ |

## Process

1. **Apply online (Form 49B)** Visit tin.nsdl.com or utiitsl.com. Click on "Apply for New TAN", "Online Application for TAN (Form 49B)." Select the deductor type (company, firm, individual, etc.).
2. **Fill application details** Complete Form 49B, deductor's name, address, PAN, contact details, and nature of business. For companies, enter CIN and director details.
3. **Pay TAN application fee** Pay the application fee online (₹65 + GST = approximately ₹77 total) via net banking, debit card, or credit card.
4. **Acknowledgement** Upon submission, a 14-digit acknowledgement number is generated. The form can be submitted online (for most cases) or a physical copy sent to NSDL/UTIITSL for cases requiring physical verification.
5. **Processing and TAN issuance** NSDL/UTIITSL processes the application and verifies details. TAN is issued within 7–15 working days. The TAN allotment letter is sent to the registered address.
6. **Register on TRACES portal** After TAN receipt, register on the TRACES portal (tdscpc.gov.in) using the TAN, PAN, and a token number from a filed TDS statement (or for new deductors, use the Conso file of the first return). TRACES registration enables TDS return filing, TDS certificate generation, and TAN-linked compliance management.
7. **Link with banking for Challan 281** Update the TAN with your bank for TDS payment, the bank registers the TAN in their system to process Challan 281 payments.

## Cost and fees

| Service | Government fee |
| --- | --- |
| TAN Application (Form 49B) | ₹65 + GST = ₹77 approx. |
| TAN reprint | ₹50 + GST |
| TAN correction | ₹65 + GST |

| Professional service | Estimated fee |
| --- | --- |
| TAN application assistance | ₹500 – ₹2,000 |
| TAN + TRACES registration setup | ₹1,500 – ₹4,000 |
| TAN correction filing | ₹500 – ₹1,500 |
| Bulk TAN applications (10+) | ₹400 – ₹800 per TAN |

Professional fees exclusive of GST @18%.

## Deadlines

| Activity | Deadline |
| --- | --- |
| TAN application | Before first TDS deduction/payment |
| TDS deposit (monthly) | 7th of following month (30th April for March) |
| TDS return Q1 (April–June) | 31st July |
| TDS return Q2 (July–September) | 31st October |
| TDS return Q3 (October–December) | 31st January |
| TDS return Q4 (January–March) | 31st May |

## Common errors

- Applying for TAN without PAN: TAN application requires the applicant's PAN, applying without PAN leads to rejection
- Using wrong TAN on challans: Quoting an incorrect or a previously surrendered TAN on Challan 281, the TDS credit does not reflect in the deductee's Form 26AS, causing disputes
- Not registering on TRACES after TAN allotment: Many new deductors receive TAN but don't register on TRACES, inability to file TDS returns and generate certificates follows
- Multiple TANs for the same entity: Inadvertently applying for multiple TANs for the same entity, surrender duplicates immediately to avoid compliance complications

## Frequently asked questions

**We are a startup that just hired our first employee at ₹8 lakh annual salary. Do we need a TAN?** Yes. As soon as your company has an employee whose salary income exceeds the basic exemption limit (₹2.5 lakh under the old regime or ₹3 lakh under the new regime), TDS on salary (Section 192) becomes applicable. Even if TDS is zero (because the employee's income after deductions doesn't result in taxable income), the employer must obtain a TAN and file a quarterly TDS return (Form 24Q), even if it is a NIL return. Apply for TAN immediately upon hiring your first employee, before the first salary payment is due.

**We are a freelancer (individual) who makes payments to other freelancers above ₹30,000 per contract. Do we need a TAN?** TDS under Section 194J (professional fees) at 10% is applicable when a person (other than an individual or HUF not subject to tax audit) makes professional payments above ₹30,000 per contract or ₹1 lakh per year to a single contractor. Individual freelancers who are NOT subject to tax audit are generally not required to deduct TDS on professional payments. However, if your professional receipts triggered a tax audit in the preceding year (above ₹50 lakh for professionals), you must deduct TDS and therefore need a TAN. Individual buyers of property above ₹50 lakh and individuals paying rent above ₹50,000/month have specific TDS obligations under Sections 194-IA and 194-IB respectively, but these use PAN-based forms (26QB, 26QC), not TAN.

**Our company received a notice saying we deducted TDS but didn't quote TAN on the challan. What is the consequence and how do we fix it?** Failure to quote TAN (or quoting an incorrect TAN) on TDS payment challans attracts a penalty of ₹10,000 under Section 272BB. To fix the specific challan: file a "Challan Correction" request with the bank or through the OLTAS (Online Tax Accounting System) on the NSDL portal, to update the TAN on the challan. Once corrected, the TDS credit will reflect correctly in deductees' Form 26AS. For the forward-looking compliance, immediately update all payment mandates and accounting systems with the correct TAN. Respond to the notice with the correction evidence and request for penalty waiver based on bona fide mistake.

**Can we use the same TAN for all our offices across different states?** Yes, one TAN can be used for TDS compliance across all offices and branches of the same legal entity, regardless of how many states they are in. However, large organizations sometimes prefer separate TANs for each branch/location for internal tracking purposes, this is permissible. If separate TANs are used for different branches, each branch files its own TDS returns under its respective TAN, and TDS certificates are issued from the respective branch TAN. Ensure all deductors' addresses in TDS returns correctly reflect the office address for the specific TAN to avoid jurisdictional confusion.

**We obtained a TAN 3 years ago but never used it (no TDS obligations materialised). Do we need to do anything to keep it active?** Unlike PAN, TAN does not become "inoperative" from non-use. However, if you have a TAN and made no TDS deductions in any quarter, you should still file NIL TDS returns for those quarters, technically, the obligation to file TDS returns exists for every entity with a TAN, and non-filing (even NIL) can trigger notices. In practice, the TDSCPC system generates notices for TANs that filed returns in previous quarters but stopped filing, assume the deductor has active TDS obligations. If you genuinely have no TDS obligations and won't have any in the foreseeable future, you can apply for TAN deactivation/surrender at your jurisdictional TDS officer.

## Disclaimer

All service content is for informational purposes. Specific income tax advice should be obtained from qualified Chartered Accountants or tax practitioners based on your individual financial situation, entity type, and applicable provisions.

services.name: PF Registration
services.shortDescription: EPF registration for establishments with 20 or more employees or voluntary coverage.
services.content:
The Employees’ Provident Fund (EPF) framework is governed by the Employees’ Provident Funds & Miscellaneous Provisions Act, 1952 and administered by the Employees’ Provident Fund Organisation (EPFO) under the Ministry of Labour and Employment, Government of India.

PF Registration is compulsory for establishments employing 20 or more persons, though voluntary registration is also permitted. Once registered, all statutory compliances under the Act must be followed. PF contributions are calculated on the basic salary (including DA and retaining allowance). Employers and employees each contribute 12% in aggregate, which is deposited into the 12‑digit Universal Account Number (UAN) of each employee.

We provide expert support for assessing applicability and obtaining PF Registration for your establishment.

packages.description: PF Registration support for businesses having fewer than 25 employees.

packages.name: Deluxe
packages.description: PF Registration support for businesses having fewer than 50 employees.

packages.name: Premium

services.name: ESI Registration
services.shortDescription: ESI registration for eligible establishments and employees under the ESI Act, 1948.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

ESI Registration is a mandatory statutory process through which eligible employers enroll their establishment under the Employees' State Insurance (ESI) scheme. This scheme is governed by the Employees' State Insurance Act, 1948 and administered by the Employees' State Insurance Corporation. The ESI framework is designed as a comprehensive social security system, providing medical care and financial benefits to employees in cases of sickness, maternity, disability, and employment injury. Once registered, the employer is required to contribute towards the ESI fund for eligible employees and ensure continuous compliance through contributions and return filings. ESI Registration becomes applicable when:

- An establishment employs 10 or more employees (in most states; threshold may vary)
- Employees earn wages up to ₹21,000 per month (subject to revision)
- The establishment operates within a notified ESI area

Upon successful registration, the employer receives a 17-digit ESI registration number, and each employee is allotted an Insurance Number (IP Number) linked to their ESI records. This ensures seamless access to medical and financial benefits under the scheme. ESI Registration is not merely a procedural requirement—it establishes a structured mechanism for employee welfare, compliance discipline, and long-term workforce security.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Comprehensive Employee Welfare

Provides access to medical treatment, hospitalization, and insurance benefits for employees and their dependents.

### Statutory Compliance Assurance

Ensures adherence to labour laws and avoids legal complications.

### Financial Protection for Employees

Offers monetary benefits during sickness, maternity, or disability.

### Employer Credibility

Enhances reputation as a compliant and responsible employer.

### Access to ESI Infrastructure

Employees can utilize ESI hospitals, dispensaries, and medical facilities.

### Reduced Workforce Risk

Minimizes financial burden on employer in case of employee medical emergencies.

### Employee Retention Support

Improves job satisfaction and long-term retention.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Mandatory Compliance Burden

Requires continuous tracking of contributions and filings.

### Limited Applicability

Applies only to employees within prescribed wage limits.

### Operational Complexity

Requires accurate payroll integration and employee data management.

### Financial Outflow

Employer must contribute a fixed percentage of wages.

### Penalties for Non-Compliance

Delay or non-registration can lead to fines and legal action.

## 4) Applicability Across India (Coverage)

ESI Registration is governed by a central law, but its applicability depends on whether the area is notified under the ESI scheme.

### Applicable in Notified Areas Across India

- North India: Delhi, Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh
- South India: Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh
- West India: Maharashtra, Gujarat, Rajasthan, Goa
- East India: West Bengal, Odisha, Bihar, Jharkhand
- North-East India: Assam, Meghalaya, Manipur, Nagaland, Tripura, Mizoram,

### Arunachal Pradesh, Sikkim

- Union Territories: Chandigarh, Puducherry, Andaman & Nicobar Islands, etc.

### Administering Authority

- Employees' State Insurance Corporation

Who Must Register?

- Factories and establishments with 10 or more employees
- Shops, hotels, restaurants, cinemas, and service entities
- Businesses operating in notified ESI zones

## 5) Key Features of ESI Registration

### Statutory Registration Requirement

Mandatory once eligibility criteria are met.

### Unique Employer Code

17-digit registration number issued to employer.

### Employee Insurance Number

Each employee receives a unique IP number.

### Wage-Based Coverage

Applicable to employees earning up to threshold limit.

### Integrated Digital System

Registration and compliance managed online via ESIC portal.

### Contribution-Based Scheme

Employer and employee contribute monthly.

### Continuous Compliance Obligation

Requires ongoing filing and payment after registration.

## 6) Documents & Information Required

ESI Registration is primarily driven by accurate business and employee data rather than extensive paperwork.

### Employer Details

- PAN of business/entity
- Address proof of establishment
- Certificate of incorporation/registration

### Business Information

- Nature of business activity
- Date of commencement
- Employee strength

### Employee Details

- Employee names and details
- Salary/wage structure
- Aadhaar and bank details

### Supporting Records

- Salary registers
- Attendance records

### Additional Requirements

- Digital signature (for companies/LLPs)
- Bank account details

Accurate information ensures smooth registration and avoids future compliance issues.

## 7) Registration Workflow (Practical Process)

1. Determine Applicability — Check employee count, wage limits, and location eligibility.
2. Access ESIC Portal — Visit ESIC online registration system.
3. Employer Sign-Up — Create login credentials and initiate registration.
4. Fill Registration Form — Enter business, employer, and employee details.
5. Upload Required Information — Provide necessary documents and data.
6. Verification by Authority — System validates details submitted.
7. Generation of ESI Code — 17-digit registration number is issued.
8. Employee Enrollment — Register employees and generate IP numbers.
9. Begin Compliance Cycle — Start monthly contributions and periodic return filings.

## 8) Government Cost / Financial Implications

### Registration Cost

- No government fee for ESI Registration

### Contribution Rates

- Employee Contribution: 0.75% of wages
- Employer Contribution: 3.25% of wages

Total Contribution: 4% of wages

### Penalty & Interest

- Interest on delayed payment: ~12% per annum
- Penalties for non-registration or late compliance

### Additional Costs

- Payroll and compliance management systems
- Professional/consultant fees

### Important Notes

- Registration must be completed within prescribed time after applicability
- Contributions are mandatory once registered
- No renewal required, but ongoing compliance is essential

## Conclusion

ESI Registration is a foundational compliance requirement that integrates businesses into India's social security ecosystem. By ensuring access to healthcare and financial protection for employees, it plays a crucial role in building a secure and sustainable workforce. While the process is streamlined through digital systems, the responsibility of maintaining accurate data, timely contributions, and continuous compliance remains critical. For employers, ESI Registration is not just about meeting legal obligations—it reflects a commitment to employee welfare, organizational integrity, and long-term operational stability.

services.name: Import Export Code (IEC)
services.shortDescription: IEC code registration for businesses engaged in import and export of goods.
services.content:
# Import Export Code (IEC) registration

## Overview

**Import Export Code (IEC)** is a 10-digit identifier issued by the **Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT)**, Ministry of Commerce. It is the primary authorisation to carry on **import** and **export** of **goods** and **services** in India, under the **Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992** and the **Foreign Trade Policy**. From **2021** integration with **PAN**, the **IEC** is typically the same as the entity **PAN** for a single unified identity. There is no expiry of the code itself, but **annual IEC update** on the **DGFT** portal is **mandatory**; failure deactivates **IEC** and blocks trade, banks, and schemes.

**IEC** is checked by **Customs** (**BoE** / **SB**), **banks** for trade remittances, **DGFT** for licences and incentives, and **export promotion councils** for **RCMC**. **Legal** import or export of goods, in the usual path, is not possible without a valid **active** **IEC** where it is required.

**Typical feature snapshot:**

| Feature | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Authority | **DGFT** |
| Act | **FTDR** Act, 1992 |
| Format | 10 character **PAN**-aligned in normal case |
| Validity | Lifetime of code, **annual** **DGFT** compliance |
| Fee | **Nil** (government) |
| Portal | **dgft.gov.in** |
| Processing | Often same day to a few days for clean data |

**Common exemptions (illustrations only):** personal non-commercial imports/exports, certain **government** departments, baggage rules, and specific **FTP** carve-outs, each as per **notification** in force.

## Applicability

| Situation | IEC |
|-----------|-----|
| Company / firm **importing** or **exporting** **goods** | **Mandatory** (usual path) |
| **Commercial** **import** or **export** by proprietor / individual | **Mandatory** where law applies |
| **LLP** / **partnership** in **EXIM** | **Mandatory** |
| **Trust** / **society** in trade | **Mandatory** if not exempted |
| **E-commerce** **goods** to overseas buyers | **Mandatory** in typical marketplace onboarding |
| **Service** **exporters** (registered entity), **FEMA** / **STPI**-linked where applicable | **Mandatory** in typical firm setups |
| **RCMC**, **Advance Authorisation**, **EPCG**, **RoDTEP** | **Prerequisite** **IEC** in usual path |

**Not required (examples, subject to fact and law):** non-trade personal movements, some **government** transactions, and goods exempt under a specific **FTP** line.

**One** **IEC** per **PAN**-entity, **all-India**; one code for all ports and products for that **legal** person.

## Benefits

- **Global trade** gateway: **IEC** is the usual licence layer for **cross-**border **goods** and **EXIM-****linked** **services** in a firm context
- **Lifetime** number (with **annual** **DGFT** **confirm**)
- **Nil** government fee for issue and for standard modify / **annual** update
- **Single** code for all products and ports for that entity
- **Customs** and **bank** process **IEC** on **BoE**, **SB**, and **FEMA-****style** remittances
- **Schemes** and **RCMC** are usually built on a live **IEC**
- **Marketplaces** and **B2B** **credibility** in **EXIM** often expect **IEC**

## Limitations

- **Annual** **April**–**June** update, or **deactivation**
- **PAN** errors flow into **IEC**; **PAN** first, then **IEC**
- **Restricted** or **prohibited** goods need **extra** licences, not only **IEC**
- **SCOMET** and similar need **further** **authorisation** beyond **IEC**
- **Misuse** / identity risk if data is not guarded, mitigated in part by **Aadhaar** / **DSC** controls in current portal design

## Process

1. Create a dgft.gov.in user. Verify e-mail and mobile.
2. Link Aadhaar for the authorised signatory as the portal requires.
3. Open Apply for IEC (Form ANF-2A or the current DGFT form). PAN-linked fields may auto-populate.
4. Complete entity name, address, partners or directors, bank account, and GST if registered.
5. Upload address proof, bank proof, and constitution documents in allowed formats and sizes.
6. Government fee is nil. Submit with Aadhaar OTP (or EVC or DSC as the rule in force requires).
7. Note the application reference (ARN) and track status.
8. On approval, download the e-IEC. Processing is often the same day for clean data; else about two to five working days.
9. After IEC: register on ICEGATE, obtain RCMC if needed, file LUT for zero-rated goods exports, align AD bank, and add STPI if you are in software and SOFTEX rules apply.

## Documents (summary)

| Document | Proprietor | Partnership | Company / LLP |
|----------|------------|------------|---------------|
| PAN | Proprietor | Firm | Company or LLP |
| Aadhaar | Proprietor | Managing partner | Director or designated partner |
| Photo | Yes | Yes | Director |
| Address proof | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Bank proof | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Partnership deed | ,  | Yes | ,  |
| LLP agreement / CoI | ,  | LLP | Yes |
| MoA / AoA | ,  | ,  | Company |
| Board resolution | ,  | ,  | Often for signatory |
| GST certificate | If registered | Yes | Yes |

## Fees

| Item | Government |
|------|------------|
| New IEC | Nil |
| Annual update | Nil |
| Modification | Nil |
| Surrender | Nil |

Professional (indicative, excl. GST 18%): sole proprietor about 1,000 to 3,000 rupees; partnership or LLP 2,000 to 4,000; private limited 2,500 to 5,000; IEC with RCMC or ICEGATE bundle as quoted by your firm.

## Timeline (typical)

| Stage | Time |
|-------|------|
| Document preparation | 1 to 2 working days |
| Portal account and Aadhaar | Same day |
| Submission | Same day |
| Auto approval (clean) | Same day to a few hours |
| Manual review | 2 to 5 working days |
| ICEGATE (next) | 3 to 7 working days |
| IEC with ICEGATE and RCMC | about 10 to 20 working days end to end |

## Use cases (short)

1. Garment exporter: IEC before international fairs, RCMC, ICEGATE, and LUT.
2. E-commerce global selling: marketplaces require IEC for Indian sellers.
3. Import of raw materials: CHA needs your IEC to file Bill of Entry.
4. Software services: IEC for SOFTEX above threshold, FEMA, and GST export reporting on advice.
5. Trust importing donated equipment: IEC as importer, plus exemption route if notified.

## Frequently asked questions

**Freelancer, only services, need IEC?** Light structures sometimes operate without; registered businesses doing formal exports, higher SOFTEX, or FEMA-linked flows usually need an IEC. Your CA should align entity type and bank path.

**Name mismatch reject?** Match DGFT form text exactly to PAN. Fix PAN with IT if it differs from RoC, then reapply.

**Multiple IECs for divisions?** One PAN, one IEC for one company. Separate legal persons get separate IECs.

**No annual update for years?** Check status on DGFT; if deactivated, complete overdue annual update, often with back years as the portal allows.

**Personal import of a collector bike?** Often personal or baggage rules apply; if commercial later, IEC may be needed. Confirm HS code and intent with a CHA.

*All service content is for informational purposes. Specific import-export compliance advice should be obtained from qualified DGFT and customs practitioners and CHAs based on your products and structure.*

services.name: FSSAI Registration
services.shortDescription: Food business registration and licensing (Registration, State and Central License).
services.content:
# FSSAI registration (Basic, FoSCoS)

## Overview

FSSAI Registration is the foundational compliance requirement for any food business operating in India. It is governed by the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, and administered by the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI). The authority sits under the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India, and is the apex body for food safety standards nationwide.

FSSAI authorisation has three broad tiers: Basic Registration (petty / small), State License (mid-size), and Central License (large, import, or multi-state). This page is about Basic FSSAI Registration for petty food business operators (PFBOs) with annual turnover up to 12 lakh.

Operating without the right FSSAI authorisation is a punishable offence under the FSS Act, with fines of up to 5 lakh and, in serious cases, imprisonment. Registration is the legal base to run your food business and to build customer trust, including on e-commerce and aggregator platforms that ask for a valid FSSAI number.

## Key features

- Single-window applications on the FoSCoS (Food Safety Compliance System) portal: foscos.fssai.gov.in (confirm the live URL and product name on the FSSAI site).
- A 14-digit FSSAI number to display on products, packaging, and digital listings where rules require it.
- Eligibility: Basic Registration is for turnover up to 12 lakh in the FSSAI / FoSCoS framework for that category, subject to product and activity conditions.
- Validity: usually 1 to 5 years at the FBOs choice, with government fee charged for the period chosen.
- No physical inspection in many Basic Registration files; decisions are document-led (confirm current FoSCoS practice and local authority guidance).

> [!TIP] If turnover crosses 12 lakh or you add a high-risk category, plan an upgrade to State or Central License. Running on a Basic Registration when the law expects a License is a compliance gap.

## Applicability

**Basic Registration (this page) typically fits:**

- Petty food manufacturers with annual turnover not exceeding 12 lakh.
- Small retailers, hawkers, itinerant vendors, temporary stall holders.
- Home-based food (cloud kitchen, tiffin, home bakers) within the PFBO / turnover line.
- Cottage industry food producers, small dhabas and roadside outlets within the same band.
- Small-scale food storage, and low-turnover distributor / supplier / retail where Basic applies.

**State License (not this page) when:**

- Turnover is between 12 lakh and 20 crore, and the activity and scale match State licensing, for example many mid-size manufacturers, processors, restaurants, hotels (up to 3-star in many guides), and caterers (for example up to 100 persons in common references).

**Central License when:**

- Turnover above 20 crore, importers, exporters, multi-state operations, and other cases the FSS Act / FSSAI licensing regulations assign to the Central line (for example FCI-approved warehouses, large cold chain, as the rules in force list).

FSSAI Registration is pan-India across all states and union territories. State Food Safety Departments issue State and Central work through the FoSCoS system and local offices as FSSAI prescribes. Basic Registration is processed through FoSCoS in the usual design for PFBO; confirm any district-level nuance in your state.

## Benefits

- Legal authorisation to run the food business and lower risk of fines, raids, and closure orders.
- Trust: the 14-digit FSSAI number on packs and in listings supports credibility.
- E-commerce: platforms such as Zomato, Swiggy, Amazon Fresh, BigBasket, and Flipkart Grocery typically require a valid FSSAI before onboarding.
- Scalability: a clean Basic file helps when you later upgrade to State or Central for growth.
- Finance: some banks and NBFCs look for FSSAI for working capital in food.
- FSSAI logo: registered FBOs can use the FSSAI logo as per FSSAI display rules.
- Schemes: government food-processing programmes (e.g. PM-FME, PMKVY food modules) often need valid FSSAI.
- Lower product-liability and complaint risk when you are aligned with FSS standards.

## Limitations

- Turnover cap: above 12 lakh, you need the right State (or in some cases Central) path, not Basic.
- Not every activity is allowed on Basic: import, some manufacturing scales, and high-risk categories can need State or Central, whatever the turnover.
- Basic is premises-based: a new place usually needs its own authorisation, not the same 14 digits copied across unlinked premises.
- You must upgrade when the business outgrows Basic; continuing on Basic is a violation.
- Proprietary, nutraceutical, novel, infant, and other high-risk categories are generally off Basic.
- No import: imports need Central in the standard licensing map.

## Process

1. **Category:** Fix annual turnover and activity (manufacture, storage, sale, import, etc.) and match Basic v. State v. Central in FoSCoS and FSSAI guidance.
2. **FoSCoS account:** Create and verify your business user on FoSCoS.
3. **Form B (Registration):** Complete business, products, and premises, and add proprietor, partner, or director data as the form requires.
4. **Upload:** Add photo, ID, address proof, product list, bank detail, and any NOC, deed, or MoA the portal asks for your entity type.
5. **Fee:** Pay the Basic Registration government fee for the years you select (1 to 5 years) online.
6. **Processing:** The Registering Authority reviews; Basic often runs without a site visit (see FoSCoS and local SOPs).
7. **Certificate:** Download the Registration Certificate and the 14-digit number from FoSCoS.
8. **Display:** Show the certificate at the premises, print the number on products, packs, menu boards, and marketing, and on aggregator and marketplace profiles.

## Documents required

| Document | Notes |
|----------|--------|
| Photo of the applicant | Recent passport size |
| Photo ID | Aadhaar, Voter ID, passport, or driving licence |
| Address proof of business premises | Electricity bill, rent agreement, NOC from owner, as accepted on FoSCoS |
| Proof of business activity / declaration | Type of business and product list |
| Form B (FoSCoS) | Completed online as per the portal in force |
| Bank details | Cancelled cheque or passbook as required |
| List of food products | Category-wise list of articles you handle |
| NOC from municipality or panchayat | Where the authority asks for it |
| Partnership deed / MoA | For firms and companies; not for every individual sole file |

## Government fees and validity (indicative, confirm on FoSCoS)

| Type | Turnover (typical guide) | Government fee (per year) | Validity (years) |
|------|--------------------------|----------------------------|------------------|
| Basic Registration | Up to 12 lakh | 100 | 1 to 5 |
| State License | 12 lakh to 20 crore | 2,000 to 5,000 (varies by activity) | 1 to 5 |
| Central | Above 20 crore / importers, etc. | 7,500 | 1 to 5 |

You choose 1 to 5 years; the fee is for the full chosen period. Longer periods reduce the number of renewals.

## Professional fees (indicative, plus GST at 18%)

| Service | INR range (typical) |
|---------|----------------------|
| Basic FSSAI Registration, end to end | 1,500 to 3,000 |
| State License application support | 3,000 to 8,000 |
| Central License application support | 8,000 to 20,000 |
| Document prep and review | 1,000 to 2,500 |
| Upgrade from Registration to License | 2,500 to 5,000 |

Government fees are paid to FSSAI / FoSCoS online. Our fee is for assistance only.

## Renewal

- File renewal before the certificate end date; start at least 30 days before, earlier if you are busy in peak season.
- Use FoSCoS renewal with the same Registration number.
- Late filing after expiry can attract late fee of 100 per day in the usual FoSCoS rule set (check current circular).
- If the registration lapses, you may need a fresh file and can lose continuity of the same 14 digits until a new one is issued.
- Update all documents and product list if details changed. Renewals are often processed in about 30 days in deemed-approval type flows, subject to the authority, category, and pending queries in your case.

## Frequently asked questions

**I sell home-made pickles on Instagram. Do I need FSSAI?** Yes, if you sell food commercially, including on social, WhatsApp, or marketplaces, you need valid FSSAI. Below 12 lakh turnover, Basic is usually the line if products are eligible. Listings often need the number, and unlisted or non-compliant sales can draw penalties and platform takedown.

**One registration for more than one kind of business at the same address?** If every activity is at the same address and the combined scale fits Basic, you can cover multiple activities in one file when FoSCoS allows that bundle. A different address, even nearby, is usually a separate application.

**How long for Basic?** Commonly 7 to 10 working days from a complete set; 3 to 5 days in smooth cases. If the authority does not act in 7 days in the Basic flow described in FSSAI material, a deemed-approval may apply, subject to the notice in force. State and Central are slower (often 30 to 60 days or more when inspection is in play).

**Cloud kitchen, delivery only?** Yes, cloud kitchens are in scope. Display FSSAI on packs and on aggregators. Above 12 lakh, State is usually required.

**What can Basic make or sell?** Many everyday categories, for example cereals, pulses, milk at small scale, fruit and vegetables, small-scale meat where licensed that way, bakery, spices, oil, many beverages, and small-scale packaged water. Infant food, health supplements, proprietary foods, some additives, and other high-risk lines usually need a higher license type even at low volume.

---

*Information only, not FSS Act or FoSCoS application advice for your case. Check FoSCoS, FSSAI, and the Food Safety office in your state for the live rule set.*

services.name: Trade License
services.shortDescription: Municipal trade/shop license (e.g. Gumasta) for local business operations.
services.content:
## 1) Overview

A Trade License is a legal certificate issued by the local municipal authority that permits a business to carry out a specific trade or commercial activity within a defined jurisdiction. It ensures that the business complies with local laws, safety standards, and ethical practices. Trade Licenses are governed by state specific municipal laws and are issued by authorities such as Municipal Corporations, Municipal Councils, or Panchayats depending on the location of the business. The primary objective of a Trade License is to regulate business activities and prevent public health hazards, nuisance, and illegal operations. Unlike registrations such as GST or company incorporation, a Trade License is location specific and must be obtained from the local authority where the business operates. A Trade License grants permission to conduct a specific activity at a particular premises and does not establish ownership of the business itself.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Legal Authorization

- Provides official permission to conduct business
- Ensures compliance with local municipal regulations

### Public Safety Assurance

- Ensures businesses follow safety, hygiene, and operational standards
- Reduces risks to public health and environment

### Business Credibility

- Enhances trust among customers, vendors, and authorities
- Acts as proof of lawful operation

### Avoids Legal Penalties

- Prevents fines, closure, or legal action by authorities

### Financial and Operational Support

- Often required by banks and financial institutions while processing business loans
- Helps in obtaining other registrations and approvals

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Location Restriction

- Valid only for the specific premises mentioned in the license

### Limited Scope

- Covers only the approved business activity
- Any change requires modification or fresh license

### Renewal Requirement

- Needs periodic renewal, usually annually

### Compliance Burden

- Subject to inspections and local authority regulations

In certain jurisdictions, operating without a Trade License may lead to strict enforcement actions including sealing of premises.

## 4) Applicability Across India (State wise Coverage)

Trade License applicability is decentralized and varies across states and local bodies, but it is mandatory across India.

### Applicable in All Regions

- North India: Delhi, Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Uttarakhand
- South India: Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh
- West India: Maharashtra, Gujarat, Rajasthan, Goa
- East India: West Bengal, Odisha, Bihar, Jharkhand
- North East: Assam, Meghalaya, Manipur, Nagaland, Tripura, etc.
- Union Territories: Chandigarh, Puducherry, Andaman and Nicobar, Lakshadweep

### Issuing Authorities

- Municipal Corporation for large cities
- Municipal Council for smaller towns

- Gram Panchayat for rural areas

The rules, fee structure, and validity periods can differ significantly depending on the city and local authority.

## 5) Key Features of Trade License

### Local Authority Approval

- Issued by municipal bodies

### Activity Specific

- Valid only for the type of business approved

### Premises Based

- Linked to a specific business location

### Time Bound Validity

- Usually valid for 1 year and renewable

### Inspection Based

- Authorities may inspect premises before issuing license

Some municipalities classify Trade Licenses into categories such as food trade, manufacturing, and hazardous activities, each with different compliance requirements.

## 6) Documents Required

### Basic Documents

- Identity proof of applicant
- Address proof of applicant
- Passport size photograph

### Business Related Documents

- Proof of business address such as rent agreement or ownership proof
- NOC from property owner
- Layout plan of premises

### Additional Documents if applicable

- Certificate of incorporation or partnership deed
- Previous Trade License for renewal
- NOC from fire department for certain businesses

In certain cities, additional approvals such as health department clearance may be required, especially for food related businesses.

## 7) Process / Procedure

### Step by Step Trade License Process

### 1. Identify jurisdiction

### Determine the local municipal authority

### 2. Fill application form

### Online or offline depending on the city

### 3. Submit required documents

### 4. Pay applicable fees

### 5. Inspection by authorities

### Conducted if required based on business type

### 6. Approval and issuance

### License is granted by the authority

### 7. Display license

### Must be displayed at the business premises

Many municipalities now offer fully online systems, making the process faster and more accessible.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

Trade License fees vary depending on city, nature of business, and size of premises.

### Typical Fee Range

- Small businesses: ₹500 to ₹2,000 per year
- Medium businesses: ₹2,000 to ₹10,000 per year
- Large or hazardous businesses: ₹10,000 and above

### Additional Charges

- Renewal fees annually
- Late penalty charges for delayed renewal

- Inspection fees in some jurisdictions

Fees are often calculated based on factors such as floor area, type of business, and risk category rather than a fixed rate.

services.name: Digital Signature Certificate (DSC)
services.shortDescription: Class II and Class III Digital Signature Certificates with USB tokens.
services.content:
# Digital signature certificate (Class 3 DSC)

## Overview

A **Digital Signature Certificate (DSC)** is the electronic equivalent of a physical signature, issued by a **Certifying Authority (CA)** licensed by the **Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA)** under the **Information Technology Act, 2000**. A DSC authenticates the holder and enables secure, legally valid electronic signing of documents, forms, and transactions.

**Class 3** is the class issued in India for regulatory signing. **Class 1** and legacy **Class 2** (new issuance for signing discontinued from January 2021) are not suitable for most **MCA, GST,** and **e-tender** work. The private key is stored on a **FIPS 140-2** certified **USB** cryptographic token so it cannot be copied off the device in normal use.

## Key features

- Legally valid for filings under the **IT Act, 2000** when issued by a **CCA**-licensed **CA** (eMudhra, Sify, NeSL, (n)Code, NSDL, and others).
- **Class 3** is the standard for company and professional regulatory signing.
- **Video KYC** (live verification) is used for identity proof in the usual flow.
- **Individual** and **organisation**-tagged use cases, depending on product.
- **Signing only** or **signing and encryption** options.
- **Aadhaar eSign** is a separate cloud product; not every portal treats it the same as a **USB** token **DSC** for a given form.

## Benefits

- **MCA, income tax, GST, EPFO, ICEGATE,** FSSAI, **DGFT,** and **e-tender** (GeM, CPPP, state portals) often require or strongly expect **Class 3 DSC.**
- **Non-repudiation** and document integrity: tampering after signing is detectable.
- **Faster** than physical couriers and wet signatures for government channels.
- **Directors and professionals** can sign from a compliant workstation without being present in an office.
- **Audit** trails on registries and portals for compliance.

## Limitations

- If the **USB token** is **lost** or **stolen**, the certificate must be **revoked** and a **new** **DSC** obtained; the private key is not restorable.
- **Drivers** and browsers are still **Windows**-leaning; **Mac** and **Linux** can be clunky.
- **Renewal** every **1–2** years; a missed renewal can **block** filings mid-process.
- A **DSC** is **not transferable**; when a director changes, the new person needs a new **DSC.**
- **eSign** may have **transaction** or **category** limits and may not be accepted on **MCA** for director-class signing.

## Process

1. **Choose a CA** (price, support, token compatibility with your PC and the portals you use).
2. **Apply online** for **Class 3**, **signing** and/or **encryption**, **1** or **2** year validity, and a **new** or **reused** token.
3. **Upload** PAN, Aadhaar, photograph, and for organisation DSC the CoI, board resolution, and GST if the CA asks.
4. **Complete video KYC** (PAN and Aadhaar to camera) as the CA requires.
5. **Pay** the **CA** fee and **token** cost if applicable.
6. **Install** the token driver, **test** a sample signature and **MCA/portal** registration as needed.
7. **Register** the **DSC** on each portal (MCA, income tax, GST, EPFO, ICEGATE) per that portal’s steps.
8. **Set** reminders **60+** days before **expiry** to renew in time for statutory deadlines.

## Documents required

| Document | Individual DSC | Organisation or director DSC |
|----------|---------------|-----------------------------|
| PAN | Yes | Yes |
| Aadhaar | Yes | Yes |
| Photograph, email, mobile | Yes | Yes |
| Video KYC | Yes | Yes |
| Certificate of Incorporation, board resolution, GST (if needed) | No | Yes for designated org DSC / role |

Foreign directors can usually obtain an Indian **Class 3** **DSC** with **passport**-based KYC, overseas address, and **PAN** as required by the **CA**.

## Fees (indicative)

| Item | CA fee (approx.) |
|------|------------------|
| Class 3 signing, 1 year | ₹800 – ₹1,500 |
| Class 3 signing, 2 years | ₹1,200 – ₹2,500 |
| Class 3 signing + encryption, 2 years | ₹1,500 – ₹3,000 |
| USB token (one-time) | ₹500 – ₹1,000 |
| Renewal | ₹800 – ₹2,000 |

| Professional (est.) | Fee |
|---------------------|-----|
| Procurement and setup | ₹500 – ₹1,500 |
| Portal registration of DSC | ₹500 – ₹1,000 |
| Bulk (10+ DSCs) | ₹400 – ₹800 per DSC |

Fees vary by **CA**. Professional fees are often exclusive of GST @ 18%.

## Applicability

**Directors,** designated **partners,** **CAs, CS,** and **authorised** signatories for GST, **EPFO,** **ICEGATE,** e-tendering, and **EXIM** businesses. **Pan-India;** KYC is typically online. No state-wise restriction on issuance.

## Validity

**1** or **2** years from issue. Renew before **expiry**; on renewal, a new certificate is loaded (same or new token). If a filing hits **expiry** during submission, the filing may **fail,** so renew well ahead of year-end and statutory **due** dates.

## Frequently asked questions

**Can one USB token hold DSCs of multiple people?** Standard practice is one **DSC** (one person) per **token** for security and non-repudiation. Do not **share** tokens.

**What if the token is lost or stolen?** Contact the **CA** to **revoke** the certificate immediately, then apply for a **new** **DSC** on a **new** token.

**Can a foreign national director get an Indian DSC?** Yes, with **passport-** based **video** KYC, **PAN,** and documents the **CA** lists for **non-resident** applicants.

**Is Aadhaar eSign a substitute for USB token DSC?** eSign is valid for many uses, but **MCA** company filings typically require a **Class 3** **USB** **DSC.** **GSTN** and income tax have broader eSign acceptance. Check each **portal** before you rely on eSign.

**The DSC expired and I missed an MCA deadline. What now?** Renew or buy a new **DSC** as soon as possible, file with **late** fees as applicable to the form, and set **reminders** for the next **cycle**.

*All content is for informational purposes. Specific government registration, tax, and compliance advice should be obtained from qualified professionals based on your entity’s circumstances, state, and business activities.*

services.name: Health License
services.shortDescription: Municipal Health License for specified trades and activities in addition to Trade License.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

A Health License in India is a mandatory approval issued by the Local Municipal Authority or Health Department that permits a business to operate in a manner that complies with public health, hygiene, and sanitation standards. It is commonly required for businesses that directly or indirectly impact public health such as:

- Restaurants, hotels, and food outlets
- Bakeries and catering services
- Food manufacturing and packaging units
- Hospitals, clinics, and healthcare facilities
- Beauty salons, spas, and wellness centers (in many states)
- Water and beverage handling units

The primary purpose is to ensure that business operations do not pose any risk to public health or hygiene.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Legal Permission for Health-Sensitive Operations

Allows lawful operation of food, hospitality, or health-related businesses.

### Ensures Public Safety Compliance

Maintains hygiene and sanitation standards.

### Improves Business Credibility

Builds trust among customers and authorities.

### Mandatory for Many Licenses

Required for FSSAI, trade, and other approvals.

### Reduces Legal Risks

Prevents penalties or shutdown orders.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Strict Hygiene Standards

Must maintain continuous cleanliness and compliance.

### Frequent Inspections

Municipal health officers may conduct surprise checks.

### Documentation Requirements

Regular reporting and renewal needed in many cases.

### Location-Based Restrictions

Certain areas require additional approvals.

### Non-Compliance Penalties

Violations can lead to fines or closure.

## 4) Applicability Across India

Health licenses are issued by Municipal Corporations, Municipal Councils, or Health Departments, and requirements vary by city and state.

### Common Applicable Businesses

- Restaurants and food outlets
- Hotels and lodging establishments
- Food manufacturing units
- Catering businesses
- Hospitals and clinics
- Spas, salons, and wellness centers
- Water bottling and beverage plants

### Regulatory Authorities

- Municipal Corporation / Local Body (Primary Authority)
- State Health Department
- Public Health Inspectorate
- Food Safety Department (linked in many cases)

## 5) Key Features of Health License

### Public Hygiene Compliance Approval

Ensures adherence to sanitation standards.

### Mandatory for Food & Hospitality Sector

Essential for restaurants and hotels.

### Periodic Inspection System

Regular checks by health officers.

### Location & Infrastructure Compliance

Facility must meet hygiene requirements.

### Linked with Other Licenses

Often required alongside FSSAI, trade license, etc.

### Renewable Authorization

Needs periodic renewal based on local rules.

## 6) Documents / Information Required

- Application form for health license
- Identity proof of owner
- Business registration certificate
- Property ownership or rental agreement
- Layout plan of premises
- Water quality report (in some cases)
- Waste disposal system details
- Fire safety certificate (if applicable)
- GST registration (if applicable)
- NOC from local municipal authority

## 7) Process / Procedure

### Step 1 — Business Setup & Hygiene Planning

Ensure premises meet sanitation standards.

### Step 2 — Application Submission

Apply to Municipal Health Department.

### Step 3 — Document Verification

Authorities verify business and premises details.

### Step 4 — Site Inspection

Health inspectors evaluate hygiene conditions.

### Step 5 — Compliance Corrections (if required)

Rectify issues pointed out during inspection.

### Step 6 — Approval of License

Health license granted upon compliance.

### Step 7 — Fee Payment

Pay applicable municipal health fees.

### Step 8 — License Issuance

Official approval for operations granted.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### License Fees

- Varies by city, business type, and size
- Small establishments: lower fees
- Large hotels/hospitals: higher fees

### Typical Range (India)

- ₹500 to ₹25,000+ annually depending on local municipal rules

### Additional Costs

- Hygiene infrastructure setup
- Waste management systems
- Periodic inspection compliance
- Staff training in hygiene standards
- Renewal charges

### Penalty Risk

- Operating without license: fines + closure
- Hygiene violations: penalties or suspension
- Non-compliance with inspection: sealing of premises
- Public health violations: legal action

## Conclusion

A Health License in India is a municipal-level approval that ensures businesses comply with essential hygiene, sanitation, and public health standards. It is a fundamental compliance requirement for food, hospitality, and health-related industries, ensuring safe operations and protecting public well-being under local regulatory frameworks.

services.name: Proprietorship
services.shortDescription: Simple proprietorship setup with MSME, TAN and optional GST & GST filing.
services.content:
Proprietorship is the simplest form of business in India, owned and managed by a single individual (the proprietor) who retains all profits. It is typically suited for micro, small and medium enterprises in the unorganised sector.

To establish business identity, a proprietorship should obtain Udyog Aadhaar / MSME Registration; without it, banks and other stakeholders may not recognise the entity as a formal business. Trade License (where applicable) and TAN Registration for TDS/TCS on payments may also be required.

We provide expert support and guidance for forming a proprietorship in your desired business name and obtaining the necessary registrations.

packages.name: Premium

services.name: Partnership
services.shortDescription: Formation and registration of partnership firms with deed drafting and GST options.
services.content:
A partnership business is a more organised form of business than a proprietorship and is governed by the Partnership Act, 1932. Two or more partners come together to carry on business and typically execute a Partnership Deed which governs profit‑sharing, management, banking operations, admission and retirement of partners and other key terms.

We provide comprehensive support in drafting a robust Partnership Deed and registering your partnership firm with the concerned authorities, along with optional GST setup and compliance.

packages.name: Standard
packages.description: Expert support in drafting the Partnership Deed and registering your partnership business.

packages.name: Deluxe

services.name: One Person Company (OPC)
services.shortDescription: Incorporation of One Person Company with DSC, DIN, PAN, TAN, GST and more.
services.content:
A One Person Company (OPC) combines the benefits of a private limited company with the simplicity of a single owner structure. It offers separate legal entity status, limited liability, easier funding options and eligibility for benefits available to small scale industries.

Key advantages include minimum compliance compared to larger companies, single ownership with quick decision‑making, limited liability protection, eligibility for SSI benefits such as easier bank funding and concessional interest, and enhanced trust and prestige. OPCs require at least one shareholder, one director (who can be the same person), one nominee and must suffix “(OPC) Private Limited” to their name.

We provide complete support in forming an OPC in line with your preferred name and MCA requirements.

packages.name: Standard
packages.description: Formation of OPC with authorised capital up to ₹1 Lakh, 2 DSC, 1 DIN, 1 name approval, Certificate of Incorporation, PAN, TAN and GST Registration (government fees and stamp duty extra).

packages.name: Deluxe
packages.description: Formation of OPC with authorised capital up to ₹10 Lakhs, 2 DSC, 1 DIN, 1 name approval, Incorporation Certificate, PAN, share certificates, TAN and GST Registration (government fees and stamp duty extra).

packages.name: Premium
packages.description: Everything in Deluxe plus MSME Registration and GST Return filing for 3 months (government fees and stamp duty extra).

services.name: Limited Liability Partnership (LLP)
services.shortDescription: Incorporation of LLP with DSC, LLP agreement, PAN, TAN and optional GST & TDS filing.
services.content:
A Limited Liability Partnership (LLP) is a hybrid business structure combining features of a company and a traditional partnership. It provides limited liability to partners while allowing operational flexibility through an LLP Agreement.

An LLP is a separate legal entity with perpetual succession. Partners’ liability is generally limited to their agreed contribution, and they are insulated from liabilities arising out of other partners’ wrongful acts or misconduct. Compared to companies, LLPs enjoy more contractual flexibility, a simpler governance structure and lower compliance burden.

We provide end‑to‑end support in forming LLPs, drafting LLP Agreements, obtaining approvals and setting up tax registrations and compliance.

services.name: Private Limited Company
services.shortDescription: Incorporation of private limited companies with DSCs, DINs, PAN, TAN, GST and MSME.
services.content:
A Private Limited Company is a widely used corporate structure in India, offering separate legal entity status, limited liability, perpetual succession and better access to funding. Shares are privately held and transfer is restricted, with a cap on the number of members as per the Companies Act, 2013.

Key characteristics include minimum 2 members and 2 directors, limited liability for shareholders, perpetual succession irrespective of changes in ownership, no need to issue a public prospectus and flexibility in internal governance. Private companies must use “Private Limited” in their name.

We provide expert guidance and complete support in forming Private Limited Companies aligned with your preferred name and MCA norms.

services.name: Public Limited Company
services.shortDescription: Incorporation of public limited companies with higher capital and compliance needs.
services.content:
A Public Limited Company is a corporate form that allows raising capital from the public and provides limited liability to its shareholders. It requires at least 7 members and 3 directors and must comply with stricter regulatory norms compared to private companies.

Public companies enjoy advantages such as unrestricted number of members, easier transferability of shares and enhanced transparency. They must use “Limited” in their name and typically issue a prospectus for inviting public subscription.

We offer complete support in forming Public Limited Companies including approvals, documentation and tax registrations.

services.name: Nidhi Company
services.shortDescription: Formation of Nidhi companies for mutual benefit deposits and loans among members.
services.content:
A Nidhi Company is a type of non‑banking financial company (NBFC) recognised under Section 406 of the Companies Act, 2013. Its primary business is accepting deposits from and lending to its members for their mutual benefit. Common examples include permanent funds and mutual benefit funds.

Nidhi Companies focus on supporting small and medium income groups and operate on principles of mutual assistance. They must comply with specific requirements around number of members, net owned funds, unencumbered term deposits and share issuance, and can only deal with members.

We provide end‑to‑end support in forming Nidhi Companies including documentation, incorporation and initial share allotment.

packages.name: Standard
packages.description: Formation of Nidhi Company with 10 Class II DSC, 3 DIN, 1 name approval, incorporation fees, PAN and TAN Registration plus company kit.

packages.name: Deluxe

packages.name: Premium
packages.description: Deluxe package plus GST or Trademark Registration as required.

services.name: Producer Company
services.shortDescription: Formation of producer companies for primary producers and agribusinesses.
services.content:
A Producer Company is a company registered under the Companies Act to carry on activities relating to production, harvesting, procurement, grading, pooling, handling, marketing, selling and export of primary produce of members, or import of goods and services for their benefit. It may also process produce and supply machinery, equipment or consumables to members.

Producer Companies are treated as private limited companies with no cap on the number of members and cannot be converted into public companies. They are particularly suited for farmer collectives, agri‑businesses and producer institutions.

We provide complete support in forming Producer Companies including name approval, incorporation, issue of shares and initial compliance.

packages.name: Standard
packages.description: Formation of Producer Company with 10 Class II DSC, 10 DIN, 1 name approval, incorporation fees, PAN and TAN Registration, company kit and share certificates.

packages.name: Deluxe
packages.description: Standard package plus one year of TDS Return filing.

packages.name: Premium
packages.description: Deluxe package plus Trademark Registration.

services.name: Section 8 Company
services.shortDescription: Formation of not‑for‑profit Section 8 companies for charitable and social objectives.
services.content:
Section 8 Companies are not‑for‑profit entities registered under the Companies Act, 2013, with the primary objective of promoting arts, commerce, science, research, education, sports, charity, social welfare, religion, environment protection and similar causes. They apply their profits solely towards furthering their objects and do not distribute dividends to members.

These companies enjoy several privileges and exemptions due to their charitable nature and do not require a minimum share capital. Famous examples include FICCI and CII.

We provide expert guidance and support in forming Section 8 Companies, including licensing from the Central Government and all related compliances.

services.name: Indian Subsidiary of Foreign Company
services.shortDescription: Formation of Indian subsidiaries and wholly owned subsidiaries of foreign companies.
services.content:
A subsidiary company is controlled by a parent (holding) company, which may be incorporated outside India. When a foreign company owns at least 50% of the equity in an Indian entity, the latter becomes its subsidiary; if it owns 100%, it becomes a wholly owned subsidiary.

Wholly owned subsidiaries of foreign companies in India are often set up through the automatic route where 100% FDI is permitted and no prior RBI or government approval is required. Such subsidiaries are separate legal entities and must comply with Indian company law and tax regulations.

We provide comprehensive support in forming Indian subsidiaries of foreign companies, including documentation for foreign promoters, regulatory compliance and ongoing tax filings.
