services.name: OPC to Public Limited Conversion
services.shortDescription: Assistance for rare cases of OPC transitioning straight to public limited.
services.content:
# OPC to public limited company conversion

## Overview

An **OPC** can become a **public limited company** when the goal is **public fund-raising**, a future **listing**, or a **broad** shareholder base. The route is often **two steps**: **OPC to private (INC-6)**, then **private to public (MGT-14 + INC-27 / articles)**, or a path your **RoC** accepts as **consolidated** in practice. **Direct** OPC to public is **possible in law**; in practice, **staged** conversion is **common**.

> [!WARNING] **Compliance jump**
> A public company carries the **heaviest** ongoing compliance: minimum members/directors, independent directors, secretarial/CSR/SEBI paths if **listed**, and more **disclosure**.

## Key features

- **Capital markets**: Access to **public** investors and, when listed, **BSE/NSE** visibility.
- **Share liquidity**: **Freer** transfer of shares (subject to law and **listing** if applicable).
- **Scale**: **Unlimited** shareholders; room for **institutional** and **retail** capital.

| Stage | Core filings (typical) |
|-------|-------------------------|
| OPC to private | **INC-6** with amended MOA/AOA; at least 2 Sh + 2 Dir |
| Private to public | **MGT-14** (special resolution) + **INC-27** + altered MOA/AOA |
| Commencement | **INC-20A** and other post-incorporation steps as applicable |

## Benefits

- **Public issue** and **prospectus** path (where applicable) and **larger** investor pool.
- **Brand** and **governance** perception with **lenders**, customers, and partners.
- **Path to listing** (SEBI, DRHP, merchant bankers) when you are ready, **separate** from the **conversion** itself.

> [!NOTE] **Limitations**
> **7** shareholders and **3** directors minimum; **independent** directors, **secretarial** audit, **CSR** (if thresholds), **SEBI LODR** if **listed**. **Listing** is **6 to 18+ months** of work and **cost**. IPO **founder** sales trigger **CGT**; **ESOPs** have **perquisite** and **sale** tax angles.

## Process

1. **Convert OPC to private limited**: **INC-6**, amended **MOA/AOA**, at least **2** shareholders and **2** directors.
2. **EGM** and **special resolution** to convert to **public**; file **MGT-14** within the prescribed window.
3. **File INC-27** (or the applicable alteration route) with **public** company **MOA/AOA**.
4. Appoint at least **7 shareholders** and **3 directors** (with **DIR-2/DIR-12** as needed) **before or with** the certificate.
5. **RoC** issues **public limited** **Certificate of Incorporation**; **CIN** continues through the chain of **form** changes.
6. **INC-20A** (commencement) if required; if **listing** is the goal, start **SEBI** and **exchange** work **separately**.

## Documents required

| Document | Requirement |
|----------|-------------|
| Certificate of incorporation (OPC) | Existing |
| MOA & AOA (OPC) | Existing |
| Board resolution for conversion | Signed |
| Altered MOA & AOA | For public limited |
| INC-6 (OPC to pvt) | MCA filing |
| INC-27 (pvt to public) | MCA filing |
| MGT-14 (special resolution) | After shareholder approval |
| Financial statements (latest audited) | CA certified |
| DSC of all directors | Class 3 |
| NOC from creditors | Signed |
| Consent of new directors (DIR-2) | Minimum 3 as needed |
| PAN, Aadhaar of new directors | KYC |

## Cost / fees

| Item | Fee (indicative) |
|------|------------------|
| INC-6 (OPC to pvt) | Rs. 1,000 to Rs. 5,000 |
| INC-27 (pvt to public) | Rs. 5,000 to Rs. 15,000 |
| MGT-14 | Rs. 300 to Rs. 600 |
| MOA/AOA stamp duty | Rs. 2,000 to Rs. 15,000 |
| DSC per new director | Rs. 1,500 to Rs. 2,500 |
| Professional fees (CA/CS) | Rs. 20,000 to Rs. 60,000 |

**Listing** and **SME** board norms are **separate** costs; **SME** paid-up band (indicative) and **main board** minimums are **in guidance only**, **verify** with current **SEBI** and **exchange** rules.

## Tax impact (summary)

**OPC to public** through **same** **PAN/CIN** is **tax-neutral** at the **entity** level: **no** deemed transfer in the **standard** chain. **IPO** proceeds in the company are **not** business income. **Founders** selling in **IPO** have **CGT**; **ESOP** at **exercise** and **sale** follow **perquisite** and **CGT** rules.

## Applicability

**OPCs in India** (all major states, **RoC** as per **jurisdiction**). **Listing** is on **BSE/NSE** (and **SME** boards) with **nationwide** membership **rules**.

## Frequently asked questions

**Must OPC go private first?** **Law** allows **direct** paths in some cases; **practice** is often **two step**. Confirm with your **CS** and **RoC**.

**Need 7 shareholders at conversion?** **Yes** for a **valid** public company; plan **evidence** before the **public** certificate.

**SEBI** immediately? **SEBI/LODR** matters mainly when **listed** or doing **securities** activities; **unlisted** public has **no** same **obligations** as **listed**.

**Name on conversion?** **"Private"** is **removed**; **extra** name change may need **INC-24** and **separate** fees.

**Minimum capital for** IPO? **SME** boards use **paid-up** **bands** in **SEBI** rules, **not** a single static number, **read** the **current** **master** circular and **NSE/BSE** pages.

services.name: Private to OPC Company Conversion
services.shortDescription: Restructuring a small private company into OPC form.
services.content:
# Private limited to OPC conversion

## Overview

A **private limited** company can convert to a **One Person Company (OPC)** under **Section 18 of the Companies Act, 2013** read with **Rule 6(4) of the Companies (Incorporation) Rules, 2014**. The route is uncommon but valid: one person ends up with **all** the shares, and the constitution is aligned to a **single-member** form while the **same CIN and PAN** continue in most cases.

> [!TIP] **When it is used**  
> After a co-founder exits, when one shareholder will hold **100%** of the equity, or when you want a **simpler** meeting and filing rhythm (for example, **MGT-7A** in place of full **MGT-7**, and no AGM) while keeping limited liability and an incorporated shell.

## Key features

- **Special resolution** and **Form MGT-14**; **Form INC-6** with **amended** MOA and AOA for an **OPC** cap table and governance.
- **Form INC-3** nominee **consent**; **NOC** from **secured** creditors if term debt is on the books.
- **One shareholder**; **at most 15** directors. **No FDI** in an OPC. **Rule 6(4)**: a private company must exist for at least **2 years** before it may **voluntarily** convert to OPC (confirm the current rule text when you file).

| Point | What to check |
|-------|----------------|
| Pre-conversion cap table | **All** issued equity should sit with one person, or a documented buy-out must complete first |
| FDI and scale | OPC cannot take **FDI**; if you need investors, plan a later **OPC to private** path |
| Re-conversion | If the business outgrows one-owner or hits statutory triggers, a **pvt ltd** re-conversion is a separate process |

## Benefits

- **Abridged** annual return and **no AGM** in the **OPC** pattern (per current rules; verify forms each year on MCA).
- **Same** legal person: **CIN and PAN** typically continue, so **bank**, **contracts**, and much of **KYC** stay on a single identifier subject to name line updates.
- **Governance** matches **operational** reality (one **economic** owner) while you retain a **body corporate**.

> [!NOTE] **Limitations**  
> **One** shareholder only; no **ESOP** path inside OPC like a full private limited. You must appoint a **nominee**. **Voluntary** conversion is **not** available within **2 years** of incorporation of the private company under **Rule 6(4)**. If you buy out a co-founder, **that** **sale** can trigger **capital gains** for the **seller**, separate from the **form** **change** of the company.

## Process

1. **Consolidate** shareholding so **one** person holds **all** the shares, or **complete** a **valid** **transfer** to that effect.
2. **Call an EGM** (or use the **sole** **member** path where the Act and **articles** allow) and pass a **special resolution** for **OPC** conversion and **M&A** amendments.
3. **File MGT-14** for the **special** **resolution** within the **statutory** time limit (typically **30 days**; confirm the current window on the live **form** and rules).
4. **File INC-6** with amended **MOA/AOA**, **INC-3** (nominee), **NOC** from creditors if there is **secured** debt, **audited** accounts, and other annexures the live **E-form** requires on **MCA**.
5. On approval, the **RoC** issues an amended **Certificate of Incorporation** as an **OPC**; **CIN** usually **continues**.
6. **Update GST, MSME, IE Code**, and **counterpart** agreements where the old **"Private Limited"** wording no longer matches the **register of members**.

## Documents required

| Document | Requirement |
|----------|-------------|
| Certificate of incorporation (pvt ltd) | On hand |
| MOA & AOA (pvt ltd) | To be altered for OPC |
| Special resolution (EGM) | As per company law and articles |
| MGT-14 | Filed for the resolution |
| INC-6 | Application for conversion |
| NOC from creditors | If secured facilities exist |
| Audited financial statements | Latest year |
| Nominee consent (INC-3) | Executed by nominee |
| PAN, Aadhaar of sole shareholder | KYC |
| DSC of director | Class 3 |
| Declaration (INC-9) | If applicable in your filing set |

## Cost / fees

| Item | Fee (indicative) |
|------|------------------|
| INC-6 filing | Rs. 1,000 to Rs. 5,000 |
| MGT-14 | Rs. 300 to Rs. 600 |
| MOA/AOA stamp duty | Rs. 500 to Rs. 3,000 (varies by state) |
| DSC (renewal or new) | Rs. 1,500 to Rs. 2,500 |
| Professional fees (CS/CA) | Rs. 10,000 to Rs. 25,000 |
| PAN update | Nil (same PAN) |

## Tax impact (summary)

A straight **pvt ltd to OPC** form change is **tax-neutral** at the company level: **same PAN and CIN**, no deemed balance-sheet transfer. A **separate** share **buyout** to reach a single owner is taxed under **income** tax and **FEMA** rules for that transaction. **Loss** carryforward and **DPIIT**-type benefits **continue** if the underlying **eligibility** tests are still met. **GST** and **TAN** usually continue; **re-KYC** with banks and **portals** after the **amended COI** is received.

## Applicability

**Private limited** companies under the **Companies Act, 2013** in all states and UTs, filed with the **jurisdictional RoC** through the current **MCA** online workflow.

## Frequently asked questions

**Can a company with two shareholders convert?** **Yes**, but only after **one** person holds **100%** of the shares. Any buy-out should be **documented** before you file the conversion set.

**Is there a two-year waiting period?** **Rule 6(4)** requires the private company to have been in existence for **at least two years** before **voluntary** conversion to OPC. Confirm the current notification text on the date of filing.

**Is a nominee mandatory?** **Yes**. The nominee’s consent in **Form INC-3** is a core filing.

**Is stamp duty payable?** Stamp duty on **MOA/AOA** alteration **varies** by state. A pure **CIN-continued** form change is **not** the same as a **land** or **conveyance** event by itself; your CS will map duty to the **amendment** in your state.

**What becomes simpler?** **No** AGM in the **OPC** model; abridged **MGT-7A** instead of full **MGT-7** where the rules allow. **Statutory** audit and **income** tax still apply on normal triggers.

services.name: Partnership to Company Conversion
services.shortDescription: Transformation of partnership firms into private/public companies.
services.content:
# Partnership to company conversion

## Overview

A **registered** or **unregistered** partnership can become a **private** or **public limited company** under **Section 366 of the Companies Act, 2013** read with the **Companies (Authorised to Register) Rules, 2014**. Growing family or trading businesses use this to obtain **limited liability**, stronger **corporate** governance, and the tools to **raise equity** and bid for work that points at a **CIN**-based entity.

> [!TIP] **When it fits**  
> When a deed-only firm needs **tenders** and **bank** comfort, you want to **onboard** investors or key staff with **shares** or **ESOPs**, or you are ready for a **separate** legal person for **succession** and **funding** planning.

## Key features

- **Form URC-1** with new **MOA** and **AOA**; a **new company CIN**; partners are mapped to **shareholders** in a fixed **ratio**.
- **Unanimous** partner consent and **NOCs** from **secured creditors** are standard on this route.
- **Two** newspaper advertisements (English and **regional** as rules require) to give creditors and the public a clear **objection** window on the URC-1 file.

| Pillar | What you get |
|--------|--------------|
| Resolutions and NOCs | Confirms **unanimity** and **lender** sign-off for the **conversion** |
| URC-1 and ads | **MCA**-visible file with the **stated** **objection** **period** |
| Certificate | A **new** company is **born**; the **partnership** is **dissolved**; **migrations** of tax and **licence** **IDs** follow the **BTA/URC** design |

## Benefits

- **Limited liability** and **perpetual succession**; ability to **list**, issue **shares**, and follow **FDI** routes where the sector table allows a **company**.
- **ESOPs** and a governance stack that many **institutional** **lenders** and **investors** expect at scale.
- **Value** is held in **shares** for **succession** and **M&A** planning.

> [!NOTE] **Limitations**  
> The path needs **unanimity**, **NOCs**, and time. Ongoing **compliance** is **higher** than for a **firm**. You will **re-align** **PAN**, **GST**, **MSME**, **FSSAI**, and other **IDs**; the new company **often** has a **new** **PAN** (the firm’s **old** one does **not** **carry** **as**-**is**).

## Process

1. **Partners' resolution** agreeing to convert the firm into a **company**.
2. **NOC** from all **secured** **creditors**.
3. **Advertisements** in **two** **newspapers** (English and **regional** as **rules** require).
4. **Statement of assets and liabilities** certified by a **CA**; **audited** **accounts** (e.g. last **3** years) as **filing** **asks**.
5. **File** **URC-1** with the **RoC** with **MOA**, **AOA**, **ads**, and **affidavits** / **declarations** as **prescribed**.
6. The **RoC** issues a **Certificate of Registration** of the company; the **partnership** is **dissolved** by **force** of **law** on the **stated** **date**.
7. Open **new PAN** for the company; register or move **GST** (including **ITC-02** on a qualifying going-concern transfer), **bank** accounts, and all **licences**; close the firm’s old IDs where the law requires.

## Documents required

| Document | Requirement |
|----------|-------------|
| Partnership deed (registered or unregistered) | As applicable |
| Consent of all partners | Written and signed |
| NOC from secured creditors | Original |
| Statement of assets & liabilities | CA certified, within 6 months where required |
| Audited financial statements | Last 3 years, as required |
| Form URC-1 | Filed online with MCA |
| MOA & AOA of new company | Drafted and stamped as per state |
| DSC of all proposed directors | Class 3 |
| PAN, Aadhaar of partners and directors | KYC |
| Address proof of registered office | Utility + NOC + rent |
| Declaration by partners | Affidavit as prescribed |
| Proof of newspaper advertisement | Two newspapers (English and regional) |

## Cost / fees

| Item | Fee (indicative) |
|------|------------------|
| Form URC-1 | Rs. 3,000 to Rs. 10,000 (capital-based) |
| Newspaper advertisement | Rs. 2,000 to Rs. 8,000 |
| MOA/AOA stamp duty | Rs. 2,000 to Rs. 15,000 |
| DSC per director | Rs. 1,500 to Rs. 2,500 |
| Professional fees (CA/CS) | Rs. 15,000 to Rs. 50,000 |
| ROC filing fees | Rs. 1,000 to Rs. 10,000 |
| PAN (new company) | Rs. 107 |

## Tax impact (summary)

**Section 47(xiii)**: **tax-neutral** if **all** **assets/liabilities** of the **firm** become the **company’s**, **all** **partners** take **shares in proportion to capital**, they hold at least **50%** **voting** for **5** **years**, and **no** **partner** gets **any** **consideration** other than **shares**. A **break** in those **rules** in the **5-year** **window** can **tax** the **gains** in the **breach** **year**. **Goodwill** and **revaluation** can **attract** **CGT** if **dealt** in **differently**. **GST** on **going**-**concern** **moves** is often **exempt** under **12/2017**-style **entries**; run **TDS** and **cross**-**entity** **transactions** with your **CA** from **day** **one** after **birth**.

## Applicability

**Partnership** **firms** under **Section 366** in **all** **states** and **UTs**; **filing** with the **correct** **RoC** for **the** **proposed** **registered** **office** **jurisdiction**.

## Frequently asked questions

**Can an unregistered firm convert?** **Yes**, the **URC-1** **path** is **framed** to **include** **unregistered** **firms** in **provisions**; **ads** and **NOCs** still **apply** in **full**.

**Are two newspaper ads mandatory?** **Yes** for the **standard** **Section** **366** **process** as **laid** **down** (one **English** and one **regional** **in** the **state** **language** **where** **required**).

**What about the firm’s PAN?** The new company has its **own PAN**. The old firm’s PAN is **surrendered** or **inactivated** as NSDL/department rules and your CA advise.

**What about staff and PF/ESI?** A **BTA**-backed handover aims for **continuity of employment**; re-map **EPFO/ESI** and obtain fresh **employer** identifiers for the company where the authorities require that.

**Must partners put new cash?** Not necessarily. Book **capital** can be converted into **share capital** in the **agreed ratio** through the **MOA** scheme and **allotment** **resolutions** your **company secretary** implements.

services.name: LLP to Company Conversion
services.shortDescription: Assistance with converting LLPs into company structures.
services.content:
# LLP to company conversion

## Overview

Converting an LLP into a private limited or public limited company is governed by **Section 366 of the Companies Act, 2013** read with the **Companies (Authorised to Register) Rules, 2014**. A registered LLP can move into a company structure while its business, assets, liabilities, and contracts stay aligned with the new entity. The conversion dissolves the LLP; a new company **inherits operations without a break in business continuity**.

> [!TIP] **At a glance**
> Ideal when you need equity investment, ESOPs, or company-only eligibility (DPIIT, tenders, FDI) while the LLP form no longer fits.

## Key features

- **Full continuity**: Assets, liabilities, and contracts are carried into the new company on approval.
- **Equity and ESOPs**: A company can issue shares and run employee stock option plans; LLPs cannot offer the same.
- **Investor and tender fit**: VCs, PE, and many tenders expect a company, not an LLP.
- **MCA process**: Filing (including Form URC-1) and regional stamp duty, NOC, and professional support apply.

| Feature | What it means for you |
|--------|------------------------|
| Statutory base | Section 366 + URC-1 process with the Registrar. |
| Partner consent | **Unanimous** partner consent; one dissenting partner can block. |
| Creditor NOC | NOC from **secured** creditors is mandatory. |
| New CIN | You receive a **new** company CIN; the LLP is formally closed. |

## Benefits

- Issue **equity** and raise funds from angels, VCs, and institutions.
- Use **ESOPs** for retention; stronger **credit** and **limited liability** with perpetual succession.
- **DPIIT** startup recognition and cleaner **valuation** for investors.
- **FDI** on the automatic route is often easier in a company than in an LLP.

> [!NOTE] **Limitations to plan for**
> The process is **lengthy** and MCA-heavy. Compliance **rises** after conversion versus an LLP. Unanimity and creditor NOC are strict.

## Process

1. **Partners' resolution** consenting to conversion (unanimous).
2. **NOC** from all **secured** creditors.
3. **Statement of assets and liabilities** certified by a CA (not older than 6 months).
4. **File Form URC-1** with MOA, AOA, and supporting documents with the **Registrar** (CRC/MCA as applicable).
5. **Certificate of registration** of the company is issued; the **LLP is deemed dissolved** from that date.
6. **File Form 14 (LLP)** to intimate **dissolution** to the LLP Registrar.
7. Apply for **fresh PAN, TAN**; **update** bank, GST, and other registrations.

## Documents required

| Document | Requirement |
|----------|-------------|
| LLP agreement (latest amended) | Filed copy from MCA |
| Certificate of incorporation (LLP) | Original |
| Consent of all partners | Board resolution / written consent |
| NOC from secured creditors | Original signed |
| Statement of assets & liabilities | CA certified, not older than 6 months |
| List of partners with addresses | Self-attested |
| Declaration by designated partners | As prescribed |
| Form URC-1 | Prepared for filing |
| MOA & AOA of proposed company | Drafted |
| PAN, Aadhaar, address proof of directors | KYC |
| DSC of all proposed directors | Class 3 |
| Latest filed Form 8 & Form 11 | From MCA portal |
| Proof of registered office | Utility bill + NOC + rent agreement |

## Cost / fees

| Item | Fee (indicative) |
|------|------------------|
| Form URC-1 filing | Rs. 5,000 to Rs. 10,000 (based on capital) |
| MOA / AOA stamp duty | Rs. 2,000 to Rs. 15,000 (state-based) |
| DSC per director | Rs. 1,500 to Rs. 2,500 |
| PAN (new company) | Rs. 107 |
| Professional fees (CA/CS) | Rs. 15,000 to Rs. 50,000 |
| ROC filing fees | Rs. 1,000 to Rs. 10,000 |

## Tax impact (summary)

Treated as a **tax-neutral** transfer under **Section 47(xiiib)** of the Income Tax Act, 1961, when conditions are met: all assets/liabilities of the LLP become those of the company, partners become shareholders **in proportion**, partners keep **at least 50% voting** for **5 years**, and no partner takes consideration **other than shares**. Breach within 5 years can **revoke** the exemption. **Stamp duty** on property transfer is often not applicable if conditions are met. **GST**: transfer as a **going concern** can be exempt under **Notification 12/2017** subject to conditions.

## Applicability

For **LLPs** registered under the **LLP Act, 2008** in **all Indian states** and **Union Territories**. Application is made to the **Central Registration Centre (CRC)** of the **MCA** (or as per current MCA routing).

## Frequently asked questions

**Does the LLP need to be wound up first?** No. The LLP is **automatically dissolved** on the new company's **Certificate of Registration**; the company **inherits** assets, liabilities, and contracts.

**Can tax losses of the LLP carry forward?** Yes, if **Section 47(xiiib)** and **Section 72A** conditions are met.

**Is unanimous partner consent mandatory?** **Yes**, one dissenting partner can block the conversion.

**What happens to GSTIN?** The LLP's **GSTIN is cancelled**; the company needs **fresh registration**. **ITC** can be moved with **Form GST ITC-02** where allowed.

**How long does it take?** Often **30 to 60 days**; NOC, stamp duty, and MCA load can **extend** this.

services.name: Private to Public Company Conversion
services.shortDescription: Converting private limited companies into public limited companies.
services.content:
# Private to public limited company conversion

## Overview

A **private limited** company becomes a **public limited** company by altering its **MOA** and **AOA** under **Section 14 of the Companies Act, 2013**. The change is used when the board plans a wider shareholder base, a future **IPO**, or contracts that need **"Limited"** without **"Private"** in the name.

> [!TIP] **When to use it**  
> You expect **more than 200** shareholders, want to issue a **prospectus** or build toward **BSE/NSE** listing, or need a **public** company profile for **large** tenders and **institutional** **comfort**.

## Key features

- **EGM** and **special resolution** (at least **75%** votes) for **conversion** and **M&A** change.
- **MGT-14** and **INC-27** (or the applicable current **MCA** e-form) with public-company **MOA/AOA**.
- **Minimum 7** shareholders and **3** directors before or when the new certificate is issued. **"Private"** is **dropped** from the legal name in the **normal** case.

| Area | Public company note |
|------|---------------------|
| Transfer of shares | Freer transfer; still read **class** and **agreements** for any **restriction** on a **set** of **securities** |
| Governance | Heavier: independent directors, secretarial audit and CSR at **stated** **thresholds** |
| Listing | **Converting** to public and **listing** are different; SEBI and exchanges **govern** an **IPO** when you **choose** that path |

## Benefits

- A **larger** shareholder base and a path to **prospectus**-based and **public** **capital** when you choose.
- **Same PAN and CIN** in the standard conversion, so **continuity** of **books** and **credits** on one **entity** id.
- **Name** line drops **"Private"**; rest of the **name** is unchanged unless you run a **separate** **name** change.

> [!NOTE] **Limitations**  
> Ongoing **compliance** is **higher** than for a pvt ltd. **SEBI/ exchange** and **DRHP** work apply **only** if you **list**; a **public** CIN without listing still has a **higher** **governance** bar than a **pvt** **ltd** at similar scale.

## Process

1. **Board** then **EGM** pass a **special resolution** for conversion to **public** and **M&A** amendment.
2. **File MGT-14** within the statutory window (commonly **30 days**; confirm the live form).
3. **File INC-27** with **amended** **MOA/AOA** for a **public** company.
4. Ensure at least **7** shareholders and **3** directors; use **DIR-2/DSC** and **DIR-12** as applicable for new appointees.
5. **RoC** issues an amended **COI**; **"Private"** is **removed** from the **registered** name (e.g. **ABC Private Limited** becomes **ABC Limited**).
6. **INC-20A** and other post-incorporation steps as applicable; **update** **GST**, **lenders**, and **KYC** with the new **M&A** and **name** line.

## Documents required

| Document | Requirement |
|----------|-------------|
| Certificate of incorporation (pvt ltd) | Existing |
| MOA & AOA (pvt ltd) | To be replaced by public version |
| Altered MOA & AOA (public) | Drafted |
| Special resolution (EGM) | Passed |
| MGT-14 | MCA filing |
| INC-27 | Application for conversion |
| List of shareholders (min. 7) | Updated cap table |
| Consent of new directors (DIR-2) | As needed |
| DIR-12 (new directors) | If appointing new directors |
| Audited financial statements | Latest year |
| PAN, Aadhaar of new directors | KYC |
| DSC of all directors | Class 3 |

## Cost / fees

| Item | Fee (indicative) |
|------|------------------|
| MGT-14 | Rs. 300 to Rs. 600 |
| INC-27 | Rs. 5,000 to Rs. 15,000 |
| MOA/AOA stamp duty | Rs. 2,000 to Rs. 15,000 (state) |
| DIR-12 per new director | Rs. 300 to Rs. 600 |
| DSC per new director | Rs. 1,500 to Rs. 2,500 |
| Professional fees (CA/CS) | Rs. 15,000 to Rs. 50,000 |

## Tax impact (summary)

**Same** **entity**: **no** new **CIN** in a **typical** private to public conversion. **PAN** and **CIN** **continue**; unabsorbed **losses**, **depreciation**, and **credits** roll on if the **income** **tax** **continuity** **conditions** for that company are met. A simple **M&A** restyle to **"Limited"** is **broadly** **tax**-**neutral** at the **entity** level. **STT** and **securities**-driven **TDS** follow **securities** **trading** and **listing** work, not the **act** of **dropping** **"Private"** from the **name** alone. **ESOPs** and **securities** **perquisites** are taxed when they **arise** under the **IT** **Act**; reconfirm **115BAA** / **115BAB** and **DPIIT**-linked **positions** with your **CA** on the **filing** **date**.

## Applicability

**Private** **limited** **companies** under the **Companies Act, 2013** in **all** **states** and **UTs**, filed with the **jurisdictional** **RoC** on the **current** **MCA** **portal** **journey** **in** **force** when you file.

## Frequently asked questions

**Can a public company exist without an IPO?** **Yes**. **Listing** and **converting** to **public** are **independent** steps.

**How many** **shareholders and directors?** **At least** **7** **shareholders** and **3** **directors** for a public **limited** company (listed companies have **extra** **board** **and** **SEBI** **rules** when **applicable**).

**Must the name change?** The word **"Private"** is **removed** (for example, **ABC Private Limited** to **ABC Limited**). A **further** **name** **change** needs its **own** **MCA** process.

**Is shareholder approval** **required?** **Yes**, a **special** **resolution** (typically **75%** **voting** **power**) at an **EGM** is **required** to **authorise** **conversion** and **M&A** **amendment**.

**Can a public** **limited** return to **private?** A **listed** company may be **delisted** under **SEBI** rules and may **later** **convert** to **private** with **NCLT**-backed or **simplified** **paths** as **applicable**; **treat** it as a **separate** **governance** project with your **CS**.

services.name: Public to Private Company Conversion
services.shortDescription: Re‑conversion of public limited companies into private companies.
services.content:
# Public to private limited company conversion

## Overview

A public limited company can reclassify as a private limited company by altering the M&A under Section 14 of the Companies Act, 2013. A proviso in Section 14(1) can require NCLT approval if the company has made a public issue of shares or has more than 200 shareholders. Listed companies usually complete a SEBI-regulated delisting before the RoC re-styles the name to add "Private" on the same CIN.

> [!TIP] **When to use**  
> After a de-listing, a take-private, or when you need pvt ltd governance, transfer restrictions, and a "Private" in the legal name on the same CIN.

## Key features

- MGT-14 and INC-27-class e-forms (V3 e-form set in force on the filing date) with M&A for a private company.
- NCLT if the Section 14(1) proviso matches your cap table and history; confirm the live proviso and rules with your company secretary.
- If BSE or NSE-listed, SEBI delisting and settlement with public holders typically comes before the pvt M&A and COI re-style on the CIN-continued file.

| Track | What to expect |
|-------|-----------------|
| Unlisted, 200 or fewer shareholders, no public offer | Simpler EGM, MGT-14, and INC-27-class path with the RoC, subject to the live MCA user manual. |
| Proviso facts or a listed name | NCLT order and/or SEBI delisting, then the pvt re-style; sequence and notices are part of a tight CS file. |

## Benefits

- The word "Private" in the name and a M&A that matches pvt ltd rules, usually on the same CIN.
- Share transfer restrictions in the AOA, which a pvt ltd can use as the Act and your deed read on the filing date.
- Lower public-disclosure and SEBI-LODR load after a de-list, versus a main-board listed CIN, subject to the facts in your re-style file.

> [!NOTE] **Limitations**  
> NCLT time and cost if the proviso applies. SEBI delisting is long and data-heavy for listed names. Public deposits, if any, must be repaid or squared before you lock the pvt re-style. Minority rights in NCLT and SEBI paths need a fair process.

## Process

1. With your CS, read whether the Section 14(1) proviso applies: public offer of shares or a cap table with more than 200 shareholders, as the Act in force on the filing date states.
2. If listed, run SEBI delisting in the order the regulations require; pay fair exit to public holders in the SEBI path you choose.
3. EGM: pass a special resolution for the pvt re-style and M&A change. File MGT-14 in the time the Act and form text give you.
4. If NCLT: petition, notices to shareholders and creditors, and a bench order, then file the order with the RoC as the manual requires.
5. File INC-27-class and M&A in the pvt set; the RoC re-issues or amends the COI so the name has "Private Limited" in the pvt class on the CIN-continued line, in the normal CIN-continued pvt re-style.
6. Update GST, banks, and CERSAI- and counterparty KYC; if you de-listed, close STT- and-listed-KYC on the bourses as the bourse and SEBI ask.

## Documents required

| Document | Requirement |
|----------|-------------|
| Certificate of incorporation (public) | In hand |
| M&A, existing and altered | AOA with pvt set |
| Special resolution (EGM / postal ballot) | As required |
| MGT-14 | Filed in time |
| INC-27 | Or the current V3 e-form in force |
| NCLT order | If proviso or court route applies |
| SEBI / exchange delisting proof | If listed first |
| List of shareholders (max 200 for pvt) | Updated if proviso and fact pattern need it |
| NOC from secured creditors | If term debt and lenders ask for it in your file set |
| Audited financials | Current year, as the form asks |
| DSC of directors | Class 3 |

## Cost / fees

| Item | Fee (indicative) |
|------|------------------|
| MGT-14 | Rs. 300 to Rs. 600 |
| INC-27 | Rs. 5,000 to Rs. 15,000 |
| NCLT filing (if any) | Rs. 1,000 to Rs. 5,000 |
| Advocate / CS for NCLT (if any) | Rs. 25,000 to Rs. 2,00,000+ |
| AOA stamp duty | Rs. 500 to Rs. 5,000 (state) |
| Delisting (SEBI process) if listed | Rs. 2,00,000 to Rs. 10,00,000+ |
| CS / CA professional (full file) | Rs. 20,000 to Rs. 75,000 |

## Tax impact (summary)

A CIN-continued pvt re-style of a CIN-continued public, where no re-allocation of the whole balance sheet to a new CIN happens in your fact pattern, is often tax-neutral at entity level: same PAN and CIN, no fresh deemed transfer in the pvt re-style on the same CIN-continued CIN, subject to a CA read of your M&A, delisting, and buyback facts. A delisting buyback can attract buyback tax under Section 115QA in the company and Section 10(34A) read with capital gains in hands of holders; map each leg with a CA. STT and securities-line TDS fall away on delisted and off-bourse tranches as the year and the facts in your bourse/SEBI and IT read show.

## Applicability

Public limited companies in all states and UTs under the 2013 Act, filed on the V3 e-form set with the jurisdictional RoC, and, when the proviso applies, at the NCLT bench the manual routes you to, and, when the name is listed, on the SEBI and exchange set first.

## Frequently asked questions

**Is NCLT always required?** No. It is required when the proviso in Section 14(1) is met, for example a past public offer of shares or more than 200 shareholders, per the text in force on the filing date and your cap table. Your CS confirms.

**Can a listed company go private in one day?** No. Delisting and public-holder settlement are long; the pvt re-style and COI re-issue follow in a second wave after a clean bourse/SEBI line.

**How long does it take?** Unlisted, at most 200 shareholders, no public offer: often 2 to 4 months. Listed: 6 to 12+ months with delisting, then 2 to 4 months for the pvt re-style, as a very rough range.

**What about minority holders?** In listed paths, SEBI reverse book-building and fair price rules protect retail and non-promoter holders. In NCLT, minority rights and creditor notice are in the NCLT order text.

**Can we go public again later?** Yes. A pvt can convert back to a public and then list under the pvt to public and SEBI path when you are ready, subject to fresh resolutions and a new growth story in law.

services.name: Temporary One Day Function License
services.shortDescription: One‑day liquor service licence for functions and events.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

A Temporary One Day Function License is a time-bound permission issued by the State Excise Department that allows the service and consumption of alcohol at a specific event for a single day. This license is commonly taken for:

- Weddings and receptions
- Birthday parties and private functions
- Corporate events and conferences
- Cultural programs and festivals
- Farmhouse parties and banquet events

The permission is strictly event-specific, date-specific, and venue-specific, meaning alcohol service is legally allowed only within the defined scope approved by authorities.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Legal Authorization for Event Alcohol Service

Ensures compliance with excise laws during functions.

### Simple Short-Term Solution

Ideal for one-time private or corporate events.

### Avoids Legal Risks

Prevents penalties for unlicensed liquor service.

### Flexible Usage for Events

Suitable for both small and large gatherings.

### Controlled Supervised Consumption

Regulated by state excise and police authorities.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Strict One-Day Validity

Cannot be extended beyond approved date.

### Venue Restrictions

License is valid only for the specified location.

### Approval Not Automatic

Subject to excise discretion and compliance.

### Heavy Documentation Requirements

Event and guest details must be provided.

### Limited Alcohol Scope (State Dependent)

Some states restrict liquor types or quantity.

## 4) Applicability Across India

Temporary function licenses are issued under State Excise Acts, and rules vary significantly by state.

### Common State Practices

- Maharashtra – Temporary excise license for events and weddings
- Delhi – Event liquor permit under excise policy
- Karnataka – One-day bar license system for functions
- Rajasthan – Temporary liquor permission for marriage halls
- Uttar Pradesh – Event-based liquor permit system
- Haryana & Punjab – Function-specific liquor permissions
- Goa – Simplified event liquor permissions due to tourism policy

### Regulatory Authorities

- State Excise Department (Primary Authority)
- Local Police Department (security clearance)
- District Administration (venue/event approval in some cases)

## 5) Key Features of Temporary One Day Function

License

### Event-Specific Permission

Valid only for the approved function.

### Strict Time Boundation

Limited to one day and specified hours.

### On-Premises Consumption Only

Alcohol must be consumed within the venue.

### Controlled Distribution System

Service must be managed under compliance rules.

### State Government Approval Required

Issued only after excise clearance.

### Venue-Specific Authorization

Cannot be transferred or reused elsewhere.

## 6) Documents / Information Required

- Application form for temporary excise license
- Identity proof of applicant/organizer
- Event details (date, time, purpose)
- Venue ownership or rental agreement
- Police NOC (mandatory in many states)
- Estimated guest list
- Layout plan of venue
- Security arrangement details
- GST or business registration (if applicable)
- Undertaking for compliance with excise rules

## 7) Process / Procedure

### Step 1 — Event Planning & Eligibility Check

Confirm eligibility under state excise rules.

### Step 2 — Application Submission

Apply to State Excise Department for one-day function permit.

### Step 3 — Document Submission

Provide event details and venue documentation.

### Step 4 — Verification by Authorities

Excise and police authorities review application.

### Step 5 — Inspection (if required)

Officials may inspect venue for compliance.

### Step 6 — Approval of License

Temporary license granted for event.

### Step 7 — Fee Payment

Pay applicable excise charges.

### Step 8 — Event Monitoring

Authorities may supervise during function.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### License Fees

- Varies based on state and event size
- Small private events: lower charges
- Large weddings/corporate events: higher fees

### Typical Range (India)

- ₹5,000 to ₹2,00,000+ per event depending on state and scale

### Additional Costs

- Security arrangements
- Police permissions (if applicable)
- Venue compliance requirements
- Excise supervision charges (in some states)

### Penalty Risk

- Serving without license: heavy fines + legal action
- Violation of timing or venue conditions: cancellation
- Over-serving or unauthorized sale: strict enforcement
- Non-compliance during inspection: seizure and prosecution

## Conclusion

A Temporary One Day Function License is a short-term excise authorization issued by State Governments in India that allows legal alcohol service during private or corporate events.

### It ensures regulated consumption, legal compliance, and controlled distribution,

making it essential for weddings, parties, and functions where alcohol service is required within a strictly defined legal framework.

services.name: One Day Permission for Foreign Liquor
services.shortDescription: Event‑based permission to serve foreign liquor.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

One Day Permission for Foreign Liquor refers to a temporary excise license issued by the State Excise Department that allows the service and consumption of foreign liquor (IMFL / imported liquor) at a specific event for a limited duration, usually one day. This permission is commonly granted for:

- Weddings and receptions
- Corporate events and parties
- Private functions and banquets
- High-end hospitality events
- Festivals and institutional gatherings

The permission is strictly event-specific, time-bound, and venue-restricted, meaning alcohol can only be served at the approved place, on the approved date, and under excise supervision.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Legal Permission for Foreign Liquor Service

Allows lawful serving of IMFL and imported liquor at events.

### Suitable for Premium Events

Ideal for weddings, corporate parties, and luxury gatherings.

### Avoids Legal Risk

Prevents illegal alcohol service violations.

### Controlled Consumption Environment

Ensures regulated and supervised distribution.

### Flexible Event-Based Approval

One-time permission without long-term licensing.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Strict Time Restriction

Valid only for the approved event duration.

### High Regulatory Control

Excise and police supervision may be required.

### State-Wise Restrictions

Rules differ significantly across states.

### Limited Usage Scope

Cannot be used for commercial or recurring operations.

### Approval Not Guaranteed

Subject to government discretion and compliance checks.

## 4) Applicability Across India

One Day Foreign Liquor Permission is governed by State Excise Departments, and policies vary by state.

### Common State Practices

- Maharashtra – Temporary permit room license for events
- Delhi – Event liquor permission under excise policy
- Karnataka – One-day bar license for private functions
- Rajasthan – Temporary liquor permission for weddings/events
- Uttar Pradesh – Event-based foreign liquor license system
- Goa – Relaxed event licensing under tourism-friendly rules
- Haryana & Punjab – Strict but available for approved functions

### Regulatory Authorities

- State Excise Department (Primary Authority)
- Local Police Authorities (security approval)
- District Administration (event-level permissions in some cases)

## 5) Key Features of One Day Foreign Liquor Permission

### Event-Specific Authorization

Valid only for a single approved event.

### Foreign Liquor Allowed (IMFL/Imported)

Includes whiskey, vodka, rum, gin, wine, etc.

### On-Premises Consumption Only

Alcohol must be consumed at the venue.

### Strict Time Boundaries

Valid only during permitted hours.

### Venue Restriction

Cannot be shifted or reused elsewhere.

### Government Supervision Possible

Excise officers may monitor compliance.

## 6) Documents / Information Required

- Application form for temporary excise permission
- Identity proof of applicant/organizer
- Event invitation or details (date, time, purpose)
- Venue ownership or rental agreement
- Police NOC (in many states)
- Estimated guest list
- Layout plan of venue
- Security arrangements details
- GST or business registration (if applicable)
- Undertaking for compliance with excise rules

## 7) Process / Procedure

### Step 1 — Event Planning & Eligibility Check

Confirm eligibility under state excise rules.

### Step 2 — Application Submission

Apply to State Excise Department for one-day foreign liquor permit.

### Step 3 — Document Submission

Attach event details, venue proof, and security arrangements.

### Step 4 — Verification by Authorities

Excise and police review application.

### Step 5 — Site Inspection (if required)

Officials may inspect venue for compliance.

### Step 6 — Approval of Permit

Temporary license granted for the event.

### Step 7 — Fee Payment

Pay applicable excise duty and permit charges.

### Step 8 — Event Monitoring

Authorities may supervise alcohol service during event.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### License Fees

- Varies by state and event size
- Private small events: lower fees
- Large or luxury events: higher charges

### Typical Range (India)

- ₹10,000 to ₹2,00,000+ per event (state and scale dependent)

### Additional Costs

- Security and compliance arrangements
- Police verification charges (if applicable)
- Venue-specific permissions
- Excise supervision charges in some states

### Penalty Risk

- Serving without permit: severe legal action + fines
- Violation of timing or conditions: cancellation and penalties
- Unauthorized sale or over-serving: strict excise action
- Non-compliance during inspection: confiscation and prosecution

## Conclusion

One Day Permission for Foreign Liquor is a temporary excise authorization issued by State Governments in India that allows controlled service of IMFL and imported alcohol at specific events.

### It ensures legal compliance, regulated alcohol distribution, and safe event

management, making it essential for weddings, corporate functions, and premium private gatherings where foreign liquor service is required within a strictly defined legal framework.

services.name: One Year & Lifelong Permission for Foreign Liquor
services.shortDescription: Long‑term foreign liquor serving permissions for eligible premises.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

One Year & Lifelong Permission for Foreign Liquor refers to a long-term excise license issued by the State Excise Department that allows an establishment to store, serve, and sell foreign liquor (IMFL and imported alcohol) within a licensed premises.

### Unlike one-day or event-based permissions, these are permanent or renewable

operational licenses granted to businesses such as:

- Hotels
- Restaurants
- Bars and pubs
- Clubs
- Resorts
- Permit rooms (state-dependent classification)

### The license permits on-premises consumption of alcohol under strict regulatory

compliance. In some states, it is issued as an annual renewable license, while in certain cases (legacy systems or specific categories), it may be treated as a long-term or transferable "lifetime" license subject to renewal conditions.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Full-Time Legal Alcohol Service

Allows continuous serving of foreign liquor in licensed premises.

### High Revenue Business Model

Alcohol sales significantly increase hospitality profitability.

### Premium Hospitality Positioning

Enables bars, restaurants, and hotels to attract higher-end customers.

### Operational Stability

Annual or long-term permission ensures uninterrupted business.

### Brand Value Enhancement

Licensed establishments gain higher market credibility.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### High Regulatory Control

Strict monitoring by excise authorities.

### Heavy Licensing Fees

Annual fees can be significant depending on location.

### Strict Compliance Requirements

Must follow excise rules on storage, sale, and reporting.

### Location Restrictions

Cannot operate near sensitive or prohibited zones.

### Policy Dependence

Licensing rules may change based on state excise policy.

## 4) Applicability Across India

Foreign liquor bar/permit licenses are governed by State Excise Departments, and categories differ across states.

### Common State License Types

- Maharashtra – FL-III (clubs), FL-IV (permit rooms), hotel bar licenses
- Delhi – Restaurant Bar License / Hotel Bar License under excise policy
- Karnataka – CL-9 / RCB (Restaurant & Bar licenses)
- Goa – Tourism-based bar and restaurant licenses
- Tamil Nadu – FL-2 / FL-3 (hotels and bars)
- Rajasthan – Hotel and bar license system under excise rules
- Uttar Pradesh – Hotel/restaurant foreign liquor licenses
- Punjab & Haryana – Structured bar and hotel licensing system

### Regulatory Authorities

- State Excise Department (Primary Authority)
- Municipal Corporation (building and trade compliance)
- Police Department (verification and enforcement)

## 5) Key Features of Long-Term Foreign Liquor

Permission

### Annual or Long-Term Validity

Typically renewed yearly; some categories are long-term under policy terms.

### On-Premises Alcohol Service

Consumption allowed only within licensed premises.

### Foreign Liquor Allowed (IMFL & Imported)

Includes whiskey, vodka, rum, gin, wine, etc.

### State-Controlled Licensing System

Issued under excise quota or application-based approval.

### Mandatory Compliance Records

Stock registers, sales records, and inspections required.

### Fixed Operating Conditions

Includes timing restrictions and zoning compliance.

## 6) Documents / Information Required

- Business registration (Company / LLP / Firm)
- Identity proof of owners/directors
- PAN and address proof
- Property ownership or lease agreement
- GST registration certificate
- FSSAI license (for restaurants/hotels)
- Layout plan of premises
- Police clearance certificate
- Application under State Excise Act
- Financial proof or solvency certificate (in some states)
- Fire safety and municipal approvals (if required)

## 7) Process / Procedure

### Step 1 — Business Eligibility Setup

Establish hotel, bar, restaurant, or club entity.

### Step 2 — Select License Category

Choose appropriate excise license type (hotel bar, permit room, etc.).

### Step 3 — Application Submission

Apply to State Excise Department.

### Step 4 — Document Verification

Authorities verify ownership, compliance, and eligibility.

### Step 5 — Premises Inspection

Excise officials inspect infrastructure and location.

### Step 6 — Approval from Excise Authority

License granted based on compliance and policy conditions.

### Step 7 — Fee Payment

Pay annual excise duty and license fees.

### Step 8 — License Issuance & Operations

Business permitted to legally serve foreign liquor.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### License Fees

- Highly state-dependent
- Metro cities: significantly higher fees
- Hospitality establishments: premium licensing cost

### Typical Range (India)

- ₹1,00,000 to ₹25,00,000+ annually depending on state, city, and category

### Additional Costs

- Excise duty on liquor sales
- Security deposits (in some states)
- Staff licensing and training compliance
- Infrastructure compliance (bar setup, storage rules)
- Annual renewal charges

### Penalty Risk

- Operating without license: heavy fines + criminal action
- Violation of excise rules: suspension/cancellation
- Illegal sale or over-service: strict enforcement
- Non-renewal: business shutdown risk

Conclusion

One Year & Lifelong Permission for Foreign Liquor in India refers to structured excise licenses that allow hospitality businesses to legally serve IMFL and imported alcohol on a continuous basis under state-controlled regulations. It is a high-value operational license category, essential for hotels, bars, restaurants, and clubs, providing long-term business stability while ensuring strict compliance with India's state excise laws.

services.name: One Day Permission for Country Liquor
services.shortDescription: Single‑day permit to serve country liquor at events.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

One Day Permission for Country Liquor refers to a temporary excise license granted by the State Excise Department that allows the sale or serving of country liquor (desi liquor) for a specific event on a single day. This permission is generally issued for private functions, public events, festivals, weddings, fairs, political gatherings, or cultural programs, where alcohol consumption is expected but a permanent liquor license is not held by the organizer. The license is strictly time-bound and event-specific, meaning it is valid only for the approved date, venue, and purpose.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Legal Authorization for Temporary Alcohol Service

Allows lawful serving of country liquor during events.

### Event Flexibility

Suitable for weddings, festivals, and large gatherings.

### Avoids Legal Penalties

Prevents illegal alcohol distribution charges.

### Controlled Consumption Environment

Regulated by excise authorities for safety.

### Quick Approval Process (in many states)

Fast-track permission for short-duration events.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Strict Time Limitation

Valid only for a single day or specific event duration.

### Limited Alcohol Type

Generally restricted to country liquor (desi alcohol).

### Heavy Compliance Conditions

Strict monitoring by excise and police authorities.

### Location Restrictions

Cannot be granted in prohibited or sensitive zones.

### No Commercial Continuity

Cannot be used for regular business operations.

## 4) Applicability Across India

One Day Country Liquor Permission is issued under State Excise Acts, and rules vary significantly across states.

### Common State Practices

- Uttar Pradesh – Temporary bar license for events
- Maharashtra – One-day permit under excise rules for functions
- Rajasthan – Event liquor permission system
- Madhya Pradesh – Temporary excise permits for functions and fairs
- Haryana – Special occasion liquor permits
- Punjab – Event-based liquor permissions under excise authority
- Bihar (restricted states) – Limited or strictly controlled permissions

### Regulatory Authorities

- State Excise Department (Primary Authority)
- Local Police Department (Security clearance)
- District Administration (event approval in some cases)

## 5) Key Features of One Day Liquor Permission

### Event-Specific License

Valid only for a particular function or event.

### Time-Bound Validity

Strictly limited to approved date and hours.

### Controlled Distribution

Alcohol must be served under supervision.

### Government-Approved Framework

Issued only after excise authority approval.

### Restricted Alcohol Category

Primarily country liquor in many states.

### Venue-Specific Permission

Cannot be transferred to another location.

## 6) Documents / Information Required

- Application form for temporary excise permission
- Identity proof of applicant/organizer
- Event details (date, time, and purpose)
- Venue ownership or rental proof
- Police NOC (in many states)
- GST or business registration (if applicable)
- Estimated number of attendees
- Layout plan of event venue
- Security arrangement details
- Undertaking for compliance with excise rules

## 7) Process / Procedure

### Step 1 — Event Planning & Eligibility Check

Confirm eligibility under state excise rules.

### Step 2 — Submit Application to Excise Department

Apply for one-day country liquor permission.

### Step 3 — Attach Event Details & Documents

Provide venue, timing, and security arrangements.

### Step 4 — Verification by Authorities

Excise and police review application.

### Step 5 — Site Inspection (if required)

Officials may inspect event location.

### Step 6 — Approval from Excise Authority

Temporary license issued for the event.

### Step 7 — Fee Payment

Pay applicable excise duty and permission fee.

### Step 8 — Event Compliance Monitoring

Authorities may supervise during event.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### License Fees

- Varies by state, event size, and duration
- Small private events: lower fees
- Large public events: higher excise charges

### Typical Range (India)

- ₹5,000 to ₹1,00,000+ per event (state and scale dependent)

### Additional Costs

- Security arrangements
- Police permissions (if required)
- Event compliance and supervision charges
- Venue-specific approvals

### Penalty Risk

- Serving without permission: heavy fines + criminal liability
- Violation of excise terms: cancellation of permission
- Over-serving or unauthorized sale: strict action
- Non-compliance with timing: penalties and seizure

## Conclusion

One Day Permission for Country Liquor is a temporary excise authorization issued by State Governments in India that allows controlled alcohol service for specific events. It ensures legal compliance, regulated consumption, and controlled distribution of country liquor, making it essential for event organizers who wish to serve alcohol legally within a strictly defined time and venue framework.

services.name: Wholesale License for Wine
services.shortDescription: Excise licensing for wholesale wine traders.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

A Wholesale License for Wine is a State Excise Department permission that allows a business entity to purchase, store, and distribute wine in bulk quantities to licensed retailers, hotels, restaurants, clubs, and authorized outlets. Wine falls under the foreign liquor (IMFL) or fermented alcoholic beverage category, depending on state excise classification. The wholesale license ensures that wine distribution happens only through regulated and tax-compliant channels. This license does not permit direct retail sale to consumers. It is strictly limited to bulk supply within the excise-approved distribution network.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Legal Bulk Distribution Rights

Allows authorized wholesale trade of wine products.

### Growing Market Demand

Wine consumption is increasing in urban hospitality sectors.

### Stable Hospitality Supply Chain

Regular demand from hotels, restaurants, and retail outlets.

### Premium Product Category

Wine generally has higher value positioning in the alcohol market.

### Long-Term Business Continuity

Renewable license ensures ongoing operations.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Strict Regulatory Control

Highly regulated by State Excise Departments.

### No Retail Sales Permission

Cannot sell directly to end consumers.

### Compliance-Heavy Operations

Requires detailed stock tracking and reporting.

### High Entry Costs in Some States

Licensing fees and deposits can be significant.

### Policy Variations Across States

Rules differ widely across India.

## 4) Applicability Across India

Wholesale wine licenses are governed by State Excise Acts, and classification varies by state policy.

### Common State Systems

- Maharashtra – Wine distribution under excise wholesale framework
- Delhi – IMFL/wine wholesale distribution licenses
- Karnataka – Licensed wine distributors under excise rules
- Goa – Liberal wine distribution due to tourism policy
- Tamil Nadu – State-controlled liquor distribution systems
- Rajasthan – Excise-based wholesale wine permits
- Uttar Pradesh – Controlled wholesale liquor distribution system
- Punjab & Haryana – Auction/quota-based licensing systems

### Regulatory Authorities

- State Excise Department (Primary Authority)
- State Beverages Corporations (in many states)
- District Excise Offices (inspection and enforcement)

## 5) Key Features of Wholesale Wine License

### Bulk Distribution Only

Permits supply to licensed entities only.

### No Direct Consumer Sale

Strict prohibition on retail transactions.

### State-Controlled Supply Chain

All movement regulated by excise authorities.

### Mandatory Stock Records

Detailed inventory and sales tracking required.

### Transport Permits Required

Each consignment must be documented.

### Annual Renewal Requirement

License validity depends on state policy cycle.

## 6) Documents / Information Required

- Business registration certificate (Company/LLP/Firm)
- PAN and identity proof of owners/directors
- GST registration certificate
- Warehouse ownership or lease agreement
- Financial statements / solvency certificate
- Police verification certificate
- Application under State Excise Rules
- Layout plan of storage facility
- Security deposit documentation (if applicable)
- Compliance undertaking for excise regulations

## 7) Process / Procedure

### Step 1 — Eligibility Assessment

Check state excise policy for wine wholesale licensing.

### Step 2 — Application Submission

Apply to State Excise Department.

### Step 3 — Document Verification

Authorities verify legal and financial compliance.

### Step 4 — Premises Inspection

Warehouse or storage facility is inspected.

### Step 5 — Approval from Excise Authority

License granted based on compliance and availability.

### Step 6 — Fee Payment

Pay license fee and security deposits.

### Step 7 — License Issuance

Wholesale distribution rights officially granted.

### Step 8 — Operational Compliance

Begin regulated wine distribution activities.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### License Fees

- Varies significantly by state and quota system
- May be fixed, tender-based, or auction-based

### Typical Range (India)

- ₹2,00,000 to ₹25,00,000+ annually depending on state policy and scale

### Additional Costs

- Security deposits (state-dependent, sometimes high)
- Warehouse compliance setup
- Transport permit system
- Excise reporting infrastructure
- Staffing and audit compliance

### Penalty Risk

- Illegal distribution: heavy penalties + license cancellation
- Stock mismatch: investigation and fines
- Unauthorized sale: criminal liability
- Non-compliance with reporting: suspension of license

## Conclusion

A Wholesale License for Wine in India is a state-regulated excise authorization that allows bulk distribution of wine to licensed retailers and hospitality establishments. It plays a key role in the controlled alcohol supply chain, ensuring regulated distribution, tax compliance, and structured trade within India's excise framework while supporting the growing wine and hospitality market.

services.name: Wholesale License for Country Liquor
services.shortDescription: Wholesale licences for country liquor operations.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

A Wholesale License for Country Liquor is a State Excise Department authorization that permits a business entity to purchase, store, and supply country liquor in bulk quantities to licensed retailers, depots, or government-approved outlets. This license is part of the controlled distribution system of alcoholic beverages in India, where country liquor (locally distilled spirits) is regulated more strictly than IMFL in many states. The license does not allow retail sale to end consumers. It is strictly for bulk distribution within the excise-controlled supply chain.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Legal Bulk Trading Rights

Allows authorized wholesale distribution of country liquor.

### High-Volume Business Opportunity

Large-scale supply contracts with retailers and depots.

### State-Controlled Demand Stability

Liquor demand is consistent and regulated.

### Structured Supply Chain Role

Acts as an intermediary between manufacturers and retailers.

### Long-Term Business Continuity

Renewable license ensures ongoing operations.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Highly Regulated Sector

Strict control by excise authorities.

### No Direct Retail Sales Allowed

Cannot sell to end consumers.

### High Compliance Burden

Regular reporting and stock audits required.

### State Monopoly in Some Regions

Some states restrict wholesale operations heavily.

### Heavy Licensing Fees & Deposits

High financial entry barrier in many states.

## 4) Applicability Across India

Wholesale country liquor licenses are issued under State Excise Acts, and availability varies significantly.

### Common State Structures

- Uttar Pradesh – FL-2 wholesale country liquor license system
- Maharashtra – Wholesale distribution under excise contractor model
- Madhya Pradesh – Government-controlled wholesale allocation system
- Rajasthan – Licensed wholesale distribution system
- Haryana – Auction-based wholesale licensing
- Punjab – Controlled distribution through licensed wholesalers
- Bihar – Highly restricted or government-controlled system

### Regulatory Authorities

- State Excise Department (Primary Authority)
- State Government Liquor Distribution Corporations (in some states)
- District Excise Offices (inspection and enforcement)

## 5) Key Features of Wholesale Country Liquor License

### Bulk Distribution Rights Only

Supply permitted only to licensed retailers or depots.

### No Direct Consumer Sale

Retail sales strictly prohibited.

### State-Controlled Supply Chain

Operations regulated under excise framework.

### Mandatory Stock Tracking

Daily inventory and movement records required.

### Fixed Operating Guidelines

Storage, transport, and sale governed by strict rules.

### Renewable License System

Issued annually or as per excise policy cycle.

## 6) Documents / Information Required

- Business registration certificate (Company/LLP/Firm)
- PAN and identity proof of owners/directors
- GST registration certificate
- Shop/warehouse ownership or lease agreement
- Financial statements and solvency proof
- Police verification certificate
- Application form under State Excise Rules
- Layout plan of warehouse/storage facility
- Security deposit documents (if applicable)
- Compliance undertaking for excise regulations

## 7) Process / Procedure

### Step 1 — Eligibility Verification

Check state excise policy for wholesale licensing.

### Step 2 — Application Submission

Apply to State Excise Department.

### Step 3 — Document Review

Authorities verify legal and financial eligibility.

### Step 4 — Premises Inspection

Warehouse or storage facility is inspected.

### Step 5 — Approval by Excise Authority

License granted based on compliance and policy.

### Step 6 — Fee Payment

Pay license fees and security deposits.

### Step 7 — License Issuance

Wholesale distribution rights officially granted.

### Step 8 — Operational Compliance Begins

Start supply under regulated framework.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### License Fees

- Varies heavily by state policy
- May be fixed or auction-based

### Typical Range (India)

- ₹5,00,000 to ₹50,00,000+ annually depending on state and quota system

### Additional Costs

- Security deposits (often substantial)
- Warehouse compliance infrastructure
- Excise duty handling systems
- Transport permits and logistics compliance
- Staff licensing and reporting systems

### Penalty Risk

- Illegal distribution: severe penalties + license cancellation
- Stock mismatch: heavy fines and investigation
- Unauthorized sale: criminal liability
- Non-compliance with excise rules: suspension

## Conclusion

A Wholesale License for Country Liquor in India is a state-regulated excise authorization that permits bulk distribution of country liquor within the government-controlled supply chain. It plays a critical role in the structured liquor distribution ecosystem, ensuring regulated supply, taxation control, and compliance-driven trade across licensed retailers and depots.

services.name: Wholesale License for Foreign Liquor
services.shortDescription: Wholesale excise licence for foreign liquor.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

A Wholesale License for Foreign Liquor is a State Excise Department approval that allows a business to purchase, store, and distribute foreign liquor (IMFL and imported alcoholic beverages) in bulk quantities to licensed retailers, hotels, bars, clubs, and government-authorised outlets. This license is a critical part of India's regulated alcohol supply chain, ensuring that foreign liquor moves only through approved and tax-compliant channels. It does not permit retail sale to consumers. The entire operation is restricted to wholesale distribution within the excise-controlled ecosystem.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Legal Bulk Distribution Rights

Authorised to supply IMFL and imported liquor to licensed entities.

### High-Value Trade Segment

Foreign liquor has higher margins compared to country liquor.

### Stable Institutional Demand

Regular demand from hotels, bars, and retailers.

### Controlled Supply Chain Role

Acts as a key intermediary in liquor distribution system.

### Long-Term Business Potential

Renewable licenses ensure continuous operations.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Strict Government Control

Heavily regulated by State Excise Departments.

### No Direct Consumer Sales Allowed

Strictly limited to licensed buyers only.

### High Compliance Burden

Stock audits, reporting, and transport documentation required.

### Heavy Financial Investment

High licensing fees and security deposits in many states.

### Policy Dependency

Rules and quotas may change annually.

## 4) Applicability Across India

Wholesale foreign liquor licenses are issued under State Excise Laws, with different models across states.

### Common State Systems

- Maharashtra – FL wholesale distribution licenses under excise policy
- Delhi – Licensed wholesale distribution under excise framework
- Karnataka – Wholesale IMFL distribution licenses
- Goa – Tourism-oriented distribution model with licensed wholesalers
- Rajasthan – Excise-controlled wholesale licensing system
- Uttar Pradesh – Distributor-based licensing structure
- Punjab & Haryana – Auction and quota-based wholesale systems

### Regulatory Authorities

- State Excise Department (Primary Authority)
- State Beverages Corporations (in many states)
- District Excise Offices (inspection & enforcement)

## 5) Key Features of Wholesale Foreign Liquor License

### Bulk Supply Authorization Only

Permits distribution only to licensed buyers.

### No Retail Rights

Cannot sell directly to consumers.

### State-Controlled Distribution System

Operates under strict excise monitoring.

### Mandatory Stock & Sales Records

Detailed tracking of inflow and outflow required.

### Transport Permits Required

Each movement of goods must be documented.

### Annual Renewal System

License validity depends on state excise policy.

## 6) Documents / Information Required

- Business registration (Company/LLP/Firm)
- PAN and identity proof of directors/owners
- GST registration certificate
- Warehouse ownership or lease agreement
- Financial statements and solvency proof
- Police verification certificate
- Application under State Excise Rules
- Layout plan of storage facility
- Security deposit details (if applicable)
- Compliance undertaking for excise regulations

## 7) Process / Procedure

### Step 1 — Eligibility Check

Verify state excise eligibility for wholesale IMFL license.

### Step 2 — Application Submission

Apply to State Excise Department or authorised body.

### Step 3 — Document Verification

Authorities verify legal, financial, and operational details.

### Step 4 — Warehouse Inspection

Excise officials inspect storage facility and infrastructure.

### Step 5 — Approval from Excise Authority

License granted based on compliance and policy allocation.

### Step 6 — Fee & Deposit Payment

Pay license fees and required security deposits.

### Step 7 — License Issuance

Official wholesale rights granted for IMFL distribution.

### Step 8 — Operational Compliance Begins

Start regulated distribution under excise supervision.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### License Fees

- Highly state-dependent
- Often based on quota, auction, or fixed annual structure

### Typical Range (India)

- ₹10,00,000 to ₹1,00,00,000+ annually depending on state and volume allocation

### Additional Costs

- Security deposits (significant in many states)
- Warehouse compliance infrastructure
- Transport permit systems
- Excise duty management systems
- Staffing and audit compliance costs

### Penalty Risk

- Illegal distribution: heavy fines + license cancellation
- Stock mismatch or diversion: strict legal action
- Non-compliance with excise records: suspension
- Unauthorized sale: criminal liability

## Conclusion

A Wholesale License for Foreign Liquor in India is a state-controlled excise authorization that enables bulk distribution of IMFL and imported alcoholic beverages within a strictly regulated supply chain. It forms a crucial part of India's alcohol distribution ecosystem, ensuring tax compliance, controlled supply, and regulated trade operations between manufacturers, wholesalers, and licensed retailers.

services.name: Permit Room License
services.shortDescription: Licensing of permit rooms attached to hotels and restaurants.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

A Permit Room License is a State Excise Department authorization that allows a hotel, restaurant, or hospitality establishment to serve foreign liquor (IMFL and sometimes wine/beer depending on state rules) for on-premises consumption. The term "permit room" is most commonly used in states like Maharashtra and parts of Western India, where a designated room or section within a restaurant or hotel is legally approved for alcohol service. This license is strictly for consumption within the premises and does not allow retail takeaway or off-premises sale.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Legal Alcohol Service in Hospitality Setup

Allows restaurants/hotels to serve liquor legally.

### Increased Revenue Generation

Alcohol sales significantly improve profit margins.

### Premium Customer Experience

Enhances dining and hospitality appeal.

### Controlled & Regulated Operations

Clear excise framework ensures lawful business.

### Established Business Model

Widely recognized structure in India's hospitality sector.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Strict Excise Compliance

Must follow state excise rules and reporting requirements.

### High Licensing Costs in Some States

Annual fees can be significant.

### Restricted Usage Area

Alcohol service limited only to designated permit room area.

### Limited Operating Hours

Service allowed only during permitted time slots.

### Frequent Inspections

Subject to excise and police checks.

## 4) Applicability Across India

Permit room licenses are governed by State Excise Departments, and classification varies by state.

### Common State Usage

- Maharashtra – Permit Room License (FL-4 / FL-III categories in some contexts)
- Goa – Restaurant/bar permit systems with relaxed norms
- Karnataka – Restaurant Bar License (CL-9 equivalent structure)
- Delhi – Hotel/restaurant liquor service license system
- Rajasthan – Hotel and restaurant liquor service permits
- Tamil Nadu – FL-2 / FL-3 hotel bar categories

### Regulatory Authorities

- State Excise Department (Primary Authority)
- Municipal Corporation (trade and building compliance)
- Local Police Authorities (verification & enforcement)

## 5) Key Features of Permit Room License

### On-Premises Consumption Only

Alcohol must be consumed inside licensed area.

### Designated Permit Area

Only specific room/section is approved for liquor service.

### Foreign Liquor Allowed (IMFL/Wine/Beer)

Depends on state excise policy.

### State-Controlled Licensing System

Issued under excise rules and quotas.

### Mandatory Record Maintenance

Stock registers and sales logs required.

### Annual Renewal Requirement

License must be renewed periodically.

## 6) Documents / Information Required

- Business registration (Company/LLP/Firm/Proprietorship)
- PAN and identity proof of owners
- Property ownership or rental agreement
- Layout plan showing permit room area
- GST registration certificate
- FSSAI license (for restaurants/hotels)
- Police clearance certificate
- Fire safety certificate (where applicable)
- Application form under State Excise Rules
- Financial documents (varies by state)

## 7) Process / Procedure

### Step 1 — Establish Hospitality Business

Hotel, restaurant, or lounge setup required.

### Step 2 — Define Permit Room Area

Designated alcohol service space must be marked.

### Step 3 — Apply to Excise Department

Submit application for permit room license.

### Step 4 — Document Verification

Authorities verify ownership and compliance.

### Step 5 — Inspection of Premises

Excise officials inspect layout and infrastructure.

### Step 6 — Approval by Excise Authority

License granted under applicable category.

### Step 7 — Fee Payment

Pay applicable excise license fees.

### Step 8 — License Issuance & Operation

Alcohol service begins under compliance rules.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### License Fees

- Varies by state and category
- Metro cities: higher annual fees
- Smaller towns: moderate charges

### Typical Range (India)

- ₹1,00,000 to ₹15,00,000+ annually depending on state and location

### Additional Costs

- Excise duty on liquor sales
- Security deposit (in some states)
- Staff licensing and compliance training
- Infrastructure modifications for permit area
- Annual renewal charges

### Penalty Risk

- Serving without license: heavy fines + legal action
- Violation of permit area rules: suspension
- Sale outside allowed hours: penalties
- Non-compliance with excise rules: cancellation risk

## Conclusion

A Permit Room License in India is a state-issued excise authorization that allows hotels and restaurants to serve foreign liquor within a designated area under strict regulatory control. It is a core hospitality licensing structure, enabling legal alcohol service, increased revenue potential, and regulated operations within India's excise framework.
