services.name: FLA Return Filing
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for FLA Return Filing — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# FLA return filing (foreign liabilities and assets)

## Overview

The FLA (Foreign Liabilities and Assets) Return is an annual return under FEMA, 1999, filed with the **Reserve Bank of India (RBI)**. The **RBI** compiles **Balance of Payments** and **international investment position** data from it. If your **Indian** company or **LLP** has FDI on the books or has made ODI, you file every year, even with no new investment in the year. The return reports the 31 **March** **stock** of foreign liabilities and assets, not just new flows in that year.

**FC-GPR** and ODI event filings cover individual tranches. The **FLA** return is the year-end snapshot, usually on the **FLAIR** (Foreign Liabilities and Assets Information Reporting) portal. Check the current **RBI** portal, form, and the deadline in force. The **usual** due date is **15 July** for the **financial** year that ended the previous 31 **March**, unless the **RBI** extends the date in a public circular.

> [!TIP] Tie **FLA** numbers to audited financials, **FIRMS** UINs, and last year’s **FLA** before you press submit, so **RBI** and diligence teams do not see a break.

## Applicability

File when, as of 31 **March**, you have:

- FDI stock (equity, preference, convertibles) in an **Indian** company, or
- ODI stock (equity, loans, guarantees) from an **Indian** company or **LLP** to an overseas **JV** / **WOS**,

even if there was no new round that year.

Branch offices, project offices, and liaison offices of foreign companies often use the **annual activity** line with **RBI** instead of this entity-level return. Map your structure in the current master direction.

You may be able to stop only after **RBI**-consistent closure of the **FDI** / **ODI** position, not merely because a company board says the investment is “done” on paper. Take advice on the facts.

**FEMA** is a central law; the **FLA** obligation is **pan-India** (all states and UTs).

## Key provisions

| Part | What it usually covers |
|------|------------------------|
| Part I | CIN, PAN, industry, turnover, entity basics |
| Part II | FDI received (equity, preference, reinvested earnings, other capital) |
| Part III | ODI made (equity, loans, guarantees) |
| Part IV | Foreign trade credits (e.g. advances, suppliers’ credit) |
| Part V | ECB on books, where the return asks for it |
| Part VI | Other foreign assets and liabilities as defined for that return |

**FX:** Convert to INR using the **RBI** reference rate as on 31 **March** of the reporting year, as the **RBI** instruction in force for that year states.

## Benefits

- Meets the **RBI** annual reporting line and cuts compounding risk from **non-filing**.
- A consistent **FLA** history helps **M&A** and **IPO** diligence and **follow-on** investment confidence.
- The preparation process often **surfaces** **FIRMS** / **book** **mismatches** before a regulator or investor does.
- The **RBI** uses the data in national **BoP** and **IIP** statistics.

## Limitations

- Catching up on missed years is expensive and can trigger compounding.
- Reconciliation is time-heavy for many funding rounds, **ECB** layers, and ODI in several jurisdictions.
- The **July 15** date is **usually** not automatically extended; treat it as a hard line unless a circular says otherwise.
- If **FC-GPR** (or ODI) reporting was always wrong, **FLA** alone does not cure **FEMA**; you need a joined-up fix.

## Process

1. Confirm **FDI** or **ODI** stock on 31 **March** for the reporting year.
2. Collect audited financials, cap table, **FIRC** set, **FC-GPR** UINs, ODI acks, **ECB** docs, and prior **FLA**.
3. Reconcile to **FIRMS**; convert with 31 **March** **RBI** reference rates.
4. Log in to **FLAIR** with current **authorised** user data.
5. Complete all return parts (entity, **FDI, ODI**, trade credit, **ECB**, other).
6. Second-pass review against the audit and last **FLA**.
7. File before 15 **July** (or extended date); keep the acknowledgement.
8. Retain the signed-off pack for at least ten years, or as your board policy says.

## Documents

| Document | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| Incorporation + PAN | Identity |
| Audited financial statements | **FLA** line items from books |
| Foreign shareholder and ODI entity schedules | **FDI** / **ODI** parts |
| FIRC, bank statements | Inward FDI and cash trail |
| FC-GPR / UIN references | **FIRMS** cross-check |
| ODI form references | ODI leg |
| ECB loan agreements, repayment tables | **ECB** part where applicable |
| **RBI** 31 **March** ref rate | FX to INR |
| Prior **FLA** return | Continuity, opening position |

## Cost / professional fees (indicative, plus GST at 18%)

| Item | INR range |
|------|-----------|
| **FLA** filing, one simple **FDI** line | 5,000 – 10,000 |
| **FLA** filing, several rounds and/or ODI | 10,000 – 20,000 |
| Complex, multi-entity, **ECB** + ODI | 20,000 – 40,000 |
| Data clean-up and reconciliation | 15,000 – 50,000 |
| Retrospective (multiple years) | 20,000 – 75,000 |
| Compounding support (non/late file) | 30,000 – 1,00,000+ |
| **FLAIR** access help | 2,000 – 5,000 |

**RBI** compounding fees, if any, are separate from professional fees.

## Compliance requirements

- **15 July** for the **FY** ended 31 **March** (see live circular for the year in question).
- **Mandatory** even when no new investment if **FDI/ODI** **stock** remains.
- **RBI** can compare **FLA** with **FIRMS**; explain material differences in your file.
- **Numbers** should align with audited financials; use **CA** or **FEMA** help when books are complex.
- **Non-filing** is a FEMA default; you may need compounding.
- **Keep** **FLAIR** credentials and **signatory** data current with **RBI** **/ MCA** **changes**.

## Frequently asked questions

**We received FDI years ago; no new money. Do we file?** Yes, while **FDI** or **ODI** **stock** remains on the books on 31 **March** and has not been **RBI**-closed, you continue to file. The return is about **outstanding** position, not this year’s news alone.

**Penalty for not filing?** FEMA **penalty** can be up to 3x the **forex** amount or **INR 2** lakh if not **quantifiable**, plus **continuing** default amounts. The cost of not filing is usually higher than a timely, supported **FLA** run.

**FDI and ODI both?** One **FLA** return, with **FDI** in the liability / equity lines and ODI in the asset / outward lines as the form asks for that year.

**We missed several years. What do we do?** File retrospective **FLA** with full reconciliation, then use the **RBI** compounding path for delay or **non**-filing, with a **FEMA** adviser on the facts.

**Does a CA have to sign the return?** The **FLAIR** submission is usually filed by the company (for example, **CFO** or company secretary) without a mandatory **CA** certificate on the form. The data must still match audited **financials**; complex files often use **CA** or **FEMA** support to prepare the numbers.

---

*General information only, not case-specific FEMA, tax, or company law advice.*

services.name: FEMA Advisory
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for FEMA Advisory — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# FEMA advisory and compliance

## Overview

The **Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA), 1999** is the main legislation for foreign exchange in India. It supports external trade, payments, and the development of the foreign exchange market, and it replaced the older, more restrictive **Foreign Exchange Regulation Act (FERA), 1973**. FEMA is administered by the **Reserve Bank of India (RBI)**. The **Enforcement Directorate (ED)** enforces serious contraventions. FEMA applies to a wide set of transactions, from foreign investment and overseas investment, to foreign loans, property outside India, foreign bank accounts, and cross-border payments. Residents, non-residents, and entities (companies, **LLPs**, firms, trusts) are covered in different ways. Our FEMA work spans advisory, structuring, compliance, compounding, and representation so you can work with a clear, dated file for banks, **RBI** rounds, and **due diligence**.

> [!TIP] A tax or corporate structure is still a FEMA problem if a leg is not permitted, not reported, or not on time. Map tax, FEMA, and the **AD** bank before you sign.

## Applicability

- **All persons resident in India**, including Indian citizens, foreign nationals living in India, and Indian companies, for foreign exchange transactions the Act reaches.
- **Persons outside India** when the transaction concerns Indian assets, Indian companies, or Indian currency.
- **Non-resident Indians (NRIs)** for investments in India, **NRO** / **NRE** / **FCNR** maintenance, remittances, and property.
- **Indian companies** that receive FDI, make ODI, or use cross-border arrangements.
- **LLPs**, partnership firms, trusts, and societies, where the rules in force apply to the fact pattern.
- **Importers and exporters** for trade finance, advance payments, and **export realisation** compliance.

FEMA is a **central** law, uniform across all states and union territories. **State** laws do not displace the **RBI** position on a cross-border act. The **DPIIT Consolidated FDI Policy** and **RBI** FEMA rules are different levers; your plan should map both.

## Key provisions

### Classification of transactions

| Type | What it covers | How it is usually treated |
|------|----------------|----------------------------|
| **Current account** | Trade, living expenses, travel, education (examples) | Generally allowed within **RBI** and government limits |
| **Capital account** | Investment, borrowing, lending across borders | Regulated; **RBI** permission unless explicitly on automatic route or free as stated |

### Major areas (indicative; confirm live circulars)

| Area | Governing reference | Key form or channel |
|------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **FDI** | FEMA (Non-debt Instruments) Rules, 2019 | **FC-GPR**, **FC-TRS**, **FIRMS** |
| **ODI** | FEMA (Overseas Investment) rules and regulations | **Form OI**, **Form FC**, **APR** |
| **ECB** | **RBI** ECB master directions | **ECB** registration, **ECB-2**, **AD** line |
| **Export** of goods and services | FEMA (current account) rules | **GR** / **SDF** form, **SOFTEX** (where they apply) |
| **Import** payments | FEMA (current account) rules | **AD** bank reporting, documentation |
| **NRI** investments | Deposit and NDI rules as they read | **NRO**, **NRE**, **FCNR** |
| **Immovable** property **outside** India | FEMA (acquisition) rules | **RBI** permission where required |
| **LRS** | **RBI** LRS for resident individuals (check current USD cap per **FY**) | **Form** **A2**, **AD** **bank** |
| **Compounding** | FEMA s. 15, **RBI** | Compounding application for contraventions |

### Key definitions (short)

- **Person resident in India (PRI):** In broad terms, a person in India for more than 182 days in the preceding **FY**, subject to stated exceptions. Confirm for your year and category.
- **Person resident outside India (PROI):** Anyone who is not a **PRI** under the test that applies to them.
- **Authorised** **dealer (AD) bank:** A scheduled commercial bank **authorised** by **RBI** to deal in foreign exchange. Most filings and remittances run through the **AD** line.

## Benefits

- Fewer missed rules across the Act, rules, **master directions**, and **circulars** in your year; expert guidance helps avoid inadvertent violations.
- **FEMA** penalties can be up to 3x the **forex** amount involved; proactive, documented **compliance** **reduces** that **exposure**.
- **Cross-border** **deals** (FDI, ODI, **ECB**) are easier to **close** when structure, **reporting**, and **bank** **KYC** align with **RBI** and **AD** practice.
- **NRI** and family work: the right **account** type, **repatriation** line, and property rules, read with your **CA** for tax in your case.
- Where **defaults** have occurred, compounding before **RBI** and, where **needed**, representation before the **ED** or tribunals is easier with a full fact file.

## Limitations

- The framework is large and it changes with circulars; a static page does not update the law for your date or product.
- Old gaps can reappear in M&A due diligence or an IPO. Retrospective compounding is not a shortcut to a complete record.
- **ED** enforcement can be slower and heavier than **RBI** compounding alone; **ATFE** and the courts add time and cost.
- **RBI** amends **master** directions and circulars often. A structure that was compliant in the past may need a refresh for a new deal.

## Process

1. **Transaction review and FEMA mapping:** Identify the nature of the **forex** transaction and map it to the correct FEMA regulation, route (automatic vs **RBI** or government approval), and **reporting** requirement.
2. **Compliance planning:** Build a plan for required approvals, filing timelines, documents to collect, and **AD** **bank** coordination.
3. **Regulatory approvals (if required):** For transactions that need prior **RBI** or government approval, prepare and file the application with the correct authority.
4. **Transaction execution:** Execute the investment, remittance, or borrowing through **AD** **bank** channels as FEMA provides.
5. **Post-transaction reporting:** File the required **RBI** / **FIRMS** or **AD** **bank** reports within the prescribed windows. Keep acknowledgement and reference numbers (for example, **UIN** where **RBI** issues one).
6. **Ongoing compliance:** Meet periodic reporting (for example, annual **FLA**, **ECB-2** where **ECB** applies, **APR** for **ODI**).
7. **Compounding (if relevant):** If past contraventions are found, file a compounding application with **RBI** with the prescribed fee and a full fact narrative.

## Documents (general; varies by matter)

| Category | What we usually need |
|----------|----------------------|
| Entity | Certificate of incorporation, **MoA**/**AoA**, **PAN**, **GST** registration (if applicable) |
| **KYC** | **PAN**, **Aadhaar** or passport, board resolution, any **FATCA**-style set the **AD** **bank** requests |
| **Forex** records | **FIRC**, **Form** **A2** (where the rule applies), **bank** **statements** for the inflow or outflow **legs** |
| Prior FEMA | Earlier **FC-GPR**/**ODI**/**ECB** **filings** and **RBI** **acknowledgements** for continuity |
| **Agreements** | Share purchase, subscription, loan, service, or similar agreements that define the **forex** **legs** |
| **Valuation** | **DCF**/**NAV** **certs** when **FEMA** **prices** the **securities** **legs** |
| **CA** or **auditor** | Auditor or **CA** certificates when a circular or the **AD** **bank** asks for a specific format |

## Cost / professional fees (indicative, plus GST at 18%)

| Service | Estimated fee (INR) |
|---------|---------------------|
| FEMA advisory or one-off transaction review | 5,000 – 20,000 |
| FDI filing (FC-GPR or FC-TRS) | 8,000 – 20,000 |
| ODI filing (Form OI class) | 10,000 – 25,000 |
| ECB filing and ongoing compliance | 15,000 – 40,000 |
| LRS or NRI remittance advice | 3,000 – 8,000 |
| NRI account and holding structure | 10,000 – 30,000 |
| RBI compounding application | 30,000 – 1,00,000+ |
| ED or tribunal representation | 50,000 – 3,00,000+ (depends on facts) |
| Annual FEMA retainer | 50,000 – 2,00,000 p.a. |
| FEMA due diligence (M&A or IPO) | 30,000 – 1,50,000 |

ED representation can vary widely with complexity and amounts. **RBI** form fees are often nominal; confirm the live fee schedule.

## Compliance requirements (summary)

- **Current account:** Many items are permitted with proper documentation; some need **Form** **A2** and fall under **LRS** (check the current USD cap per **FY** per resident individual).
- **Capital account:** Specific approval or automatic route rules apply; **post-deal** **reporting** to **RBI** is often mandatory.
- **ECB:** Monthly **ECB-2** to **RBI** through the **AD** **bank**; loan agreement reporting in the window the **master** **direction** states.
- **Export realisation:** Proceeds must be realised within the period the **RBI** rule states (for example, nine months from shipment for many exports, with extensions for certain categories as per circular).
- **SOFTEX:** Software and **IT** services exporters may need **SOFTEX** with **STPI**/**RBI** as the product line requires.
- **Records:** Keep FEMA-related records, contracts, and correspondence for at least five years unless a longer period applies to your case.

## Frequently asked questions

**Are Indian students abroad covered by FEMA?** Yes. Students are usually **PRI** for an initial period. Remittances for education and living costs can use **LRS** up to the current USD cap per **FY**, with **Form** **A2** and **TCS** rules as they read. Residency under FEMA can change after a long stay abroad.

**Can an Indian company borrow from a foreign parent without RBI approval?** Sometimes, if the loan is an **ECB** on the automatic route. It must meet **RBI** **ECB** rules on borrower, lender, maturity, end use, and cost cap. The agreement is reported through the **AD** **bank** in the stated window, and **ECB-2** continues monthly.

**NRO vs NRE?** **NRO** holds India-sourced income; repatriation beyond **USD** 1 million per year has conditions and needs clean tax compliance. **NRE** holds foreign earnings repatriated to India and is fully repatriable; **NRE** interest is usually tax-free in India while **NRO** interest is taxable. Pick the line with your **CA** for your fact pattern.

**What is a FEMA contravention?** Any breach of FEMA, including missed FDI reporting, unpermitted **ODI**, export proceeds not realised, or inbound payment off the banking channel. Penalties can be up to 3x the amount, or **INR** 2 lakh if not quantifiable, plus daily amounts for continuing default. Compounding is often available before **RBI** for many cases.

**Can a foreign national on a work visa buy property in India?** A foreign national who is **PRI** (long-stay employment or business) can buy **non-agricultural** **immovable** property in India subject to FEMA and registration law. **PRI** status, visa class, and agricultural land restrictions still matter. Short-stay visitors generally need **RBI** permission for purchase. Take **Title** and FEMA advice on the facts.

---

*Content is for general information. Get **FEMA** advice for your transaction, **entity**, and **date** from a qualified professional.*

services.name: FSSAI Renewal
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for FSSAI Renewal — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# FSSAI renewal (FoSCoS)

## Overview

FSSAI Renewal extends the life of a Basic Registration, State License, or Central License before the end date. Every certificate has 1 to 5 years of validity, chosen at issue or at last renewal. After expiry, the FBO is not lawfully in business under that FSSAI line and is exposed to penalties, show-cause, and stop-work risk.

Start early, ideally 60 days out and at least 30 days before the end date, as FSSAI practice recommends. Late renewal after expiry can add a 100 per day late fee in the standard FoSCoS rule. If the file lapses for a long time, you may need a fresh application and a new 14-digit number, with all the customer and listing updates that follow.

## Key features

- Online renewal on FoSCoS, often with pre-filled data from the current certificate.
- Time to update address, product list, owners, and to upgrade the licence class if the business has grown.
- 1 to 5 year extension when you pay the matching government fee.
- For State and Central, renewal can include inspection if the law or the authority schedules it.
- FSSAI may send 60- and 30-day reminders to the email and phone on the account, if they are up to date; you still need your own compliance calendar.

> [!TIP] If turnover crossed the line for a higher class (for example from Basic to State, or from State to Central, including multi-state), use renewal to upgrade instead of only renewing the wrong class.

## Applicability

Renewal is for any FBO with FSSAI Basic, State, or Central that is due to expire or that has lapsed, including:

- Petty and small FBOs on Basic;
- State manufacturers, restaurants, stores, and distributors;
- Central: large plant, importers, exporters, e-commerce, and multi-state.

State renewals are with the state licensing line on FoSCoS; Central with the Central authority. Basic is often at district-level processing in many states, still through FoSCoS.

## Benefits

- Unbroken legal right to make, store, and sell food under that certificate.
- Avoids 100 per day style late fees and operating-with-expired risk.
- Keeps the same 14-digit number when you renew in time, so packs and platforms need fewer changes.
- Marketplaces: Swiggy, Zomato, Amazon, and others expect current FSSAI; expired shows get suspended.
- Bank and some GST and audit use-cases for food often want a current FSSAI.
- A clean string of on-time renewals is a good sign in M&A, franchise, or funding work.
- One window to fix old modifications you forgot and to add products or locations if rules allow in renewal.

## Limitations

- There is no legal grace day after printed expiry, operation in the gap is a defect.
- Late fee is 100 per day in the common FSSAI rule, so long delay is costly.
- Lapsed a long time: authority may only allow a new application, not renewal.
- State and Central renewals can include inspection, hidden problems if you never filed modifications.
- FSSAI will not backdate, any gap in the line stays a compliance gap in the fact pattern.
- At renewal, turnover may be rechecked, you may be told to move to a higher class.

## Process

1. Check the expiry date on FoSCoS and in your compliance calendar, and start 60 days out where possible.
2. Compare the live business to the certificate: if anything is wrong, plan modification, renewal, or both.
3. Check turnover and activity for a possible upgrade in licence type.
4. On FoSCoS, use Renewal, confirm pre-filled data, and correct any field that is now old.
5. Upload fresh proofs as the portal and state checklist ask (premises, products, KYC, water, pest, medical for handlers, as applicable).
6. Choose 1 to 5 years; longer periods can cut the number of future deadlines.
7. Pay renewal fee; if you are in the late period, the portal usually adds the late part.
8. If inspection is required, show the site and clear observations in time.
9. Download the renewed certificate and put it on packs, in stores, and on every online profile; update the QR pack if a new one is issued.

## Documents required

| Document | When |
|----------|------|
| Current FSSAI certificate | Always |
| Renewal form on FoSCoS | As per product |
| Updated address, product list, KYC, MoA, deed | If changed |
| Turnover or CA certificate if class may change | When upgrading |
| Site photos, water test, medical, pest, NOC, fire NOC | When State or Central and local rules ask |

## Government fees and validity (illustrative)

| Type | Renew fee (per year) | Validity you can pick |
|------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Basic | 100 | 1 to 5 years |
| State | 2,000 to 5,000, varies by activity | 1 to 5 years |
| Central | 7,500 | 1 to 5 years |
| Late renewal | 100 per day, after expiry, per usual FoSCoS rule | N/A |

## Professional fees (indicative, plus GST at 18%)

| Service | INR range |
|---------|------------|
| Basic renewal | 1,500 to 3,000 |
| State renewal | 3,000 to 7,000 |
| Central renewal | 6,000 to 15,000 |
| Late renewal with penalty | 3,000 to 8,000 |
| Combined renewal and modification | 4,000 to 12,000 |
| New file after long lapse | 3,000 to 10,000 |

## Validity and renewal (cycle)

A renewed certificate gets a new end date from the date of the renewal order, not a silent extension of the old line. A five year choice at renewal is common for less admin. After that, the same cycle repeats, set 90, 60, and 30 day internal reminders, do not wait only for FSSAI email.

## Frequently asked questions

**Expired 2 months ago, can I renew?** It depends on state practice and how long the gap is. In many areas a short post-expiry renewal is still possible with late fee. A long break may need a new registration or licence, and the period of operation without a valid FSSAI can still be a past violation, take advice for your state.

**Run the kitchen while renewal is in progress?** If you applied before the old end date, continuation during pendency is often read as allowed under the deemed / continuation type principles in the FSSAI / FoSCoS world, as your professional will explain. If you apply only after expiry, the gap is usually unlawful operation.

**Add products and renew together?** Yes, FoSCoS can run a joint modification and renewal, often with one inspection, file early to avoid a gap at the end date.

**One outlet, now five, two states.** Multi-state and scale usually needs Central, not a simple State renew on one unit. Get a design before the old licence expires, often surrender old state blocks and one Central.

**Does FSSAI remind you?** FSSAI and FoSCoS can email or SMS, but the FBO is responsible for the date; keep a calendar, wrong contact in FoSCoS means no reminder to you.

---

*This note is for awareness. Use qualified FSSAI and food-safety help for your licence class and state.*

services.name: FSSAI Return Filing
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for FSSAI Return Filing — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# FSSAI return filing (Form D-1, Form D-2, FoSCoS)

## Overview

FSSAI return filing is a periodic statutory duty for certain food business operators under the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, and the Food Safety and Standards (Licensing and Registration of Food Businesses) Regulations, 2011. The returns that most licensed manufacturers and importers see are:

- Form D-1, annual: for every State or Central **manufacturer** and for **importers** on the correct licence, for the year April 1 to March 31, with a filing deadline in the next May, usually 31 May (use the FSSAI / FoSCoS notice in force for your year).
- Form D-2, half-yearly: for **milk and milk product** manufacturers, with two parts of the year and deadlines in the October and April window (again, confirm the live calendar on FoSCoS).

The authority uses returns to see volumes, product mix, and compliance. Non-filing or wrong filing can mean penalties, renewal holds, and inspection risk. Many SMEs only discover returns at renewal, enforcement is stricter, so the return should be in your annual compliance stack.

## Key features

| Return | Who (typical) | Period / deadline (confirm live) |
|--------|----------------|-----------------------------------|
| D-1 annual | State or Central **manufacturers** and **importers**; not Basic-only PFBO, not pure trade without own manufacture in the D-1 sense | Financial year, file by following May, often 31 May |
| D-2 half-yearly | Licensed **dairy** manufacturers | H1 about April to September, file by 31 Oct; H2 about October to March, file by 30 Apr |

> [!TIP] Keep product-wise, month-wise production and sales books so the return matches GST, stock, and FSSAI licence categories before you press submit, amendment on FoSCoS is not a simple one-click.

## Applicability

D-1 is mandatory in principle for licensed (State or Central) food **manufacturing** and for **importers** as the regulations apply. It is not in scope for Basic Registration; pure traders, retailers, and distributors with no in-house **manufacturing**; and storage, transport, and most restaurants and caterers that do not **manufacture**, unless a special rule applies. If you add in-house packaged product under your licence, the D-1 **manufacturing** obligation can attach.

**D-2** is for **milk and milk product** units as defined in the return rules.

**Pan-India** for every state and union territory where a covered licence is issued.

**Where to file** depends on the licence: State licence with the state authority path on FoSCoS; Central with the central path, not the geography of your sales per se.

## Benefits

- Stays on the right side of FSS Act penalties, often quoted in the 1 to 5 lakh range for some breaches.
- Smoother renewal: pending returns are a common hold on the file.
- Verifiable record for government, export, and B2B on-boarding.
- Aligns with GST and income-tax story when numbers are one consistent set.
- Due diligence: buyers like a clean D-1 history.
- Exporters: APEDA, MPEDA, and global buyers may expect FSSAI track record, returns support that.

## Limitations

- Traders and restaurants often think they need D-1, they do not, **unless** they manufacture, confusion causes wrong filings or false confidence.
- Small plants may lack MRP, batch, and category-wise data, the return is harder than it looks.
- FoSCoS does not make late correction easy; re-file or letter to the authority, case by case, so the first file must be right.
- Penalty for missing is large compared to the work of one annual compile.
- Portal stress on deadline day is real, file early in May.
- Tier 2 and 3 under-reporting is common, and enforcement is rising.

## Process

1. **Scope:** For your licence, decide D-1, D-2, or both (dairy and general manufacture in one business).
2. **Data:** Pull production, import, sell, and closing stock, by FSSAI product class and your licence list.
3. **Match:** Reconcile to GSTR-1, GSTR-3B, and financials, fix gaps before the authority sees them.
4. **FoSCoS:** Log in, open Annual or Half-yearly return, licence details usually auto-populate.
5. **Form:** Enter each line as FoSCoS asks, quantity in MT, KL, units, or the unit in the return for the year.
6. **Check:** No blank mandatory cell, product names match the licence.
7. **Submit:** Before the hard date, print or save the acknowledgement and reference.
8. **Store:** Keep workpapers and the filed return, many teams keep 5 years for tax and M&A, FSSAI inspection often asks 2+ years in practice.

## Documents required (internal, for preparation)

| Item | Use |
|------|------|
| State or Central FSSAI licence | Eligibility, categories |
| Product-wise make and sell data | D-1 body |
| Import papers for importers | B/E, CIF, quantity, HS |
| GSTR-1, GSTR-3B, books | Reconcile |
| Product list and FSSAI codes | Match categories |
| Recall or complaint file | If the form asks in your year |
| Last year D-1 or D-2 | Continuity, bridge |
| Milk procurement, processing, D-2 | Dairy units |

## Fees

| Type | Government fee | Note |
|------|----------------|------|
| D-1 | Nil | As general practice for the return only |
| D-2 | Nil | As general practice for the return only |
| Operate in breach if return missing | FSS Act and licence terms | Penalty can apply at inspection or renewal |

**Professional (indicative, plus GST at 18%):**

| Service | INR range |
|---------|------------|
| D-1, simple, one category | 2,000 to 4,000 |
| D-1, many categories | 4,000 to 8,000 |
| D-2, one return | 2,500 to 5,000 |
| Data and reconciliation | 3,000 to 8,000 |
| Many past years, catch-up | 8,000 to 20,000 |
| Retainer, returns and advisory | 15,000 to 40,000 p.a. |

## Validity and use of filed returns

Filed returns are a permanent record of that year; they are most used at renewal, FSO visit, and M&A. Keep them with your FSSAI dataroom. At renewal, open returns can block the next licence, file catch-up first.

## Frequently asked questions

**State licence restaurant, no factory pack. D-1?** No, if you only cook and serve and do not manufacture a packaged product under the licence, D-1 is for manufacture and importers, not a pure restaurant, if you add packed sauces or snacks under license, the manufacturing prong is in play.

**We manufacture in one state and sell in India, where we file?** The manufacturer files where the **licence** is: state licensing office path for a state licence, central for a central. Sales geography is not the trigger for the office, the licence type is.

**What is in D-1?** Product, FSSAI category, quantity made, import if any, sold, and stock, often in MT or KL, plus recall line if the form in your year has it, follow FoSCoS help text.

**Small organic jam unit, state licence. Accountant said no D-1.** FSSAI usually expects D-1 for licensed **manufacturing**, size is not a general exemption, unless you are only Basic, check your class.

**Change after file?** No easy re-open on FoSCoS; write the authority, attach evidence, in genuine error cases. Better to get it right before the clock runs in May.

---

*This page is for general information. D-1 and D-2 scope, dates, and fields change with FSSAI notifications, confirm FoSCoS and a food-compliance adviser for your licence.*

services.content:
# FSSAI modification (FoSCoS)

## Overview

FSSAI Modification updates an existing FSSAI Registration or License when the business changes, for example premises, product list, legal name, partners or directors, capacity, new unit, or activity type. Food business operators must keep the certificate true. Working on an old certificate after a move, a bigger range of products, or a new owner is non-compliance and can lead to cancellation and penalties.

Modification is filed on FoSCoS with evidence. Some changes are paper-based and quick; others need a Food Safety Officer visit. Good modification discipline helps with audits, marketplace checks, and investor or franchise due diligence.

## Key features

- One portal (FoSCoS) for Basic, State, and Central paths, with state and central officers as the rules set.
- Types of change: name, address, categories, capacity, partners, signatory, upgrade from Basic to State or State to Central, and more.
- Minor changes may avoid inspection; premises, capacity, or new units often trigger a site visit.
- Modified certificate keeps the same expiry date as the original in a simple modification, until you renew. A full upgrade can start a new validity window (see FoSCoS for the case you file).

> [!TIP] If the business is near renewal, your consultant may file modification and renewal together to avoid conflicting data in the FSSAI database.

## Applicability

Modification applies to any current FSSAI Basic, State, or Central holder when there is a change such as:

- Legal or trade name (map whether the change is only brand v. legal entity with your CA and FoSCoS).
- Address of manufacturing, warehouse, or retail.
- Add or remove food product categories.
- Change in proprietor, partners, or directors.
- Installed capacity or new manufacturing block.
- New manufacturing premises.
- Nature of business (for example retail to manufacturing).
- Contact, email, mobile, or authorised signatory.
- Upgrade of tier: Basic to State, State to Central.

All India: state-level files are with the State Food Safety Department; Central License with FSSAI Central Licensing Authority. FoSCoS is the common application front.

## Benefits

- Keeps the licence legally aligned with how you actually operate.
- Inspections and marketplace checks match the certificate, so fewer mismatch notices.
- You can add categories in a controlled way instead of informal production.
- Buyers and investors see an up-to-date FSSAI record.
- Timely address change can avoid the need for a separate fresh licence when one modification will do.
- Renewal is easier when the record is already correct.

## Limitations

- Premises and plant changes often mean inspection and longer timelines.
- Non-inspection paths are still often up to 30 days; inspection cases can run 30 to 60 days or more.
- FSSAI does not backdate; if you moved last year and only modify now, the gap can be a defect on the file.
- Big capacity steps can need a Central file instead of a small State amendment.
- Adding high-risk categories can force a higher licence class, not only a line add.
- Many changes in one form can slow review and increase query risk.

## Process

1. List what changed and whether FoSCoS treats it as minor, major, or upgrade.
2. Log in to FoSCoS and open Modification against the current FSSAI number.
3. Complete the modification form with accurate new data; wrong fields cause rejection or delay.
4. Upload supporting PDFs or images in the size and format FoSCoS asks for.
5. Pay the modification fee for your category and type of change.
6. Wait for review; attend inspection if scheduled and close observations.
7. Download the modified certificate and update packs, boards, marketplaces, and contracts with the new detail.
8. If the change is only part of a growth path, plan renewal and any upgrade in the same timeline.

## Documents required

| Document | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| Copy of existing FSSAI certificate | Reference |
| Modification form on FoSCoS | As completed online |
| New address proof, NOC, layout | For premises change |
| Updated product list | For category add or remove |
| New MoA, board resolution, partnership deed | For structure or name change |
| Affidavit for change in management | If required in your file |
| Machinery list, layout, capacity | For production change |
| CA turnover or net-worth letter | If the change forces upgrade |
| Photo and KYC of new signatory or new partner | KYC and signatory change |

## Government fees (approximate; confirm in FoSCoS)

| Modification | Approx. government fee (INR) |
|--------------|------------------------------|
| Basic Registration | 100 to 200 |
| State, minor | 1,000 to 2,000 |
| State, major (new site or capacity) | 2,000 to 5,000 |
| Central, minor | 2,500 to 5,000 |
| Central, major | 5,000 to 7,500 |

## Professional fees (indicative, plus GST at 18%)

| Service | INR range |
|---------|------------|
| Basic modification, end to end | 1,500 to 3,000 |
| State modification | 3,000 to 7,000 |
| Central modification | 6,000 to 15,000 |
| Eligibility and route consult | 1,000 to 2,500 |
| Inspection support | 2,000 to 5,000 |

## Validity and renewal

A simple modification does not by itself reset the end date. You renew on the same cycle unless you have upgraded the licence, in which case the new class may have a new validity. Prefer to clear modifications before a messy renewal, or file modification and renewal together with professional help so the database stays consistent.

## Frequently asked questions

**Only the brand name changed, not the company.** If the legal entity is unchanged, FSSAI may not need a full modification for a pure brand refresh. If the legal name on MCA and COI changed, you usually modify FSSAI with the new name and support papers.

**New category, for example we added dairy to bakery.** You need a modification to add the category, and you must not make the new line until the modified licence allows it, unless a temporary bridge is in law for your case (rare; treat as not allowed until cleared). The officer may visit.

**We moved within the same city.** File modification for address, expect inspection, and do not run the new site on the old address on the certificate.

**How long?** Many document-only files finish in about 30 days; inspection cases often need more. Track status on FoSCoS. Deemed rules may exist for some non-inspection cases if the authority is silent, subject to the FSSAI notice in your year.

**We want to add manufacturing on Basic.** Only if the full business still fits Basic and the new activity is not a restricted line. If you move into a high-risk or import path, you may need a new State or Central file instead of a light modification.

---

*General information. Take case-specific FSSAI and food-law advice for your state and product mix.*

services.name: GSTR 10 (Final Return)
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for GSTR 10 (Final Return) — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# GSTR-10 final return (on cancellation and similar end states)

## Overview

GSTR-10 is the final return to be filed when registration is cancelled or surrendered. It is not the same as the annual 9, and the due date in law is a fixed window from the date of cancellation, subject to the year’s form and the portal text. The return captures closing stock, ITC, output tax, and the bridge to the ITC-03 story where the rules need it, so that 9 and audit can see a full path from open to close.

## Key features

- One return per event of cancellation, with 16 and 10 links and stock truth.
- ITC-03: reversal or payment on stock, inputs, and capital goods as the year’s rule and form ask.
- DRC-03 in the same story if 10 or review finds a gap in tax for the tranche.

## Benefits

- Clean closure, fewer 50 and DRC-04 class issues on a dead GSTIN.
- A single, dated pack for 9, bank, and M&A due diligence on that GSTIN.

## Process

1. Reconcile 1, 2B, 3B, and stock up to the effective date of cancel.
2. File ITC-03 when in scope, and pay if the law does not let you just reverse in the 10.
3. File 10 in the time window, with 16 and the cancel order in hand.
4. File DRC-03 if 10 or working finds a tax gap.

## Documents

| Item | Use |
|------|-----|
| Cancel order, 16, last 1, 2B, 3B | 10 and date trail |
| Stock, WIP, capital goods, ITC-03 | Credit and 10 line |
| Bank, DRC, payment of tax | 10, DRC-03 |

## Fees (indicative, professional, excl. GST 18%)

| Service | Band (rupees) |
|---------|-----------------|
| Tidy, single 10 | 4,000 to 10,000 |
| Stock, 03, 10, DRC in a bad year | 10,000 to 40,000 plus |
| Government: late on 10 | As per the Act, not the 3B late fee in some years |

## Applicability

Every GSTIN that is cancelled, except where a specific exemption in the Act applies for a class of person, on advice.

## Common mistakes (short)

- 10 with stock that is not the same as the last 3B and 1.
- Forgetting 03, or 10 after the 03 window, when the law needs both in a set order.
- 9 filed but 10 still open, or 10 in the wrong financial year of effect.

## Frequently asked questions

**Cancel in March, 10 in new FY?** Map the “from date of order” and the 10 form text for the year; do not use a web post from 2018.

*All service content is for informational purposes. Specific GST advice should be obtained from qualified practitioners based on your business facts, transactions, and the law in force.*

services.name: ICEGATE Registration
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for ICEGATE Registration — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# ICEGATE registration

## Overview

**ICEGATE** (Indian Customs Electronic Gateway) is the national portal of the **Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs (CBIC)**, Government of India. It is the single electronic interface between trade, cargo, and Indian Customs: e-filing of import and export documents, online payment of customs duties, consignment tracking, and access to customs-related services. Developed and maintained by the **National Informatics Centre (NIC)** with **CBIC**, it handles a very large volume of customs transactions each year.

The portal links to **ICES** (Indian Customs EDI System), the **DGFT** portal for EXIM policy, **RBI** for FEMA reporting, the **GST** portal for e-way bill and refund linkages, and **Port Community Systems (PCS)** at major seaports and airports.

**ICEGATE registration** is how an importer, exporter, **customs house agent (CHA)**, freight forwarder, shipping line, airline, port operator, or other trade entity registers on ICEGATE to use these services. Without a valid ICEGATE registration, you cannot electronically file **Bills of Entry** (imports) or **Shipping Bills** (exports), which are central to customs clearance.

**ICEGATE** is distinct from **IEC**: **IEC** is DGFT-issued permission to carry on import or export; **ICEGATE** is the customs channel through which filings and payments are processed.

## Key services after registration

- Electronic filing of **Bills of Entry** and **Shipping Bills**
- Online payment of customs duty, **IGST**, and other levies through **ICEPAY**
- Real-time tracking of consignment status and clearance progress
- Duty drawback status and refund tracking
- **e-Sanchit**, paperless upload of supporting documents
- **SWIFT** (Single Window Interface for Facilitating Trade) for imports needing NOC from multiple agencies
- Export promotion scheme tracking: **MEIS**, **RoDTEP**, **Advance Authorisation**, and similar
- **Risk Management System (RMS)** for assessment and examination
- **IGST** refund for exporters, linked to **GSTN** where the process is automated for eligible categories

## Applicability

**ICEGATE registration** is needed by:

- **Importers** who file or cause **Bills of Entry** to be filed
- **Exporters** who file or cause **Shipping Bills** to be filed
- **CHAs** / customs brokers filing on behalf of others
- **Freight forwarders**, **shipping lines**, **airlines** (e.g. **IGM** / **EGM** where applicable)
- **Port operators**, **ICDs**, **CFS** operators, **steamer agents**, **air cargo agents**
- **Banks** in the online duty payment chain
- **Export promotion councils** for **RCMC** and related data links

**Geography:** Pan-India across major customs stations, e.g. **JNCH** (Nhava Sheva), Chennai, Kolkata, Mundra, Kandla, Hazira, **IGIA** Delhi, **CSIA** Mumbai, Bengaluru, Hyderabad, and other notified ports, **ICDs**, and **CFSs**.

## Benefits

- **Paperless clearance:** BoE, SB, manifests, and annexures filed electronically; less physical submission at customs houses
- **24×7 access** for filing, duty payment, and tracking
- **Faster clearance:** Data flows into **ICES** and **RMS** for risk-based processing; compliant cargo can clear with minimal examination
- **e-Sanchit:** Upload invoices, packing lists, certificates, licences, reference by **IRN**-style linkage in filings
- **SWIFT:** Single-window applications for **FSSAI**, quarantine, **BIS**, **CDSCO**, and other NOCs where the module applies
- **ICEPAY:** Net banking, **NEFT**, **RTGS**, or duty credit scrip, with quicker credit confirmation
- **IGST** refund automation where **ICEGATE** and **GSTN** data match for the category
- **Drawback**, **RoDTEP**, **MEIS** / **FPS** tracking and credit visibility where rules allow
- **Business insight** from consignment-level data for cost and cycle time analysis

## Limitations

- **Learning curve** for first-time users; correct user type and format matter
- **IEC** must be active on **DGFT** before **ICEGATE** validates successfully
- **Class 3 DSC** is often mandatory for **CHA**, shipping lines, and airline agents (extra cost and setup)
- **Port-specific** registration codes: multi-port operations may need multiple registrations or correct port mapping
- **Downtime** during maintenance can affect time-sensitive filings
- Many **SMEs** rely on a **CHA** and may not operate **ICEGATE** directly without support
- **Post-filing amendments** are harder; data quality at first submission is critical
- **e-Sanchit** file size limits can pinch large consignments

## Process

1. **IEC from DGFT:** Ensure **IEC** is active and aligned on **dgft.gov.in**; stale data blocks **ICEGATE** validation.
2. **Portal:** Open **icegate.gov.in**, use **Register** / **New User Registration**.
3. **User type:** **IE** (importer/exporter), **CHA**, **SL** (shipping line), **AIR**, port, bank, etc. Forms and documents differ by type.
4. **Form:** Entity name, **IEC**, **GSTIN**, **PAN**, contact, bank, port of operation; for **CHA**, licence number and **DSC** details.
5. **Upload:** **IEC**, **PAN**, **GST** proof, incorporation or partnership papers, authorisation where multi-user.
6. **DSC:** For **CHA** and carriers, register **Class 3 DSC** on the portal for signing filings.
7. **Verification:** **CBIC** / system checks against **IEC**, **PAN**, **GST**; many **IE** paths are largely automated.
8. **ICEGATE code:** User ID issued for future transactions; keep email and credentials secure.
9. **ICEPAY:** Link bank account for duty payment and complete bank-side checks.
10. **e-Sanchit:** If you upload supporting documents electronically, complete **e-Sanchit** setup for **IRN**-style references in **BoE** / **SB**.

## Documents required

| Document | Details |
|----------|---------|
| **IEC** | Active **DGFT** **IEC**, mandatory prerequisite |
| **PAN** | Entity **PAN** matching **IEC** |
| **GSTIN** | Active **GST** registration |
| **Constitution proof** | Certificate of incorporation, partnership deed, or equivalent |
| **Bank** | Account linked for **IEC** and **ICEPAY** |
| **Email, mobile** | For account creation and **OTP** |
| **Class 3 DSC** | For **CHA** / shipping / airline paths |
| **CHA licence** | **CBIC**-issued, for customs brokers |
| **Authorisation letter** | For additional users, where used |
| **Port** | Customs station for which registration is sought |
| **KYC of key persons** | **PAN**, **Aadhaar**, photos of directors or partners as asked |

## Fees

**Government (typical):**

| Registration type | Government fee | Remarks |
|-------------------|---------------|--------|
| Importer / exporter | Nil | Free |
| **CHA** | Nil | Licence cost is separate |
| Shipping / airline (portal) | Nil | |
| **DSC** | Nil on portal | **DSC** device often **₹800**–**₹3,000** or so to vendors |

**Professional (indicative, excl. GST @ 18%):**

| Service | Estimated fee (INR) |
|---------|---------------------|
| **IE** registration | 2,000 – 5,000 |
| **CHA** / forwarder | 5,000 – 12,000 |
| **DSC** + setup | 2,000 – 5,000 |
| **ICEPAY** bank linkage | 1,000 – 3,000 |
| **e-Sanchit** setup / training | 2,000 – 5,000 |
| Full onboarding | 8,000 – 20,000 |
| Error / troubleshooting | 3,000 – 8,000 |

## Timeline (typical)

| Step | Timeline |
|------|----------|
| **IEC** (if not held) | 1–3 working days |
| Form submission | 1 day |
| Validation and code | 2–5 working days |
| **DSC** (where needed) | 3–7 working days for token and registration |
| **ICEPAY** linkage | 1–3 working days |
| **e-Sanchit** | Same or next day often |
| **End-to-end first time** | 5–15 working days |

**CHAs** needing **DSC** may take up to about 15 working days. **IE** with **IEC** already in hand often land in about 3–7 working days for registration.

## Use cases (short)

1. **First-time machinery import:** **BoE**, duty on **ICEPAY**, track status in **ICES**, with **e-Sanchit** documents.
2. **Garment exporter, RoDTEP:** **Shipping bill** and scheme credit visibility through integrated data, with **RCMC** / **LUT** as applicable elsewhere.
3. **CHA onboarding:** **DSC**-based **ICEGATE** account to file for clients; licence plus portal registration.
4. **Pharma, SWIFT:** **CDSCO** / drug agency NOC in single-window flow with **BoE** where configured.
5. **E-commerce exports, IGST:** Track refund linkage between **ICEGATE** and **GSTR-1** / **GSTN** for eligible cases.

## Frequently asked questions

**Is ICEGATE the same as IEC?** No. **IEC** is **DGFT**’s authorisation to trade; **ICEGATE** is the **CBIC** portal for customs filings. **IEC** is almost always a prerequisite; you may still use only a **CHA**’s **ICEGATE** account, but you then lack direct visibility.

**We only use a CHA. Do we need our own ICEGATE?** Not strictly; the **CHA** can file. A direct account gives you live status, **ICEPAY**, drawback / incentive views, and direct communication, which many firms want as volume grows.

**We export only software. Do we need ICEGATE?** **ICEGATE** is for **goods** crossing customs. Pure digital service exports use **DGFT** / **GST** / **STPI**-**SOFTEX** and banking paths; **ICEGATE** matters if you ship tangible media, hardware, or other physical goods.

**We lost CHA login; how to recover?** **Helpdesk** at **icegate.gov.in** (e.g. helpdesk@icegate.gov.in, **1800-3010-1000**), with **IEC** and **PAN**; re-registration may be possible if recovery fails.

**Is e-Sanchit mandatory?** For electronic **BoE**, officers increasingly expect **e-Sanchit**-referenced documents instead of only paper. Treat it as effectively standard for clean clearance.

*All service content is for informational purposes. Specific import and customs advice should be obtained from qualified **DGFT** / **CHA** / customs practitioners based on your products, ports, and transactions.*

services.name: IEC Modification
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for IEC Modification — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# IEC modification

## Overview

**IEC modification** updates an existing **Import Export Code** on the **DGFT** portal when business facts change, or when you run the **mandatory annual IEC update** (usually **1 April to 30 June**). After **April 2021** integration, **IEC** is tied to **PAN** and **GSTN**; mismatches can block customs and scheme use. The **annual update** (confirm or change) is required even if nothing changed, or **IEC** may be **deactivated**, which blocks imports, exports, and many bank and scheme uses until fixed.

**IEC** is issued once per **PAN** in normal cases and remains the same 10 characters; the certificate reflects updated legal name, address, bank, directors, or branches.

## Why modification matters

- **Port and bank** checks compare **IEC** to **BoE** / **SB** and remittance data
- **GST** and **DGFT** alignment avoids cross-system friction
- **RCMC** and **schemes** (RoDTEP, AA, EPCG) need a live, accurate **IEC**
- **ICEGATE** profile must be updated **separately** after **IEC** changes, especially for bank and **ICEPAY**

## Applicability

**IEC modification** applies when:

- **Annual update** (April–June) for all holders, including “no change” confirmation
- **Address** of principal or branch
- **Bank** account / **IFSC** change
- **Directors** / **partners** / signatories
- **Legal name** after **RoC** / deed change
- **New** export or import **branches**
- **GSTIN** change, new registration, or cancellation in your structure
- **Proprietor** name change (gazette, **Aadhaar**, **PAN**)

**All India**, central **DGFT** process.

## Benefits

- Fewer **customs** mismatches and **BoE** / **SB** queries
- **Annual** confirmation avoids **deactivation** and a hard stop to trade
- **GSTN**-aligned **IEC** for clean **1**, **2B**, **3B** and **scheme** stories
- **Banks** and **FEMA**-style reconciliation on the declared account
- **RCMC** and **EPC** alignment when council records depend on **IEC** text
- **Legal** record matches **RoC** / deed reality

## Limitations

- **Annual** window is **mandatory**; missing it deactivates **IEC**
- **Reactivation** needs completing the overdue update, with possible gap risk for old shipments
- **PAN**-linked: fix **PAN** at **IT** first if the database name is wrong
- **New signatories** may need **DSC** / **Aadhaar** as the portal year requires
- **Dispute** or **suspension** on **IEC** can block modification until resolved
- **ICEGATE** does not auto-refresh; update profile after **DGFT** approval

## Process

1. **Login** at **dgft.gov.in**; during April–June, expect an **annual** prompt.
2. **Navigate** to **IEC** profile, **update** / **modify** as the menu shows for the year.
3. **Review** all fields: entity, address, bank, signatory, **GSTIN**, **directors** / **partners**.
4. **Edit** what changed, or **confirm** unchanged for **annual** compliance.
5. **Upload** proofs: address, bank certificate, **RoC** for name, resolution for signatory, etc., in **PDF** within size limits.
6. **Authenticate** with **Aadhaar** **OTP** and/or **DSC** as the portal requires for your entity type.
7. **Submit**; note **ARN**.
8. **DGFT** often clears simple changes in 1–3 days; name / complex cases can take longer.
9. **Download** updated **e-IEC**; number unchanged, details new.
10. **Update** **ICEGATE**, **RCMC**, and **AD bank** as needed.

## Documents (by change type)

| Document | When needed |
|----------|-------------|
| **ANF-2A**-class online form | All modifications |
| **PAN** | All |
| **Aadhaar** / **DSC** of signatory | All, per portal rule |
| Address proof (bill, rent, title) | Address change |
| Bank certificate / cancelled cheque | Bank change |
| **CoI** / name change order | Name change |
| **MoA** / **AoA** / **ROC** order | Company name or structure |
| Board resolution, new signatory | Director / signatory change |
| New director **PAN** / **Aadhaar** | Director add |
| Updated partnership deed | Partner change |
| **GST** certificate | If **GSTIN** changed |
| Trade / **MSME** (if any) | Supporting business proof |

## Fees

| Type | Government fee |
|------|----------------|
| **IEC** modification | Nil |
| **Annual** update | Nil |

**Professional (indicative, excl. GST @ 18%):**

| Service | Band (INR) |
|---------|------------|
| Simple address / bank | 1,000 – 3,000 |
| Director or name | 2,500 – 6,000 |
| Restructure / complex | 5,000 – 12,000 |
| **Annual** only, no change | 500 – 1,500 |
| **Annual** + changes | 2,000 – 5,000 |
| **ICEGATE** sync after **IEC** | 1,000 – 3,000 |
| **Reactivation** after lapse | 2,000 – 5,000 |

## Timeline

| Stage | Typical |
|-------|---------|
| Document prep | 1–3 days |
| **DGFT** submit | Same day |
| **Standard** approval | 1–3 days |
| **Name** / heavy docs | 3–7 days |
| **ICEGATE** update | 1–2 days |
| **End-to-end** (simple) | 2–5 days |
| **End-to-end** (complex) | 5–10 days |

**Annual** “no change” can be instant on login once confirmed.

## Use cases (short)

1. **Office moved:** Update **PPOB** before the next **SB** / **BoE** uses old address.
2. **Annual, no change:** **Confirm** in window to stay active.
3. **Funding, new directors:** **Modify** list and new **authorised** person **DSC** / **Aadhaar** path.
4. **Bank** switch: **One** primary account on **IEC**; change **IEC** before routing export proceeds to new bank.
5. **New** **Silvassa** **branch** export: **Map** extra branch in **IEC** so filings match place of business.

## Frequently asked questions

**Name change after RoC, new IEC?** **Modify**, do not re-issue; same **PAN**-linked **IEC** number, new legal name on certificate with **CoI** / **RoC** proof.

**Missed April–June, IEC deactivated?** Log in, complete the **annual** / overdue update; **reactivation** usually in a few days, no separate government fee, but check operations during the gap period on advice.

**Proprietor married, name change?** **Modify** with gazette, **Aadhaar**, **PAN** in one aligned name; **IEC** number stays the same.

**Add second bank, keep first?** **DGFT** link is typically **one** primary **IEC** account at a time; change to the account that will take export or import money under your **FEMA** plan.

**Merger, which IEC?** Surviving entity’s **IEC** continues, with post-merger **IEC** and entity updates; **absorbed** company’s **IEC** surrendered separately; **M&A** needs coordinated **DGFT**, **ICEGATE**, **RCMC**, and bank work.

*All service content is for informational purposes. Specific **EXIM** advice should be obtained from qualified **DGFT** and customs / **CHA** professionals.*

services.name: RCMC Registration
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for RCMC Registration — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
# RCMC registration (import export council)

## Overview

The **RCMC (Registration-cum-Membership Certificate)** is issued by an Export Promotion Council (EPC) or Commodity Board to register the exporter as a member of that body. It is both your industry registration and your proof of membership. For many Foreign Trade Policy benefits (RoDTEP, Advance Authorisation, EPCG, and similar schemes) you need a valid RCMC from the council that covers your product line, and you must link it to your IEC on the DGFT portal. The correct EPC depends on the main HS heading you export, for example APEDA for a large share of food and agri, MPEDA for marine, EEPC for engineering, GJEPC for gems and jewellery, and others. Some firms need more than one RCMC if they export very different product families.

## Key features

- Dual use: registration with the council and membership fees in one certificate in the usual path.
- Must match the product and the council; a wrong council can block a benefit or a customs check in a few lines.
- IEC is the first step; RCMC comes after.
- Five-year style life in many councils, with annual membership **fees** during the period. Some bodies use a slightly different calendar; read the certificate.
- E-RCMC and DGFT linkage: the exporter’s profile on the DGFT site should show the RCMC so that scheme forms pre-fill or validate.

## Benefits

- Claim export benefits that the current FTP and the scheme condition list for an active RCMC.
- For some agri and marine lines, APEDA and MPEDA registration is also a gate to phytosanitary and export certification, not only for benefit.
- Access to buyer-seller meets, MDA, and EPC intelligence and help-desks.
- Certificate of origin and FTA help in some councils.
- Stronger supplier pass in RFPs that want an EPC card.

## Limitations

- Multi-product exporters may need many RCMCs and many annual fees.
- If you miss annual fees, the council can mark you as not in good standing; benefits and some clearances can fail.
- Wrong product-to-council mapping is a common fault; use the HS map and the council’s help desk.
- RCMC is not the same as IEC; both are often required, in that order.

## Process

1. Take an **IEC** from DGFT if you do not already have one.
2. Map your export product to the right EPC or board (HS and the council’s sector list).
3. File the online or offline form of that council with company, IEC, tax, and bank proof.
4. Pay the membership and certificate fee to the council.
5. On approval, receive the RCMC. Note the certificate number and valid years.
6. Log in to the DGFT portal and add the RCMC under RCMC management or the current path so that scheme and benefit tools see it.
7. Pay each year’s membership on time and refresh the RCMC when the five-year block ends if the council needs a new file.

## Documents required (typical)

| Document | Notes |
|----------|--------|
| IEC | Required first |
| EPC application form | Each council’s own |
| Incorporation or partnership | Legal person |
| PAN, GST | Company and firm |
| Banker’s letter or bank proof | As the council asks |
| Export performance, if renewal or for some boards | Sales or FOB where needed |
| Udyam, if MSME fee tier | If any |
| DSC of signatory | For e-filing where used |
| Photo of signatory | Some boards still want a file copy |

## Fees (indicative, move with EPC board)

| Council or board | Membership or RCMC (band) |
|--------------------|---------------------------|
| FIEO | ₹5,000 – ₹15,000 per year class |
| APEDA | About ₹5,900 for a long block in many years (check site) |
| EEPC India | ₹5,000 – ₹25,000 per year style |
| PHARMEXCIL | ₹10,000 – ₹25,000 per year style |
| AEPC, GJEPC, CHEMEXCIL | ₹5,000 – ₹30,000 by class and size |

| Professional (est.) | Fee |
|----------------------|-----|
| IEC + RCMC together | ₹5,000 – ₹12,000 |
| One council file | ₹2,000 – ₹6,000 |
| Multi-council management | ₹5,000 – ₹15,000 |

Professional fees are often plus GST at 18%. Council fees go to the council.

## Applicability

Any Indian firm that exports and needs an EPC or board for FTP benefits, or that must show APEDA or MPEDA as a non-tariff gate for their line, in any state. The council is chosen by product, not by state.

## Validity

Many RCMCs are valid for five years from the start of the April block the council uses, with **annual** membership in between. At the end of the block, file a fresh RCMC if the council does not auto-extend. Always read the note on the PDF you hold.

## Frequently asked questions

**Can I export without RCMC?** For many lines, yes, if you do not take FTP benefits and your product is not in a line that needs APEDA or MPEDA for port or health steps. For benefits, you almost always need the right RCMC.

**Garments and engineering: one or two RCMCs?** Two, if you export both; AEPC for apparel and EEPC for engineering, with the same IEC.

**RCMC vs IEC?** IEC is the 10-digit DGFT code for import and export. RCMC is membership in a sector council for that product; it is not a replacement for IEC.

**How to link RCMC to IEC?** On the DGFT portal, after login with the IEC, use the RCMC or similar service to key the council name, number, dates, and HS lines. The portal will try to match to the e-RCMC feed where live.

**Expired two months, can I claim old RoDTEP?** Many scheme engines test RCMC and IEC as of the **export** or **shipment** date in the file. If the RCMC was valid for that day, a late renewal does not by itself void that past ship if the claim window is open. For **future** ships, fix membership first.

*All content is for informational purposes. Specific government registration, tax, and compliance advice should be obtained from qualified professionals based on your entity’s circumstances, state, and business activities.*

services.name: One Person Company Annual Compliance
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for One Person Company Annual Compliance — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## Overview

A One Person Company (OPC), introduced under the Companies Act, 2013, allows a single entrepreneur to operate a corporate entity with limited liability. While it enjoys several relaxations compared to private limited companies, OPCs must still comply with annual filing requirements under the MCA, Income Tax Department, and GST laws where applicable.

## Applicability

OPC annual compliance applies uniformly across India. Every OPC registered under the Companies Act, 2013 must comply, regardless of whether it has conducted business.

## Key features

| Theme | What it covers |
| --- | --- |
| MCA | AOC-4, MGT-7A, director KYC |
| AGMs | OPCs are exempt from AGMs, but financials and filings are mandatory |
| Audit | Statutory audit is mandatory for every OPC |
| Tax | ITR-6, tax audit when applicable, GST as per registration |

## Compliance requirements

| Compliance | Form | Authority | Due date |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Financial statements | AOC-4 | MCA | Within 180 days of FY end |
| Annual return | MGT-7A | MCA | Within 60 days of AGM equivalent |
| Director KYC | DIR-3 KYC | MCA | September 30 |
| Statutory audit | Auditor report | ICAI | Annual (mandatory) |
| Income tax return | ITR-6 | IT Dept | October 31 |
| Tax audit | Form 3CA-3CD | IT Dept | If applicable |
| Board meetings | Minutes | Internal | Min 2 per year, 90-day gap |
| GST returns | GSTR-1, 3B, 9 | GST | Monthly / annual |

## Benefits

- Continuity of limited liability and separate legal entity status
- Easier path to convert to a private limited company when thresholds are crossed
- Better credibility for bank loans and B2B contracts
- Cleaner cap table and filings if you later bring in investors

> [!IMPORTANT] AGM exemption
> OPCs are not required to hold an AGM, but financial statements, audit, and prescribed filings must still be completed on time.

## Penalties and risks

| Non-compliance | Penalty |
| --- | --- |
| AOC-4 delay | Rs. 100 per day |
| MGT-7A delay | Rs. 100 per day |
| Director KYC default | Rs. 5,000 + DIN deactivation |
| Audit default | Rs. 25,000 to Rs. 5 lakh |
| ITR non-filing | Rs. 1,000 to Rs. 10,000 + interest |
| Continued default | Director disqualification, company strike off |

## Documents required

| Category | Examples |
| --- | --- |
| Incorporation | Certificate of Incorporation, MOA, AOA |
| Tax | PAN, TAN, previous ITR |
| Operations | Bank statements, invoices, purchase records, salary records |
| GST / TDS | Returns, challans if applicable |
| Other | DSC of director, nominee details, financial statements |

## Process and timeline

1. Close books and complete statutory audit (always required for OPC).
2. No AGM: director signs off financials per law.
3. File AOC-4 within 180 days from the end of the financial year (illustrative deadline around late September for March year-end).
4. File MGT-7A within 60 days thereafter.
5. File ITR-6 by October 31; align tax audit if applicable.
6. Keep board meetings and GST or TDS cycles on track.

## Government fees (indicative)

| Form | Fee range |
| --- | --- |
| AOC-4 | Rs. 200 to Rs. 600 (based on capital) |
| MGT-7A | Rs. 200 to Rs. 600 |
| DIR-3 KYC | Nil if on time |
| ADT-1 | Rs. 200 to Rs. 600 |
| Late filing | Rs. 100 per day |

## Compliance calendar (typical)

| When | Activity |
| --- | --- |
| April to May | Book closure, audit preparation |
| June to August | Statutory audit |
| ~September 27 | AOC-4 filing (180 days from March FY end) |
| September 30 | DIR-3 KYC |
| October 31 | ITR-6 |
| ~November 28 | MGT-7A (60 days from window start) |
| Quarterly | TDS, board meeting compliance |

## Frequently asked questions

### Q1. Is an AGM required for an OPC?

No, OPCs are specifically exempted from holding Annual General Meetings. However, financial statements must still be prepared and filed.

### Q2. What is MGT-7A and how is it different from MGT-7?

MGT-7A is an abridged annual return for OPCs and small companies, with less information than MGT-7.

### Q3. Can an OPC skip audit if it has no revenue?

No, statutory audit is mandatory for every OPC regardless of turnover or business activity, including zero-revenue years.

### Q4. When must an OPC convert to a private limited company?

An OPC must convert when paid-up capital exceeds Rs. 50 lakh or average annual turnover exceeds Rs. 2 crore for three consecutive years (verify current law and notifications at filing time).

### Q5. How many board meetings must an OPC conduct?

An OPC with more than one director must hold at least one board meeting in each half of the calendar year, with a minimum gap of 90 days between the two meetings.

services.name: Auditor Appointment (Form ADT-1 Filing)
services.shortDescription: Expert assistance for Auditor Appointment (Form ADT-1 Filing) — documentation, filings and follow-ups.
services.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

Auditor Appointment Form ADT-1 Filing refers to the mandatory statutory filing submitted by companies to inform the government about the appointment of an auditor after approval in the Annual General Meeting (AGM) or Board Meeting, as applicable. This filing is governed under the provisions of the Companies Act, 2013 and regulated by the Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA). As per compliance requirements, every company registered in India must appoint an auditor to audit its financial statements and must file Form ADT-1 to officially notify the Registrar of Companies (ROC) about such appointment. The filing ensures:

- Transparency in financial reporting
- Legal validation of auditor appointment
- Corporate governance compliance
- Proper audit structure for financial year

## 2) Importance / Benefits

### Mandatory Legal Compliance

Ensures adherence to statutory audit requirements.

### Official Recognition of Auditor

Auditor appointment is formally recorded with MCA.

### Strengthens Corporate Governance

Enhances financial transparency and accountability.

### Prevents Penalties and Non-Compliance Issues

Avoids ROC penalties for non-filing.

### Supports Financial Credibility

Audited financials improve trust with banks and investors.

### Smooth Business Operations

Ensures uninterrupted audit compliance cycle.

## 3) Applicability / Who Must File

Form ADT-1 filing is applicable to:

- Private Limited Companies
- Public Limited Companies
- One Person Companies (if audit applicable)
- Section 8 Companies
- Subsidiaries and holding companies

### Regulatory Requirement

- Auditor must be appointed within 30 days of incorporation or AGM
- Filing must be done with ROC via MCA portal

## 4) Legal Framework

This compliance is governed under:

- Companies Act, 2013
- Audit appointment provisions under Section 139
- Filing requirement under MCA rules

## 5) Key Features of ADT-1 Filing

### Auditor Appointment Reporting

Official intimation of appointed auditor to ROC.

### Time-Bound Compliance

Must be filed within prescribed statutory timeline.

### Digital Filing System

Filed electronically via MCA portal.

### Linked to Financial Audit Cycle

Ensures audit validity for financial year.

### Mandatory for Most Companies

Required irrespective of company size.

## 6) Documents & Information Required

### Basic Company Details

- Company CIN (Corporate Identification Number)
- PAN of company
- Registered office address

### Auditor Details

- Name and membership number of auditor
- Firm registration details (if applicable)
- Auditor consent letter

### Appointment Proof

- Board resolution or AGM resolution
- Appointment letter of auditor

### Additional Requirements

- Digital Signature Certificate (DSC) of director
- Details of financial year of appointment

## 7) Process / Procedure (Filing Workflow)

1. Auditor Appointment Approval — Auditor is appointed in AGM or Board Meeting.
2. Obtain Auditor Consent — Written consent from auditor is collected.
3. Prepare Form ADT-1 — Details of company and auditor are filled.
4. Attach Required Documents — Resolution, consent letter, and auditor details attached.
5. DSC Verification & Submission — Form signed using Digital Signature Certificate.
6. Filing with MCA — Form submitted through MCA portal.
7. ROC Approval & Acknowledgement — Filing is recorded and acknowledged by ROC.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### Government Filing Fees

- ₹300 – ₹600 (approx., depends on company share capital)

### Additional Charges

- Late filing penalty (if delayed): ₹100 per day per form (subject to MCA rules)

### Professional Fees

- ₹1,000 – ₹5,000+ depending on complexity and documentation support

### Important Notes

- Timely filing avoids penalties
- Delay may affect audit validity and compliance status

## 9) Penalty & Non-Compliance Impact

- Late filing attracts continuous penalty per day
- Non-filing may lead to compliance defaults in MCA records
- May impact company's ability to raise funding or loans
- Auditor appointment may be considered invalid without filing

## Conclusion

Auditor Appointment Form ADT-1 Filing is a critical statutory compliance under the Companies Act, 2013 that formally records the appointment of a company auditor with the Ministry of Corporate Affairs. While it is a routine annual compliance, timely and accurate filing ensures financial transparency, regulatory compliance, and smooth audit processes. For every registered company in India, ADT-1 filing is an essential part of maintaining a legally compliant corporate structure.
