So what we commonly call FY 2025-26 is the "Previous Year" in the law's language, and AY 2026-27 is when it is assessed.

This is purely a legal convention. For practical purposes:
**FY = Previous Year = the year you earn. AY = the year you file and get assessed.**

## Practical Example: How AY and FY Work Together

Kavya joined a software company in May 2025. Over the course of the year (May 2025 to March 2026), she earns Rs. 9 lakh in salary.

- **FY 2025-26:** This is when Kavya earned the Rs. 9 lakh.
- **AY 2026-27:** This is when she files her ITR (by July 31, 2026) and pays any remaining tax.

When Kavya logs into the ITR portal to file, she selects **AY 2026-27**. The system automatically knows she is reporting income earned in FY 2025-26.

## Where AY vs. FY Appears in Real Life

| Document / Process | Which Year Is Referenced |
| --- | --- |
| Form 16 issued by employer | Shows the FY for which salary is paid |
| ITR filing on e-filing portal | Always select the AY (e.g., AY 2026-27) |
| Income tax notice from department | Refers to the AY under scrutiny |
| Advance tax payment challan | Select the AY in which assessment will happen |
| TDS certificates (Form 16A) | Show FY of deduction |
| Tax audit report (Form 3CD) | References the previous year (FY) |
| Intimation under Section 143(1) | Refers to AY |
| Refund status on portal | Shows AY for which refund is being processed |

## Common Mistakes Caused by AY-FY Confusion

### Mistake 1: Selecting the Wrong AY While Filing

Filing under AY 2025-26 when you should be filing for AY 2026-27 means your return is filed for the wrong period. The department may treat this as a return filed for income that was already assessed, leading to mismatches and notices.

### Mistake 2: Paying Advance Tax Under Wrong AY

If you fill the advance tax challan with the wrong AY, your payment may not get credited correctly against your outstanding liability.

### Mistake 3: Confusing Budget Year with Tax Filing Year

The Union Budget for FY 2025-26 is presented in February 2025 and announces tax slabs effective from April 1, 2025. These slabs apply to income earned in FY 2025-26 and are used when filing ITR in **AY 2026-27**.

## Quick Reference Table

| If You Are Filing For Income Earned In... | Select This AY |
| --- | --- |
| April 2022 to March 2023 | AY 2023-24 |
| April 2023 to March 2024 | AY 2024-25 |
| April 2024 to March 2025 | AY 2025-26 |
| April 2025 to March 2026 | AY 2026-27 |
| April 2026 to March 2027 | AY 2027-28 |

> [!IMPORTANT] Filing deadlines and notice windows
> The last date to file an ITR for a given AY is **July 31** (for individuals without audit). Belated returns can be filed by **December 31** of the same AY, with a late fee under Section 234F. Tax notices can be issued for up to **3 years** from the end of the relevant AY in routine cases, and up to **10 years** in cases involving income over Rs. 50 lakh escaped from assessment.

## FAQs

### Q1. My employer issued Form 16 for FY 2025-26. Which AY should I select when filing my ITR?

Select **AY 2026-27**. Form 16 always covers the financial year in which salary was paid. The ITR is filed in the immediately following year, which is the Assessment Year.

### Q2. I received a tax notice mentioning AY 2023-24. Which period's income does it relate to?

A notice for AY 2023-24 relates to income you earned during **FY 2022-23** (April 1, 2022 to March 31, 2023). The department is assessing your tax liability for that earning period.

### Q3. Can two different financial years overlap for a company with a different accounting year?

For income tax purposes in India, the financial year is fixed: **April 1 to March 31**. This applies to all individual taxpayers, HUFs, firms, and most companies. Companies incorporated outside India may have different accounting years, but they must still report income to the Indian tax department on an April-March basis.

### Q4. Which AY do I enter on the advance tax challan?

When paying advance tax during FY 2025-26 (in June, September, December 2025, and March 2026), enter **AY 2026-27** on the challan. The advance tax is being paid against the liability that will be finally assessed in AY 2026-27.

### Q5. I want to revise my ITR filed for AY 2025-26. Until when can I do this?

A revised return for AY 2025-26 can be filed up to **December 31, 2025** (or before the assessment is completed, whichever is earlier). After that date, no further revision is permitted for that AY.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Form 67 & Claim Of Foreign Tax Credit
articles.excerpt: Global income reporting is a standard requirement for residents under the Income Tax Act,
articles.content:
## Overview

Global income reporting is a standard requirement for residents under the Income Tax Act,

1. **When income is earned outside India and taxed in a foreign jurisdiction, the same** — income may also become taxable in India. To prevent double taxation, the law provides relief

in the form of Foreign Tax Credit (FTC). Form 67 plays a central role in this mechanism. It is the prescribed form through which a taxpayer declares foreign income and taxes paid abroad to claim credit in India. Without filing Form 67, the credit may not be allowed, even if taxes have already been paid overseas. Explanation / Core Concept Foreign Tax Credit allows taxpayers to reduce their Indian tax liability by the amount of tax already paid in another country on the same income. This relief is governed by:

- Double Taxation Avoidance Agreements (DTAA), where applicable
- Unilateral relief provisions where no DTAA exists

### Form 67 is the formal declaration that captures

- Nature and source of foreign income
- Country where income was earned
- Amount of tax paid outside India
- Supporting evidence for such tax payment

### The credit is restricted to the lower of

- Tax payable in India on such income, or
- Tax paid in the foreign country Key Rules / Applicability
- Applicable to resident taxpayers earning foreign income
- Mandatory filing of Form 67 before filing the Income Tax Return (ITR)
- Credit is allowed only if tax has been actually paid or deducted abroad
- FTC is computed country-wise and source-wise
- Conversion of foreign tax into INR must be done using prescribed exchange rates
- Relief is governed by DTAA provisions, if available, otherwise Section 91 applies

Failure to comply with procedural requirements may result in denial of credit.

## Features

- Prevents double taxation on the same income
- Applicable across multiple income types (salary, dividends, capital gains, etc.)
- Requires detailed country-wise reporting
- Linked directly with ITR filing
- Supported by documentary evidence
- Credit cannot exceed Indian tax liability on that income Documents Required

To file Form 67 and claim FTC, the following documents are typically required:

- Tax payment proof from foreign country
- Foreign tax withholding certificates
- Statement of foreign income
- Bank statements reflecting income received
- Tax return filed in foreign jurisdiction (if applicable)
- DTAA reference (if treaty benefits are claimed)

Proper documentation is critical, as the claim is subject to verification.

## Process / Procedure

The process of claiming Foreign Tax Credit through Form 67 involves structured reporting and timely compliance:

1. **Compute foreign income and tax paid** — Identify income earned outside India and the corresponding tax paid.
2. **Convert amounts into INR** — Use prescribed telegraphic transfer buying rates as per RBI guidelines.
3. **Prepare Form 67 details** — Include country-wise breakdown of income and taxes.
4. **Upload Form 67 on the income tax portal** — The form must be filed online before submitting the ITR.
5. **File Income Tax Return** — Reflect FTC in the relevant schedule of the ITR.
6. **Verification and processing** — The Income Tax Department validates the claim during return processing. Timeliness is critical—delayed submission of Form 67 can impact eligibility.

## Examples

Example 1: Salary Income Abroad An individual works for a few months in the UK and pays tax there. The same income is taxable in India. By filing Form 67, the individual can claim credit for the UK taxes against Indian liability.

Example 2: Dividend from Foreign Company A taxpayer receives dividends from a US-listed company, where tax is withheld at source. This withholding can be claimed as FTC in India through Form 67.

Example 3: Freelance Income from Overseas Clients Income earned from international clients and taxed abroad can be offset against Indian taxes, provided proper disclosure is made.

## Common Mistakes

- Not filing Form 67 before ITR submission

This is one of the most frequent reasons for FTC rejection.

- Incorrect currency conversion

Using unofficial or inconsistent exchange rates leads to mismatches.

- Claiming credit without proof of tax payment Documentary evidence is mandatory.
- Ignoring DTAA provisions

Misapplication of treaty benefits can result in incorrect claims.

- Clubbing income across countries

FTC must be computed separately for each country and income source.

## Conclusion

Form 67 is not merely a reporting requirement—it is a decisive compliance step for taxpayers with global income. Proper filing ensures that taxes paid abroad are appropriately recognized in India, avoiding unnecessary tax burden. In an increasingly global income environment, accuracy, documentation, and timing determine whether Foreign Tax Credit is successfully claimed. Aligning disclosures with the provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961 ensures both compliance and tax efficiency.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Claiming Relief under Section 89(1) on Salary Arrears
articles.excerpt: Salary structures do not always follow a smooth, year-wise pattern. In many cases, employees receive arrears or advance salary, often due to delayed revisions, bonuses, or retrospective pay adjustments. Since such income is taxed in the…
articles.content:
## Overview

Salary structures do not always follow a smooth, year-wise pattern. In many cases, employees receive arrears or advance salary, often due to delayed revisions, bonuses, or retrospective pay adjustments. Since such income is taxed in the year of receipt, it can push the taxpayer into a higher tax bracket, resulting in a disproportionate tax burden. To address this, relief under Section 89(1) of the Income Tax Act, 1961 allows taxpayers to neutralize the additional tax impact caused by such timing differences. Explanation / Core Concept Section 89(1) provides a mechanism to recalculate tax liability as if the arrears or advance income had been taxed in the year(s) to which it actually pertains. Instead of taxing the entire amount in the year of receipt, the law permits:

- Spreading the income across relevant past years
- Comparing tax liability under both scenarios
- Claiming the difference as relief

This ensures that taxpayers are not penalized purely due to delayed payment.

## Key Rules / Applicability

- Applicable to salary arrears, advance salary, gratuity, compensation, or commuted pension
- Relief is available only when income is taxed in a different year than when it was earned
- Mandatory filing of Form 10E before claiming relief
- Relief must be computed using prescribed methodology
- Applicable only to individual taxpayers

If Form 10E is not filed, the claim may be disallowed even if calculations are correct.

## Features

- Reduces excess tax due to income bunching
- Applies to both arrears and advance salary
- Requires year-wise recomputation of tax
- Linked directly with Form 10E submission
- Prevents artificial increase in tax slab impact Documents Required
- Salary slips showing arrears or advance payment
- Employer-issued arrear statements
- Previous years’ income details
- Tax computation records for relevant years
- Form 16 for current and past years
- Form 10E (mandatory for claim)

Accurate historical data is essential for correct computation.

## Process / Procedure

1. **Identify arrears or advance income received** — Determine the portion of income that relates to previous or future years.
2. **Segregate income year-wise** — Allocate the arrears to the respective financial years to which they belong.
3. **Recompute tax for each relevant year** — Calculate tax as if the income had been received in those years.
4. **Compute tax for current year including arrears** — Compare it with recomputed tax values.
5. **Calculate relief amount** — Relief equals excess tax paid due to inclusion of arrears in the current year.
6. **File Form 10E online** — Submit details before filing the Income Tax Return.
7. **Claim relief in ITR** — Reflect the computed relief under the appropriate section. Examples

Example 1: Salary Revision Arrears An employee receives ₹2,00,000 as arrears in FY 2025–26 for salary pertaining to FY 2023–24 and FY 2024–25. Instead of taxing the full amount in one year, the income is distributed across the respective years, reducing the overall tax burden.

Example 2: Advance Salary If a portion of salary for next year is paid in advance, it may push the taxpayer into a higher slab in the current year. Section 89(1) allows adjustment by recalculating tax across both years.

## Common Mistakes

- Not filing Form 10E

This is the most common reason for denial of relief.

- Incorrect year-wise allocation

Misclassification of arrears across years leads to inaccurate calculations.

- Using approximate tax figures

Precise recomputation based on applicable slab rates of each year is required.

- Ignoring impact of deductions and exemptions

Past year tax computations must reflect actual deductions applicable then.

- Claiming relief without proper documentation

Lack of supporting records may lead to scrutiny issues.

## Conclusion

Section 89(1) provides a structured solution to a common taxation issue arising from irregular salary payments. By allowing tax recalculation across relevant years, it ensures fairness and prevents undue tax liability caused by timing mismatches. However, the benefit is strictly procedural. Proper computation, accurate historical data, and timely filing of Form 10E are essential to successfully claim relief under the provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Home Loan Tax Benefits: Every Deduction You Are Entitled To
articles.excerpt: Home loans are not just financing tools—they are among the most structured avenues for tax optimization under the Income Tax Act, 1961. The tax treatment of home loans is layered across principal repayment, interest payments, and…
articles.content:
Home loans are not just financing tools—they are among the most structured avenues for tax optimization under the Income Tax Act, 1961. The tax treatment of home loans is layered across principal repayment, interest payments, and additional incentives for first-time buyers. When used strategically, these provisions can significantly reduce overall tax liability.

## Why It Matters

For salaried individuals and professionals, housing loans often represent the largest long-term financial commitment. The tax benefits attached to such loans are designed to:

- Encourage home ownership
- Reduce the effective cost of borrowing
- Provide annual tax relief across the loan tenure

However, the benefits depend on usage, ownership structure, and compliance with specific conditions.

## What Qualifies for Tax Benefits

Tax deductions on a home loan are available across three key components:

## Interest on Home Loan

Interest paid on a housing loan qualifies for deduction under Section 24(b):

- Up to ₹2,00,000 per year for self-occupied property
- No upper limit for let-out property (subject to overall loss set-off rules)
- Pre-construction interest is allowed in five equal installments

## Principal Repayment

### Principal repayment qualifies under Section 80C

- Maximum deduction up to ₹1,50,000
- Includes stamp duty and registration charges (in the year of purchase)
- Property should not be sold within 5 years of possession

## Additional Deduction for First-Time Buyers

### Under Section 80EE and 80EEA

- Additional deduction of ₹50,000 (80EE) or ₹1,50,000 (80EEA), subject to conditions
- Applicable for first-time home buyers
- Based on property value and loan sanction timelines How the Benefit Works in Practice

The tax savings depend on how the property is classified: Self-Occupied Property

- Interest deduction capped at ₹2 lakh
- No rental income considered
- Ideal for primary residence Let-Out Property
- Entire interest is deductible
- Rental income is taxable
- Loss from house property can be set off up to ₹2 lakh annually Under-Construction Property
- No deduction during construction period
- Interest accumulated is claimed post-completion in installments Step-by-Step Approach to Claim Benefits

1. **Obtain interest certificate from lender** — This provides a breakup of principal and interest paid during the year.
2. **Determine property status** — Identify whether the property is self-occupied or rented.
3. **Calculate eligible deductions** — Apply limits under Section 24(b), 80C, and other applicable provisions.
4. **Include deductions while filing ITR** — Report under “Income from House Property” and Chapter VI-A deductions.

## Retain supporting documents

Loan agreement, possession certificate, and payment proofs may be required.

## Important Conditions

- Loan must be taken from a recognized financial institution or bank
- Construction must be completed within 5 years to claim full interest benefit
- Ownership and loan repayment must be in the same taxpayer’s name
- For joint loans, deduction is available proportionately Illustrative Example

### An individual takes a home loan and pays

- Interest: ₹2,20,000
- Principal: ₹1,50,000 Tax treatment:
- ₹2,00,000 allowed under interest deduction
- ₹1,50,000 allowed under Section 80C Total deduction: ₹3,50,000

If eligible under Section 80EEA, an additional ₹1,50,000 may further reduce taxable income. Mistakes to Avoid

- Claiming deduction before possession

Interest cannot be claimed until construction is completed.

- Ignoring co-ownership structure

Benefits must be claimed in proportion to ownership and repayment.

- Exceeding statutory limits

Claiming beyond allowed caps leads to adjustments during processing.

- Incorrect classification of property

Misreporting as self-occupied or let-out affects deduction eligibility.

- Overlooking pre-construction interest rules

These must be spread over five years, not claimed in one go.

## Conclusion

Home loan tax benefits, when applied correctly, offer a structured way to reduce taxable income while building a long-term asset. The interplay between interest deductions, principal repayment, and additional incentives makes it one of the most comprehensive tax-saving avenues available. However, these benefits are conditional and documentation-driven. Accurate classification, adherence to limits, and proper reporting under the Income Tax Act, 1961 are essential to ensure that the tax advantages translate into actual financial savings.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Form 26Q: TDS Return Filing for Non Salary Deductions
articles.excerpt: Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) is a core compliance mechanism under the Income Tax Act, 1961, ensuring tax collection at the point of income generation. While salary-related TDS is reported separately, all non-salary payments subject to…
articles.content:
## Overview

Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) is a core compliance mechanism under the Income Tax Act, 1961, ensuring tax collection at the point of income generation. While salary-related TDS is reported separately, all non-salary payments subject to TDS—such as contractor payments, professional fees, rent, and interest—are reported through Form 26Q. Form 26Q is a quarterly TDS return that must be filed by deductors to report details of tax deducted on payments made to residents other than salary. It is a critical compliance document, linking deductions with the recipient’s tax records. Explanation / Core Concept Form 26Q captures:

- Details of payments made to residents
- Amount of TDS deducted and deposited
- PAN of deductees
- Nature of payment and applicable section

It applies to a wide range of transactions, including payments under Sections like 194C, 194J, 194H, and 194I. The return ensures that the deducted tax is properly credited to the deductee’s account and reflected in their Form 26AS.

## Key Rules / Applicability

- Applicable to all deductors (individuals, firms, companies, etc.) responsible for non-salary TDS
- Covers payments made to resident individuals or entities
- Must be filed quarterly
- Mandatory quoting of valid PAN of deductees
- TDS must be deposited before filing the return
- Filing is done electronically through prescribed utilities

Failure to file or incorrect filing may lead to penalties and disallowance of expenses.

## Features

- Covers multiple types of non-salary payments
- Filed every quarter of the Financial Year
- Includes deductee-wise transaction details
- Linked with PAN for tax credit tracking
- Enables reconciliation with Form 26AS
- Mandatory for compliance even if no tax is payable after adjustments Documents Required
- TAN (Tax Deduction and Collection Account Number)
- PAN details of deductees
- TDS challan details (Challan Identification Number – CIN)
- Payment records and invoices
- TDS calculation sheets
- Return preparation utility (RPU) / software

Accurate data collection is essential to avoid mismatches and notices.

## Process / Procedure

1. **Deduct TDS at applicable rates** — At the time of payment or credit, whichever is earlier.
2. **Deposit TDS with the government** — Within prescribed timelines using challan ITNS 281.
3. **Prepare Form 26Q** — Use the official utility or authorized software to compile transaction details.
4. **Validate return using File Validation Utility (FVU)** — Ensure format and data consistency.
5. **Upload return on TRACES / income tax portal** — Submit digitally using TAN credentials.
6. **Generate acknowledgment** — Retain for records and future reference.

## Issue TDS certificates (Form 16A)

Provide deductees with proof of tax deduction. Due Dates for Filing

- Q1 (Apr–Jun): 31 July
- Q2 (Jul–Sep): 31 October
- Q3 (Oct–Dec): 31 January
- Q4 (Jan–Mar): 31 May

Timely filing ensures smooth credit flow to deductees and avoids penalties.

## Examples

Example 1: Contractor Payment A company pays ₹5,00,000 to a contractor and deducts TDS under Section 194C. This transaction is reported in Form 26Q for the relevant quarter.

Example 2: Professional Fees A consultant receives ₹1,00,000 with TDS deducted under Section 194J. The deductor reports this in Form 26Q, ensuring the consultant can claim credit.

Example 3: Rent Payment TDS deducted on office rent under Section 194I is included in quarterly Form 26Q filing.

## Common Mistakes

- Incorrect PAN details

Leads to higher TDS rates and credit mismatches.

- Mismatch between challan and return data Causes rejection or notices.
- Late filing of returns Attracts fees under Section 234E.
- Failure to issue TDS certificates

Impacts deductee’s ability to claim credit.

- Incorrect section reporting

Misclassification of payment type leads to compliance issues.

## Conclusion

Form 26Q is a central component of TDS compliance for non-salary payments. It ensures transparency, accurate credit allocation, and alignment between deductors and deductees within the tax system. Given its detailed reporting requirements, accuracy and timeliness are critical. Proper reconciliation of payments, challans, and deductee data helps maintain compliance under the Income Tax Act, 1961 and avoids downstream complications in tax processing.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Form 24Q- TDS Return on Salary Payment
articles.excerpt: Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) on salary is a primary compliance obligation for employers under the Income Tax Act, 1961. Unlike non-salary payments, salary-related deductions follow a structured annual computation, considering exemptions,…
articles.content:
## Overview

Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) on salary is a primary compliance obligation for employers under the Income Tax Act, 1961. Unlike non-salary payments, salary-related deductions follow a structured annual computation, considering exemptions, deductions, and applicable tax regimes. Form 24Q is the prescribed quarterly TDS return used by employers to report salary payments and tax deducted from employees. It ensures that employee-wise tax deductions are accurately recorded and reflected in their tax statements. Explanation / Core Concept Form 24Q captures detailed information about:

- Salary paid to employees
- Tax deducted based on estimated annual income
- Allowances, exemptions, and deductions claimed
- PAN of employees and tax computation details

The return is divided into multiple annexures, with the final quarter requiring comprehensive disclosure of annual salary details. It serves as the basis for generating Form 16, which employees use to file their Income Tax Returns.

## Key Rules / Applicability

- Applicable to all employers deducting TDS on salary
- Covers payments made to resident employees
- Filed quarterly, with detailed annual data in the last quarter
- TDS must be calculated based on estimated annual income
- Employers must consider deductions, exemptions, and declarations submitted by employees
- Filing is mandatory even if no tax is deducted in a particular month, provided salary is paid

Non-compliance may result in penalties and disallowance of expenses.

## Features

- Dedicated return for salary-based TDS
- Includes employee-wise income and tax details
- Accounts for deductions under Chapter VI-A
- Linked with Form 16 issuance
- Requires annual consolidation in Q4
- Enables accurate tax credit in employee records Documents Required
- TAN of employer
- PAN of employees
- Salary structure and payroll records
- Investment declarations and proofs
- TDS challan details (CIN)
- Previous employment details (if applicable)
- Return preparation utility (RPU)

Maintaining updated payroll data is critical for accurate reporting.

## Process / Procedure

1. **Estimate annual taxable salary** — Include basic salary, allowances, perquisites, and other income declarations.
2. **Consider exemptions and deductions** — Apply eligible exemptions (HRA, LTA) and deductions (Section 80C, 80D, etc.).
3. **Compute TDS liability** — Calculate tax based on applicable slab rates and chosen tax regime.
4. **Deduct TDS monthly** — Spread tax liability evenly across remaining months.
5. **Deposit TDS with the government** — Within prescribed timelines using challan ITNS 281.
6. **Prepare Form 24Q** — Compile employee-wise and challan-wise details.
7. **Validate and file return** — Use File Validation Utility (FVU) and upload on the portal.

## Issue Form 16

After filing Q4 return, provide employees with TDS certificates. Due Dates for Filing

- Q1 (Apr–Jun): 31 July
- Q2 (Jul–Sep): 31 October
- Q3 (Oct–Dec): 31 January
- Q4 (Jan–Mar): 31 May

The fourth quarter return is more detailed, as it includes complete annual salary information.

## Examples

Example 1: Regular Salary Deduction An employee earns ₹10,00,000 annually. After deductions, taxable income is ₹7,50,000. The employer calculates total tax liability and deducts TDS monthly, reporting it in Form 24Q.

Example 2: Employee with Previous Employment If an employee joins mid-year, the employer includes previous salary details (based on Form 12B) to compute total tax liability and reflect it in Form 24Q.

Example 3: Investment Declaration Impact An employee submits proof of investments under Section 80C. The employer adjusts TDS accordingly and reports revised figures in quarterly filings.

## Common Mistakes

- Incorrect estimation of annual income

Leads to excess or short deduction of TDS.

- Ignoring employee declarations

Failure to consider deductions results in higher tax deduction.

- Mismatch between challan and return data Causes processing issues and notices.
- Late filing of returns Attracts fees under Section 234E.
- Errors in PAN details

Leads to credit mismatches in employee records.

## Conclusion

Form 24Q is central to employer compliance in relation to salary payments. It ensures that tax deductions are accurately computed, reported, and credited to employees. Given its linkage with Form 16 and employee tax filings, precision in payroll data, timely filing, and proper documentation are essential. Adherence to requirements under the Income Tax Act, 1961 ensures smooth compliance and avoids downstream discrepancies in tax processing.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 194C - Guide on TDS on Payment to Contractor
articles.excerpt: Payments to contractors and subcontractors are a routine part of business operations, particularly in sectors such as construction, logistics, manufacturing, and services. To ensure tax collection at the source of such payments, Section…
articles.content:
## Overview

Payments to contractors and subcontractors are a routine part of business operations, particularly in sectors such as construction, logistics, manufacturing, and services. To ensure tax collection at the source of such payments, Section 194C of the Income Tax Act, 1961 mandates deduction of Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) on specified contractual payments. This provision applies to a wide range of contractual arrangements, making it one of the most commonly invoked TDS sections in practice. Explanation / Core Concept Section 194C requires any specified person making payment to a resident contractor for carrying out any work (including supply of labor) to deduct TDS at prescribed rates. The term “work” is broadly defined and includes:

- Advertising
- Broadcasting and telecasting
- Carriage of goods or passengers
- Catering
- Manufacturing or supplying products using material provided by the customer

The deduction is triggered at the time of credit or payment, whichever is earlier.

## Key Rules / Applicability

- Applicable to payments made to resident contractors or subcontractors
- Deductor includes individuals, firms, companies, and other entities, subject to turnover thresholds for individuals/HUFs
- TDS is required when:

○ Single payment exceeds ₹30,000, or ○ Aggregate payments exceed ₹1,00,000 in a financial year

- TDS Rates:

○ 1% for payments to individual/HUF contractors ○ 2% for others

- No TDS if payment does not exceed prescribed thresholds Features
- Covers a wide range of contractual activities
- Applies to both contractors and subcontractors
- Threshold-based applicability
- Different rates based on type of payee
- Deduction at earlier of payment or credit
- Integrated with quarterly TDS return filing (Form 26Q) Documents Required
- PAN of contractor
- Contract agreement or work order
- Invoice details
- Payment records
- TDS challan details (CIN)
- TAN of deductor

Proper documentation supports accurate classification and compliance.

## Process / Procedure

1. **Identify nature of payment** — Confirm whether the payment qualifies as “work” under Section 194C.
2. **Check threshold limits** — Determine if the payment exceeds ₹30,000 (single) or ₹1,00,000 (aggregate).
3. **Determine applicable TDS rate** — Based on the status of the contractor.
4. **Deduct TDS at the time of payment or credit** — Whichever occurs earlier.
5. **Deposit TDS with the government** — Within prescribed timelines using challan ITNS 281.
6. **Report in quarterly TDS return (Form 26Q)** — Include deductee and challan details.
7. **Issue TDS certificate (Form 16A)** — Provide proof of deduction to the contractor. Examples

Example 1: Transport Contract A company pays ₹2,50,000 to a transporter for goods carriage. Since the amount exceeds threshold limits, TDS is deducted at applicable rates and reported.

Example 2: Advertising Agency Payment Payment made to an advertising agency for campaign services falls under “work” and attracts TDS under Section 194C.

Example 3: Subcontracting Arrangement A contractor outsources part of the work to another party. Payments to the subcontractor are also subject to TDS under this section.

## Common Mistakes

- Incorrect classification of services

Confusing professional services (Section 194J) with contractual work leads to wrong TDS application.

- Ignoring aggregate threshold

Multiple small payments crossing ₹1,00,000 collectively still attract TDS.

- Failure to deduct at correct time

Deduction must be at earlier of credit or payment.

- Missing PAN details

Leads to higher TDS rates and compliance issues.

- Non-reporting in TDS returns

Causes mismatch in deductee records and potential notices.

## Conclusion

Section 194C plays a critical role in ensuring tax compliance on contractual payments across industries. Its broad scope and frequent applicability make it essential for businesses to correctly identify qualifying transactions and apply TDS provisions accurately. Timely deduction, correct rate application, and proper reporting under the Income Tax Act, 1961 help avoid penalties and ensure seamless credit flow to contractors.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 194J: TDS on Professional Fees and Technical Services
articles.excerpt: Payments for professional expertise and technical services form a significant part of business expenditure. To ensure tax collection at source on such payments, Section 194J of the Income Tax Act, 1961 mandates deduction of Tax Deducted…
articles.content:
## Overview

Payments for professional expertise and technical services form a significant part of business expenditure. To ensure tax collection at source on such payments, Section 194J of the Income Tax Act, 1961 mandates deduction of Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) on specified categories of services. This provision primarily targets payments made to professionals such as consultants, doctors, lawyers, engineers, and technical experts, where income is earned through specialized knowledge or skill. Explanation / Core Concept Section 194J applies to payments made to resident professionals or service providers for:

- Professional services
- Technical services
- Royalty
- Non-compete fees
- Director’s remuneration (excluding salary)

The section distinguishes between professional services (requiring specialized qualifications) and technical services (involving managerial, technical, or consultancy functions). TDS is required to be deducted at the time of credit or payment, whichever is earlier.

## Key Rules / Applicability

- Applicable to specified persons making payments to residents
- Individuals and HUFs are required to deduct TDS only if they exceed prescribed turnover limits
- Threshold limit:

○ ₹30,000 per financial year per category (professional, technical, royalty, etc.)

- TDS Rates:

○ 10% for professional services, royalty, and non-compete fees ○ 2% for technical services (in specified cases)

- No TDS if payment does not exceed threshold limits

The classification of payment determines the applicable rate and compliance requirement.

## Features

- Covers a wide range of professional and technical services
- Threshold-based applicability per category
- Different rates based on nature of service
- Includes director remuneration (non-salary)
- Deduction at earlier of payment or credit
- Linked with Form 26Q for reporting Documents Required
- PAN of service provider
- Service agreement or contract
- Invoice raised by professional
- Payment records
- TDS challan details (CIN)
- TAN of deductor

Maintaining clear agreements helps in correct classification of services.

## Process / Procedure

1. **Identify nature of service** — Determine whether the payment qualifies as professional or technical service.
2. **Check threshold limit** — Confirm whether aggregate payments exceed ₹30,000 in the financial year.
3. **Apply correct TDS rate** — Based on classification of service.
4. **Deduct TDS at time of credit or payment** — Whichever occurs earlier.
5. **Deposit TDS with the government** — Within prescribed timelines.
6. **File TDS return (Form 26Q)** — Report deductee and challan details quarterly.
7. **Issue TDS certificate (Form 16A)** — Provide proof of deduction to the recipient. Examples

Example 1: Legal Fees A company pays ₹1,00,000 to a lawyer. Since the amount exceeds ₹30,000, TDS is deducted at 10% under professional services.

Example 2: Technical Consultancy Payment made to an IT consultant for system implementation may attract TDS at 2% or 10%, depending on classification.

Example 3: Director’s Fees Sitting fees paid to a director (not salary) are subject to TDS under Section 194J.

## Common Mistakes

- Incorrect classification of services

Confusing technical services with contractual work (Section 194C) leads to incorrect TDS rates.

- Ignoring threshold limits per category

Each category must be evaluated separately.

- Failure to deduct at correct time

Deduction must be at earlier of credit or payment.

- Not considering PAN availability

Absence of PAN results in higher TDS rates.

- Incorrect rate application

Misinterpreting 2% vs 10% applicability causes compliance issues.

## Conclusion

Section 194J governs TDS on payments involving professional expertise and technical services, making it a key compliance area for businesses and organizations. Its correct application depends heavily on proper classification and adherence to thresholds and rates. Accurate deduction, timely deposit, and correct reporting under the Income Tax Act, 1961 ensure smooth compliance and prevent disputes or penalties. Dividend Income? Tax on Dividend Income: Do I Need to Pay Tax on Dividend Income?

## Overview

Dividend income has undergone a significant shift in its tax treatment in India. Earlier, dividends were largely tax-free in the hands of investors, with companies paying Dividend Distribution Tax (DDT). However, this system has been replaced, and dividends are now taxable in the hands of shareholders under the Income Tax Act, 1961. This change has made dividend taxation more aligned with individual tax slabs, requiring careful reporting and planning. Explanation / Core Concept Dividend refers to the portion of profits distributed by a company to its shareholders. Under current provisions:

- Dividends are taxed as “Income from Other Sources”
- Tax is levied at the applicable slab rate of the taxpayer
- No separate concessional rate applies for most taxpayers

This means that individuals in higher tax brackets may face significantly higher tax liability on dividend income compared to the earlier regime.

## Key Rules / Applicability

- Applicable to all taxpayers receiving dividend income
- Covers dividends from:

○ Domestic companies ○ Foreign companies ○ Mutual funds

- Taxable in the year of receipt or declaration, depending on accounting method
- TDS is applicable if dividend exceeds prescribed thresholds
- Deduction for interest expense is allowed up to 20% of dividend income

Dividend income must be disclosed in the Income Tax Return under the correct head.

## Features

- Taxed at individual slab rates
- No exemption for most taxpayers
- TDS applicable beyond threshold limits
- Limited deduction for interest expenses
- Applicable to both domestic and foreign dividends
- Integrated with overall income computation Documents Required
- Dividend statements from companies or brokers
- Bank statements reflecting dividend credits
- Form 26AS / AIS for TDS verification
- Investment statements (demat account)
- Interest expense records (if claiming deduction)

Accurate reconciliation ensures proper reporting and avoids mismatches.

## How Taxation Works in Practice

1. **Identify total dividend received** — Aggregate dividends from all sources during the Financial Year.
2. **Verify TDS deducted** — Check Form 26AS or AIS for tax deducted by companies.
3. **Compute taxable amount** — Include dividend income under “Income from Other Sources.”
4. **Claim allowable deductions** — Interest expense (if any) up to 20% of dividend income.
5. **Include in total income** — Taxed as per applicable slab rate.
6. **Pay additional tax if required** — If TDS is insufficient, balance tax must be paid. Examples

Example 1: Dividend from Listed Company An individual receives ₹50,000 as dividend. If they fall in the 30% tax bracket, the income is taxed accordingly, regardless of the company’s tax position.

Example 2: Multiple Dividend Sources Dividends received from various companies and mutual funds are aggregated and taxed as a single income stream.

Example 3: Interest Deduction Claim If ₹10,000 interest is paid on funds borrowed for investment, only up to 20% of dividend income can be claimed as deduction.

## Common Mistakes

- Assuming dividends are tax-free

This misconception persists from the earlier DDT regime.

- Ignoring small dividend entries Even minor amounts must be reported.
- Not reconciling with Form 26AS/AIS

Leads to mismatches and possible notices.

- Claiming excess interest deduction

Deduction is capped at 20% of dividend income.

- Overlooking foreign dividend reporting

Foreign dividends must also be disclosed and taxed.

## Conclusion

Dividend income is now fully integrated into the personal taxation framework, making it subject to slab-based taxation. This shift places greater responsibility on taxpayers to accurately track, report, and compute tax liability on such income. With proper documentation, reconciliation, and compliance under the Income Tax Act, 1961, dividend income can be managed efficiently without unexpected tax exposure.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 194A - TDS on Interest Other than Interest on Securities
articles.excerpt: Interest income forms a significant component of taxable earnings for individuals and businesses. To ensure tax collection at the source, Section 194A of the Income Tax Act, 1961 governs the deduction of Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) on…
articles.content:
## Overview

Interest income forms a significant component of taxable earnings for individuals and businesses. To ensure tax collection at the source, Section 194A of the Income Tax Act, 1961 governs the deduction of Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) on interest payments other than interest on securities. This provision primarily applies to interest earned on bank deposits, loans, and other financial instruments, making it one of the most widely applicable TDS sections. Explanation / Core Concept Section 194A requires specified persons to deduct TDS when paying interest income (excluding interest on securities) to a resident.

### Common types of interest covered include

- Interest on fixed deposits (FDs)
- Interest on recurring deposits (RDs)
- Interest on unsecured loans
- Interest paid by firms to partners (in certain contexts)
- Interest from non-banking financial companies (NBFCs)

The deduction is triggered at the time of credit or payment, whichever is earlier.

## Key Rules / Applicability

- Applicable to payments made to resident individuals or entities
- Deductors include banks, financial institutions, companies, firms, and certain individuals/HUFs
- Threshold limits:

○ ₹40,000 (₹50,000 for senior citizens) for bank/post office interest ○ ₹5,000 in other cases

- TDS Rate:

○ 10% if PAN is available ○ Higher rate if PAN is not furnished

- Certain entities (banks, co-operative societies, post offices) have specific applicability rules Features
- Covers a wide range of interest payments
- Different thresholds based on payer type
- Higher exemption limit for senior citizens
- Deduction at earlier of credit or payment
- Linked with Form 26Q for reporting
- Enables tracking through Form 26AS Documents Required
- PAN of recipient
- Interest certificates from bank or institution
- Deposit or loan agreements
- Payment records
- TDS challan details (CIN)
- TAN of deductor

Accurate records ensure proper reconciliation of income and tax deducted.

## Process / Procedure

1. **Identify interest payment** — Confirm that the payment falls under Section 194A.
2. **Check threshold limits** — Determine if total interest exceeds prescribed limits.
3. **Apply applicable TDS rate** — Based on availability of PAN.
4. **Deduct TDS at time of credit or payment** — Whichever occurs earlier.
5. **Deposit TDS with the government** — Within prescribed timelines.
6. **File TDS return (Form 26Q)** — Report transaction details quarterly.
7. **Issue TDS certificate (Form 16A)** — Provide proof to the recipient. Examples

Example 1: Bank Fixed Deposit An individual earns ₹60,000 as interest on FD. The bank deducts TDS since the amount exceeds ₹40,000 (or ₹50,000 for senior citizens).

Example 2: Loan Interest Payment A company pays ₹25,000 as interest on a loan to a resident individual. Since it exceeds ₹5,000, TDS is applicable.

Example 3: Multiple Deposits in Same Bank Interest across all deposits in the same bank branch is aggregated to determine threshold applicability.

## Common Mistakes

- Ignoring aggregate interest across accounts

Threshold applies to total interest, not individual deposits.

- Not submitting PAN Leads to higher TDS deduction.
- Assuming no tax liability due to low income

TDS may still be deducted unless proper declaration is submitted.

- Mismatch with Form 26AS

Incorrect reporting leads to reconciliation issues.

- Failure to submit Form 15G/15H (if eligible) Results in unnecessary TDS deduction.

## Conclusion

Section 194A plays a crucial role in ensuring tax compliance on interest income across financial instruments. Given its broad applicability, both deductors and recipients must be attentive to thresholds, rates, and reporting requirements. Proper documentation, timely deduction, and accurate reporting under the Income Tax Act, 1961 help avoid mismatches and ensure seamless tax credit for recipients.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 194H - TDS on Commission and Brokerage
articles.excerpt: Commission and brokerage payments are integral to many business models, particularly in sectors such as sales, real estate, insurance, and financial services. To ensure tax collection at the source of such earnings, Section 194H of the…
articles.content:
## Overview

Commission and brokerage payments are integral to many business models, particularly in sectors such as sales, real estate, insurance, and financial services. To ensure tax collection at the source of such earnings, Section 194H of the Income Tax Act, 1961 mandates deduction of Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) on specified commission and brokerage payments. This provision applies broadly to payments made to intermediaries who facilitate transactions but do not take ownership of goods or services. Explanation / Core Concept Section 194H requires deduction of TDS on payments made to resident persons by way of:

- Commission
- Brokerage

These payments arise when a person acts on behalf of another to:

- Facilitate sale or purchase of goods
- Provide services
- Arrange transactions between parties

The key characteristic is the agency relationship, where the recipient earns income for acting as an intermediary rather than as a principal. TDS is deducted at the time of credit or payment, whichever is earlier.

## Key Rules / Applicability

- Applicable to payments made to resident agents, brokers, or intermediaries
- Deductor includes companies, firms, and individuals/HUFs (subject to turnover limits)
- Threshold limit:

○ ₹15,000 per financial year

- TDS Rate:

○ 5%

- Not applicable to:

○ Commission paid by BSNL/MTNL to public call office operators (subject to conditions) ○ Certain discounts that are not in the nature of commission Correct classification between commission and trade discount is critical.

## Features

- Covers agency-based transactions
- Applies to both commission and brokerage
- Threshold-based deduction
- Fixed TDS rate of 5%
- Deduction at earlier of credit or payment
- Reported in Form 26Q Documents Required
- PAN of recipient
- Agreement or arrangement defining commission structure
- Invoice or commission statement
- Payment records
- TDS challan details (CIN)
- TAN of deductor

Clear documentation helps establish the nature of the transaction.

## Process / Procedure

1. **Identify nature of payment** — Confirm whether the payment qualifies as commission or brokerage.
2. **Check threshold limit** — Determine if total payments exceed ₹15,000 in the financial year.
3. **Apply TDS rate** — Deduct tax at 5% on applicable amount.
4. **Deduct TDS at time of credit or payment** — Whichever occurs earlier.
5. **Deposit TDS with the government** — Within prescribed timelines.
6. **File TDS return (Form 26Q)** — Report deductee and challan details.
7. **Issue TDS certificate (Form 16A)** — Provide proof to the recipient. Examples

Example 1: Sales Commission A company pays ₹50,000 as commission to a sales agent. Since the amount exceeds ₹15,000, TDS is deducted at 5%.

Example 2: Real Estate Brokerage A property broker receives brokerage for facilitating a sale. The payer deducts TDS under Section 194H before making payment.

Example 3: Insurance Commission Commission paid to an insurance agent is subject to TDS if threshold conditions are met.

## Common Mistakes

- Confusing commission with discount

Trade discounts are not subject to TDS, but commission is.

- Ignoring threshold aggregation

Multiple payments exceeding ₹15,000 collectively attract TDS.

- Incorrect classification under other sections

Misclassifying under Section 194C or 194J leads to incorrect rates.

- Failure to deduct at correct time

Deduction must be at earlier of credit or payment.

- Incorrect PAN details

Leads to higher TDS and compliance issues.

## Conclusion

Section 194H ensures tax compliance on income earned through intermediary services. Given its wide applicability across industries, correct identification of commission-based transactions is essential. Accurate deduction, proper classification, and timely reporting under the Income Tax Act, 1961 help avoid disputes and ensure seamless tax credit for recipients.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 194T: TDS on Payment by Partnership Firm to Partners
articles.excerpt: Payments made by partnership firms to their partners—such as remuneration, interest, bonus, or commission—have traditionally been governed through specific deduction and taxation rules. With the introduction of Section 194T under the…
articles.content:
## Overview

Payments made by partnership firms to their partners—such as remuneration, interest, bonus, or commission—have traditionally been governed through specific deduction and taxation rules. With the introduction of Section 194T under the Income Tax Act, 1961, such payments are now also brought within the scope of Tax Deducted at Source (TDS). This provision strengthens reporting and ensures tax collection at the point of payment, aligning partner-level taxation with broader TDS compliance mechanisms. Explanation / Core Concept Section 194T mandates that a partnership firm (including LLPs) must deduct TDS on specified payments made to partners. These include:

- Salary or remuneration
- Interest on capital
- Bonus or commission

The deduction is required at the time of credit or payment, whichever is earlier. This is significant because such payments, while allowable as expenses (subject to limits), are also taxable in the hands of partners. TDS ensures early tax collection and improved transparency.

## Key Rules / Applicability

- Applicable to partnership firms and LLPs
- Covers payments made to resident partners
- Includes:

○ Remuneration ○ Interest ○ Bonus and commission

- TDS Rate:

○ 10%

- Threshold limit:

○ ₹20,000 per financial year (aggregate per partner)

- Deduction required at earlier of:

○ Credit to partner’s account, or ○ Actual payment This section applies irrespective of whether the firm claims deduction for such payments under other provisions.

## Features

- Specifically targets partner-level payments
- Covers multiple forms of compensation
- Threshold-based applicability
- Uniform TDS rate of 10%
- Enhances reporting and traceability
- Linked with quarterly TDS return filing (Form 26Q) Documents Required
- PAN of partners
- Partnership deed specifying remuneration/interest terms
- Capital account statements
- Payment and ledger records
- TDS challan details (CIN)
- TAN of the firm

The partnership deed is particularly important, as it governs the allowability and structure of payments.

## Process / Procedure

1. **Identify eligible payments** — Determine payments to partners that fall under Section 194T.
2. **Check threshold limit** — Confirm if aggregate payments exceed ₹20,000 in the financial year.
3. **Apply TDS rate** — Deduct tax at 10% on applicable payments.
4. **Deduct TDS at time of credit or payment** — Whichever occurs earlier.
5. **Deposit TDS with the government** — Within prescribed timelines.
6. **File TDS return (Form 26Q)** — Report partner-wise deduction details.
7. **Issue TDS certificate (Form 16A)** — Provide partners with proof of deduction. Examples

Example 1: Partner Remuneration A firm pays ₹3,00,000 as remuneration to a partner. Since it exceeds ₹20,000, TDS is deducted at 10%.

Example 2: Interest on Capital Interest of ₹50,000 is credited to a partner’s capital account. TDS applies at the time of credit.

Example 3: Commission to Partner Commission paid based on profits or performance is also subject to TDS under this section.

## Common Mistakes

- Ignoring threshold aggregation

Multiple payments exceeding ₹20,000 collectively attract TDS.

- Not deducting at time of credit

Even book entries trigger TDS liability.

- Mismatch with partnership deed terms

Incorrect or undocumented payments may lead to disallowance.

- Incorrect reporting in TDS returns Leads to credit mismatch for partners.
- Assuming exemption due to partner relationship

TDS applies despite the internal nature of payments.

## Conclusion

Section 194T introduces a structured TDS framework for payments made by partnership firms to partners, enhancing transparency and compliance. It ensures that such income is appropriately taxed at the recipient level while maintaining consistency in reporting. For firms, adherence to threshold limits, correct timing of deduction, and proper documentation under the Income Tax Act, 1961 is essential to avoid penalties and ensure seamless tax credit for partners.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 194I - TDS on Rent
articles.excerpt: Rental payments constitute a significant and recurring expenditure for businesses, particularly in sectors dependent on leased infrastructure such as offices, warehouses, factories, and equipment. To ensure tax collection at source on…
articles.content:
## Overview

Rental payments constitute a significant and recurring expenditure for businesses, particularly in sectors dependent on leased infrastructure such as offices, warehouses, factories, and equipment. To ensure tax collection at source on such payments, Section 194I of the Income Tax Act, 1961 mandates deduction of Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) on rent. The provision is designed to capture a broad spectrum of rental arrangements, extending beyond conventional property leases to include plant, machinery, and equipment rentals. Scope and Definition of Rent Under Section 194I, “rent” is defined expansively. It includes any payment made under a lease, sub-lease, tenancy, or similar arrangement for the use of:

- Land
- Building (including factory buildings)
- Land appurtenant to a building
- Plant and machinery
- Equipment
- Furniture and fittings

The definition focuses on right to use an asset, rather than ownership transfer, making the provision applicable across varied commercial arrangements.

## Applicability and Threshold

### The obligation to deduct TDS arises when

- The payer is a specified person (including companies, firms, and certain individuals/HUFs based on turnover), and
- Aggregate rent paid or credited exceeds ₹2,40,000 in a financial year

This threshold applies per payee, and aggregation across multiple payments is required to determine applicability. TDS Rates The rate of TDS depends on the nature of the asset:

- 2% for rent of plant, machinery, or equipment
- 10% for rent of land, building, furniture, or fittings

The distinction is critical, particularly in composite agreements where multiple assets are involved. In such cases, correct classification or bifurcation becomes essential. Timing and Trigger Point TDS must be deducted at the earlier of:

- Credit of rent to the account of the payee, or
- Actual payment

This includes situations where rent is credited to a suspense account or similar ledger, reinforcing the principle that accounting recognition itself triggers TDS liability. Operational Considerations In practice, compliance under Section 194I requires careful alignment between contractual terms and accounting treatment. Lease agreements should clearly specify:

- Nature of asset being rented
- Break-up of rent components (especially in composite arrangements)
- Payment frequency and escalation clauses

For long-term leases or advance payments, TDS applicability must be evaluated at the time of credit, even if the benefit of the asset extends over multiple periods. Illustrative Scenarios A company leasing office premises for ₹30,000 per month crosses the annual threshold of ₹2,40,000. TDS at 10% applies on the rent paid or credited. In another scenario, a manufacturing unit rents machinery for ₹5,00,000 annually. Here, TDS is deducted at 2%, reflecting the classification of the asset. Where a single agreement includes both building rent and equipment usage, the absence of a clear bifurcation may lead to the entire payment being taxed at the higher rate. Compliance and Reporting Once TDS is deducted:

- It must be deposited within prescribed timelines
- Reported in quarterly TDS returns (Form 26Q)
- Reflected in the payee’s Form 26AS

Issuance of TDS certificates (Form 16A) ensures that the recipient can claim credit appropriately. Areas of Practical Concern A recurring issue in application is misclassification of payments, particularly distinguishing between rent and contractual or service payments. For instance, payments involving bundled services may sometimes fall under different TDS provisions depending on their dominant nature. Another area of concern is threshold tracking, especially where multiple agreements or periodic payments cumulatively exceed limits. Delays in deduction or deposit not only attract interest but may also lead to disallowance of the corresponding expense.

## Conclusion

Section 194I plays a central role in taxing income arising from the use of assets, reflecting the economic significance of rental transactions across sectors. Its broad definition and dual-rate structure require careful interpretation and consistent application. Effective compliance depends on accurate classification, timely deduction, and proper documentation, all aligned with the requirements of the Income Tax Act, 1961.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 44AB Income Tax Audit: Due Date, Criteria, Audit Report, Penalty
articles.excerpt: Tax audit under Section 44AB of the Income Tax Act, 1961 is a key compliance mechanism aimed at ensuring accuracy in financial reporting and proper disclosure of income. It applies to specified categories of taxpayers whose turnover,…
articles.content:
## Overview

Tax audit under Section 44AB of the Income Tax Act, 1961 is a key compliance mechanism aimed at ensuring accuracy in financial reporting and proper disclosure of income. It applies to specified categories of taxpayers whose turnover, receipts, or income structure warrants independent verification by a chartered accountant. Beyond a procedural requirement, the tax audit framework plays a significant role in standardizing financial reporting and minimizing discrepancies in tax computation. Concept and Objective The objective of a tax audit is not merely verification of accounts but ensuring that:

- Income is correctly computed in accordance with tax provisions
- Deductions and exemptions are properly claimed
- Books of accounts reflect a true and fair view
- Compliance with various provisions of the Act is maintained

The audit culminates in the submission of prescribed audit reports, forming part of the taxpayer’s overall return filing process.

## Applicability and Threshold Criteria

Tax audit applicability varies based on the nature of activity: Business

- Turnover exceeds ₹1 crore in a financial year
- Threshold increased to ₹10 crore where cash transactions are within prescribed limits (generally ≤5%) Profession
- Gross receipts exceed ₹50 lakh Presumptive Taxation Cases Audit becomes applicable where:
- Income declared is lower than prescribed presumptive rates under Sections such as

44AD or 44ADA, and

- Total income exceeds the basic exemption limit

This makes the audit requirement not just turnover-driven but also linked to the method of income declaration. Due Date for Tax Audit The due date for furnishing the tax audit report is typically:

- 30th September of the Assessment Year
- Extended timelines may apply based on notifications issued in specific years

The audit report must be filed electronically before or along with the Income Tax Return. Audit Report Forms Tax audit reports are furnished in prescribed formats:

- Form 3CA – Where accounts are already audited under another law
- Form 3CB – Where no other statutory audit is applicable
- Form 3CD – Detailed statement of particulars forming part of the audit

Form 3CD is particularly comprehensive, requiring disclosures on:

- Depreciation
- Related party transactions
- TDS compliance
- Disallowances under various sections Process and Compliance Flow

The tax audit process follows a structured sequence:

1. **Maintenance of books of accounts** — Proper records must be maintained as per applicable provisions.
2. **Appointment of a chartered accountant** — The auditor conducts examination and verification of records.
3. **Audit execution** — Includes checking compliance, reconciliations, and reporting requirements.
4. **Preparation of audit report** — Forms 3CA/3CB and 3CD are finalized.
5. **Online submission** — The report is uploaded by the auditor and accepted by the taxpayer.
6. **ITR filing** — The return is filed referencing the audit report details. Illustrative Situations

A trading business with turnover of ₹1.8 crore falls within the audit requirement and must obtain a tax audit report before filing its return. A professional with receipts of ₹65 lakh is required to undergo audit due to exceeding the prescribed threshold. In another case, a taxpayer opting for presumptive taxation declares income lower than the prescribed percentage while having total income above the exemption limit—triggering mandatory audit.

## Penalty for Non-Compliance

Failure to comply with Section 44AB attracts penalty under Section 271B:

- 0.5% of turnover or gross receipts, subject to a maximum of ₹1,50,000

However, penalty may not be imposed if the taxpayer can demonstrate reasonable cause for non-compliance.

## Practical Considerations

### In application, tax audit compliance often hinges on

- Accurate turnover determination
- Proper classification between business and professional income
- Monitoring of cash transaction thresholds
- Timely coordination between taxpayer and auditor

Delays in initiating the audit process frequently lead to last-minute compliance risks, particularly in cases involving complex transactions or incomplete documentation.

## Conclusion

Section 44AB establishes a structured framework for ensuring transparency and accuracy in tax reporting. Its applicability extends beyond large businesses, covering professionals and certain presumptive taxation scenarios. Given its detailed reporting requirements and statutory timelines, proactive planning and disciplined record-keeping are essential. Compliance under the Income Tax Act, 1961 not only avoids penalties but also strengthens the integrity of financial reporting.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Form 15CA and Form 15CB: Compliance for International Payments
articles.excerpt: Mandatory TDS compliance forms for foreign remittances — when to file each Part of 15CA, when you need a CA certificate (15CB), DTAA considerations, and Rule 37BB exempt categories.
articles.content:
## Purpose

Form 15CA and Form 15CB are documents required when a resident of India makes a payment to a non-resident or foreign company. They are a compliance mechanism through which the government tracks whether tax has been correctly deducted at source on international remittances **before the money leaves India**.

The logic is straightforward: payments made to non-residents may be taxable in India depending on the nature of income and the applicable tax treaty. The government wants to ensure that TDS is correctly applied before the foreign payee takes the money abroad.

- **Form 15CA** is the declaration by the remitter (payer).
- **Form 15CB** is the certificate from a Chartered Accountant confirming that the payment is taxable and that correct TDS has been applied.

## Who Should Use These Forms

### Form 15CA is required from

- Any individual, firm, or company making a remittance to a non-resident that may be taxable in India
- This includes salary to NRI employees, consultancy fees, royalties, dividends, property purchase payments from NRIs, loan repayments, and similar transactions

### Form 15CB is required from

- A **Chartered Accountant** (the payer obtains this from their CA)
- Required only for certain categories of payments above specified thresholds where taxability is not straightforward

> [!NOTE] Exemptions exist
> Some remittances are exempt from these requirements (e.g., import payments covered under specific RBI guidelines, travel expenses, education payments abroad under personal accounts).

## Form 15CA: Four Parts Explained

| Part | When to Use |
| --- | --- |
| Part A | Payment not exceeding Rs. 5 lakh in a financial year (aggregate) and taxable |
| Part B | Order or certificate from the Assessing Officer has been obtained regarding TDS |
| Part C | Amount is taxable, exceeds Rs. 5 lakh, and Form 15CB has been obtained from CA |
| Part D | Payment is not taxable in India (covered by tax treaty or exempt) |

## Step-by-Step Process

1. **Determine the nature of the payment.** Is it for services, royalty, interest, salary, dividend, or something else?
2. **Check the applicable tax treaty (DTAA)** between India and the recipient's country. India has DTAAs with over 90 countries. The treaty may reduce or eliminate Indian tax on the payment.
3. **Determine taxability.**
    - If the payment is **not taxable** in India (due to treaty or exemption): File Form 15CA Part D.
    - If taxable and payment is **below Rs. 5 lakh** in aggregate: File Form 15CA Part A.
    - If taxable and payment **exceeds Rs. 5 lakh**: Obtain Form 15CB from a CA, then file Form 15CA Part C.
4. **Engage a Chartered Accountant for Form 15CB.** The CA reviews the nature of the remittance, applicable treaty provisions, correct TDS rate, and whether TDS has been or should be deducted. The CA then issues Form 15CB certifying the above.
5. **File Form 15CA online.** Log in to `incometax.gov.in` → e-File → Income Tax Forms → File Income Tax Forms. Select Form 15CA. Enter all details about the remitter, remittee, nature of payment, country, currency, amount, and TDS deducted. Upload or reference the Form 15CB details (acknowledgment number).
6. **Generate the acknowledgment** and furnish it to your bank or authorised dealer (AD) through which the remittance will be sent.
7. **The bank processes the remittance** only after receiving the Form 15CA acknowledgment and Form 15CB (where applicable).

## Transactions Exempt from Form 15CA / 15CB

| Category | Condition |
| --- | --- |
| Import of goods | Standard commercial imports |
| Personal travel expenses abroad | Within permissible LRS limits |
| Maintenance of close relatives abroad | Under LRS |
| Studies abroad | Under LRS |
| Medical treatment abroad | Under LRS |
| Many others under Rule 37BB | Specifically listed 28 categories |

> [!NOTE] Rule 37BB
> Rule 37BB of the Income Tax Rules lists **28 specific types** of remittances that are exempt from the Form 15CA / 15CB requirement.

## Common Mistakes

1. **Not filing Form 15CA before the remittance is processed.** The form must be filed and presented to the bank before the money is sent. Filing it afterward is not compliant.
2. **Choosing the wrong Part of Form 15CA.** Selecting Part D when the payment is actually taxable (to avoid obtaining Form 15CB) is a misrepresentation with serious legal consequences.
3. **Not consulting a CA when Form 15CB is required.** Some payers try to self-assess taxability and skip Form 15CB. This is non-compliant and can expose them to penalties.
4. **Not considering the DTAA.** Payments that appear taxable under domestic law may be fully exempt or taxed at a reduced rate under an applicable treaty.
5. **Using the form for payments that are fully exempt under Rule 37BB.** While this is not a penalty-attracting mistake, it creates unnecessary compliance work and delays.

## Tips

1. **Maintain a clear paper trail** of every international payment that required Form 15CA / 15CB, including the CA certificate, the online acknowledgment, and the bank's SWIFT confirmation.
2. **If you are a small business** making regular payments to foreign consultants or software vendors, set up a standardised process with your CA firm so that Form 15CB issuance and 15CA filing happen smoothly.
3. **Track DTAA provisions** carefully for your key foreign payee countries. Some treaties require the foreign payee to provide a **Tax Residency Certificate (TRC)** for the treaty benefit to apply.
4. **Do not wait for the invoice payment due date** to initiate compliance. Start the process **5 to 7 working days** before the payment is due.
5. **Monitor changes in tax treaties.** India has renegotiated several DTAAs in recent years. A rate that was applicable last year may have changed.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. I need to pay a foreign software company for a SaaS subscription. Do I need Form 15CA?

It depends on whether the payment is classified as a **royalty** (taxable in India) or a purchase of services (may not be taxable if the company has no permanent establishment in India). Many SaaS subscriptions fall under royalty in Indian tax law, making Form 15CA necessary. Consult a CA.

### Q2. Can Form 15CB be issued by a CA who is not the company's statutory auditor?

Yes. **Any practising Chartered Accountant** can issue Form 15CB. It does not need to be the same CA who audits your financial statements.

### Q3. My bank rejected my international transfer because I did not submit Form 15CA. What now?

File Form 15CA online immediately, get Form 15CB from your CA if required, and present the acknowledgments to the bank. The bank will then process the transfer.

### Q4. Is Form 15CA required for repayment of an ECB (External Commercial Borrowing)?

**Repayment of principal** of ECB is typically not taxable in India and may fall under exempt categories under Rule 37BB. **Interest payments** on ECB, however, may be taxable and would require Form 15CA. Verify with a CA based on specific loan terms and the applicable DTAA.

### Q5. What is the penalty for not filing Form 15CA before a foreign remittance?

Under **Section 271-I** of the Income Tax Act, a penalty of **Rs. 1,00,000** can be levied for failure to furnish information or for furnishing inaccurate information in Form 15CA. The bank may also refuse to process the transfer without the form.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Form 16: Your Complete TDS Certificate from Employer
articles.excerpt: Form 16 is the salary TDS certificate issued by your employer every year. Structure (Part A and Part B), how to use it for ITR filing, common mistakes, and cross-checks with Form 26AS.
articles.content:
## Purpose

Form 16 is a TDS (Tax Deducted at Source) certificate issued by employers to employees every financial year. It is one of the most important documents in the hands of a salaried taxpayer. It certifies how much tax was deducted from your salary during the year and deposited with the government, and provides a complete breakup of your salary and the deductions that were considered while computing your tax liability.

Think of Form 16 as a **pre-filled summary of everything your employer knows about your income and tax**. For most salaried taxpayers with straightforward income, Form 16 contains almost everything they need to file their income tax return.

## Who Should Use It

- Every **salaried employee** whose employer has deducted TDS from salary
- Individuals filing **ITR-1 or ITR-2** (primarily salaried filers)
- Anyone applying for a home loan, visa, or bank credit (Form 16 is commonly required as income proof)
- Tax professionals preparing returns for salaried clients

> [!NOTE] When Form 16 is not required
> If your total salary income is below the taxable threshold and no TDS was deducted, your employer is not legally required to issue Form 16. However, many employers issue it anyway as a salary summary.

## Structure of Form 16

Form 16 has two parts.

### Part A

| Detail | Contents |
| --- | --- |
| Employer's TAN and PAN | Identifying the deductor |
| Employee's PAN | Identifying the deductee |
| Employment period | Duration of employment during the year |
| Summary of TDS deducted | Quarter-wise TDS deducted and deposited |
| Challan details | Government deposit reference numbers |

### Part B

| Detail | Contents |
| --- | --- |
| Gross salary | Total salary before any deductions |
| Allowance exemptions | HRA, LTA, and other exempt allowances |
| Net salary (taxable) | After exemptions |
| Deductions under Chapter VI-A | 80C, 80D, 80CCD(1B), etc. |
| Total taxable income | Final income after all deductions |
| Tax computation | Tax on total income, surcharge, and cess |
| Relief under Section 89 | If applicable |
| TDS deducted | Amount deducted and deposited |

> [!IMPORTANT] Part A is government-authenticated; Part B is employer-generated
> Part A is generated directly from TRACES (the TDS Reconciliation Analysis and Correction Enabling System) and carries a digital signature. Part B is generated by the employer and may vary in format.

## Step-by-Step: How to Use Form 16 for ITR Filing

1. **Collect Form 16 from your employer.** It should be issued by **June 15** following the end of the financial year (e.g., by June 15, 2025 for FY 2024-25).
2. **Check Part A carefully.** Verify your PAN, employer TAN, and the TDS amounts match what is shown in your Form 26AS or AIS on the Income Tax portal.
3. **Review Part B.** Verify that the salary figures, allowance exemptions, and deductions match your actual salary slips and investment declarations you submitted to HR.
4. **Cross-check the TDS credit in Form 26AS.** Go to the Income Tax portal, download your Form 26AS, and confirm that the TDS shown in Form 16 Part A matches what is credited under your PAN.
5. **Identify any other income not covered in Form 16.** Bank interest, rental income, capital gains, or freelance income are not in Form 16 and must be added separately in your ITR.
6. **Proceed to file your ITR**, using Form 16 data as the primary input.

## Common Mistakes

1. **Trusting Part B blindly without cross-checking.** Part B is prepared by HR or the payroll team. Errors in HRA exemption calculations, wrong investment declarations, or missed salary components are not uncommon. Always verify against your salary slips.
2. **Not checking Form 26AS for TDS credit mismatch.** If your employer deposited TDS but under a wrong PAN or there was a delay, the credit may not appear in your name. Filing without resolving this causes tax demand notices later.
3. **Assuming Form 16 covers all income.** It covers only salary. Interest income, mutual fund gains, rental income, and other sources must be added by you.
4. **Using last year's Form 16 details by mistake.** Some taxpayers file using the previous year's Form 16 values when the current year's document is delayed.
5. **Not collecting Form 16 from previous employer if you changed jobs during the year.** Both Part A documents (from each employer) are needed to report the full year's salary correctly.

## Tips

1. **If you changed employers during the year**, submit the previous employer's Form 16 or at least the salary details to your new employer. Otherwise, TDS might be computed incorrectly at the new employer, leaving you with a large tax shortfall.
2. **Cross-verify HRA exemption in Part B** with your actual rent receipts and rent agreement. Employers sometimes make errors in this calculation.
3. **Request Form 16 early**, especially if you plan to apply for a home loan. Many banks require Form 16 from the last 2 to 3 years as income proof.
4. **If your employer cannot issue Form 16** because the company shut down, use salary slips, bank statements, and your AIS to reconstruct the income data for ITR filing.
5. **Use Form 16 to verify if your employer correctly accounted for all investment proofs** you submitted. If they missed something, you can claim the deduction directly in your ITR even if it is not reflected in Form 16.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. Is Form 16 mandatory for filing ITR?

Form 16 is **not legally required** for ITR filing. You can file using your salary slips, bank statements, and AIS. However, Form 16 simplifies the process significantly and is considered the authoritative salary summary from your employer.

### Q2. My employer did not deduct any TDS because my income was below the threshold. Will they issue Form 16?

If no TDS was deducted, the employer is not obligated to issue Form 16. However, they may still issue a salary certificate or Part B alone as a courtesy.

### Q3. I noticed my Form 16 has an incorrect PAN. What should I do?

Contact your employer's HR or payroll team immediately. An incorrect PAN in Form 16 means the TDS credit will not appear under your correct PAN in the tax system, and you will not be able to claim that credit. The employer must correct and reissue the certificate.

### Q4. Can I get Form 16 for a company I worked for that is now closed?

If the company has filed its TDS returns with the correct PAN, your TDS credit will still appear in your **Form 26AS**. Download your 26AS for the relevant year from the Income Tax portal to verify and use those figures for filing.

### Q5. I received a bonus in April that relates to last financial year. Will it appear in my Form 16?

Bonus is taxable in the year it is **received**, not the year it relates to. If the bonus was paid in April 2025, it will appear in the Form 16 for FY 2025-26, not FY 2024-25.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Form 16 vs Form 16A: Understanding the Difference
articles.excerpt: Form 16 is for salary TDS. Form 16A is for every other kind of TDS — FD interest, professional fees, rent, commission. A clear side-by-side comparison with sections, deductors, and filing steps.
articles.content:
## Purpose
