1. **Assuming the 80C limit is Rs. 1.5 lakh on top of EPF.** EPF counts within the Rs. 1.5 lakh ceiling. Many people over-invest not realising this.
2. **Buying endowment or money-back policies for 80C benefits.** The returns are poor, the lock-in is long, and the insurance coverage is inadequate. This is perhaps the most common and costly 80C mistake.
3. **Investing in 80C instruments after switching to the new tax regime.** Contributions made after opting for the new regime do not yield any 80C benefit.
4. **Ignoring NSC interest reinvestment.** The interest accrued on NSC after the first year is treated as a fresh 80C-eligible investment each year. This is a hidden benefit many miss.
5. **Forgetting to claim tuition fees for children.** You can claim fees paid to any school, college, or university for up to two children. This is often overlooked in salary-class households.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. Can I claim 80C deduction for principal repayment on a home loan taken for a second property?

Yes, you can claim principal repayment under 80C for a second home loan as well. However, if you sell the property within 5 years of purchase, the deductions claimed will be added back to your income in the year of sale.

### Q2. Is there any way to go beyond the Rs. 1.5 lakh 80C limit?

Yes. Section 80CCD(1B) allows an **additional Rs. 50,000 deduction** for NPS contributions, over and above the 80C ceiling. This brings your total deduction potential to Rs. 2 lakh on retirement savings alone.

### Q3. My employer contributes to NPS on my behalf. Does that count under 80C?

No. Employer contributions to NPS are deductible under **Section 80CCD(2)**, which has its own separate limit and does not eat into your 80C ceiling.

### Q4. Can a HUF invest in ELSS and claim 80C deduction?

Yes. HUFs are eligible for 80C deductions and can invest in ELSS in the HUF's name to claim the benefit.

### Q5. Can I claim 80C for a life insurance policy I took for my spouse or children?

Yes. Premium paid for life insurance on the life of your spouse or children qualifies for 80C deduction, subject to the overall Rs. 1.5 lakh limit.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: How to Save Tax at Different Income Levels: Strategies for Rs. 10L to Rs. 1 Crore+
articles.excerpt: Practical tax-saving strategies for Indian salaried professionals at Rs. 10L, 20L, 30L, 50L, and 1 crore+ income levels: regime comparison, deductions, surcharge thresholds, and HNI-specific tools.
articles.content:
## Overview

Tax planning is not a one-size-fits-all exercise. The right strategy for someone earning Rs. 10 lakh per year is very different from what makes sense at Rs. 50 lakh or Rs. 1 crore. As income rises, the marginal tax rate changes, the instruments that offer meaningful savings change, and the trade-offs between the old and new tax regimes shift considerably.

This guide walks you through practical, income-level-specific tax saving strategies under the Indian tax system, grounded in the FY 2024-25 / AY 2025-26 framework. All calculations assume the individual is salaried unless noted otherwise.

## Tax Slab Reference

### Old Regime (FY 2024-25)

| Income Slab | Tax Rate |
| --- | --- |
| Up to Rs. 2,50,000 | Nil |
| Rs. 2,50,001 to Rs. 5,00,000 | 5% |
| Rs. 5,00,001 to Rs. 10,00,000 | 20% |
| Above Rs. 10,00,000 | 30% |

### New Regime (FY 2024-25)

| Income Slab | Tax Rate |
| --- | --- |
| Up to Rs. 3,00,000 | Nil |
| Rs. 3,00,001 to Rs. 7,00,000 | 5% |
| Rs. 7,00,001 to Rs. 10,00,000 | 10% |
| Rs. 10,00,001 to Rs. 12,00,000 | 15% |
| Rs. 12,00,001 to Rs. 15,00,000 | 20% |
| Above Rs. 15,00,000 | 30% |

> [!NOTE] Standard deduction
> Rs. 75,000 standard deduction is available under the new regime as well (enhanced in Budget 2024).

## Strategy for Rs. 10 Lakh Income

At Rs. 10 lakh gross, the **new tax regime often wins** for those with minimal investments.

### Without investments

| Regime | Taxable Income | Approx Tax |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Old (SD Rs. 50K + 80C Rs. 1.5L) | Rs. 8,00,000 | Rs. 92,500 |
| New (SD Rs. 75K) | Rs. 9,25,000 | Rs. 54,600 |

New regime saves roughly **Rs. 37,900** without any investment.

### With home loan and full 80C

| Regime | Taxable Income | Approx Tax |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Old (SD + 80C Rs. 1.5L + 24b Rs. 2L) | Rs. 6,00,000 | ~Rs. 32,500 |

The old regime wins decisively with a home loan.

**Key tools at Rs. 10 lakh:** 80C, 80D (health insurance), HRA exemption (if renting), and 24(b) if you have a home loan.

## Strategy for Rs. 20 Lakh Income

At Rs. 20 lakh, you are firmly in the 30% slab in both regimes. The value of each deduction is higher.

### Maximum deductions available

| Deduction | Section | Amount |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Standard deduction | Salary | Rs. 50,000 |
| Investments (EPF, ELSS, PPF, insurance) | 80C | Rs. 1,50,000 |
| NPS voluntary contribution | 80CCD(1B) | Rs. 50,000 |
| Health insurance (self + parents) | 80D | Rs. 50,000 to Rs. 75,000 |
| Home loan interest | 24(b) | Rs. 2,00,000 |
| HRA exemption (if applicable) | 10(13A) | Varies |

**Potential total deductions:** Rs. 5,00,000 to Rs. 5,75,000

At 30% slab, Rs. 5 lakh in deductions saves Rs. 1,50,000 + cess. The **old regime** is typically better at Rs. 20 lakh for individuals who are home buyers or have significant health insurance outgo.

## Strategy for Rs. 30 Lakh Income

At Rs. 30 lakh, you are deep in the 30% bracket. Tax planning becomes multi-dimensional.

### Additional tools beyond the standard stack

- **NPS via employer (80CCD(2)):** If your employer contributes to NPS, up to 10% of basic + DA is deductible in **both regimes**. This is the only deduction available in the new regime beyond the standard deduction.
- **House property loss:** If you have a second or rented property, the loss from house property (interest minus rent received) can offset up to Rs. 2 lakh of other income.
- **Capital gains optimisation:** At Rs. 30 lakh, managing short-term vs long-term capital gains becomes important. Shift profit-booking to long-term wherever possible.

### Estimated tax saved through full optimisation (old regime)

| Deduction | Amount | Tax Saved at 30% |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 80C | Rs. 1,50,000 | Rs. 45,000 |
| 80CCD(1B) | Rs. 50,000 | Rs. 15,000 |
| 80D | Rs. 75,000 | Rs. 22,500 |
| 24(b) — home loan | Rs. 2,00,000 | Rs. 60,000 |
| **Total** | **Rs. 4,75,000** | **Rs. 1,42,500** |

## Strategy for Rs. 50 Lakh Income

At Rs. 50 lakh, **surcharge of 10% applies** under both regimes. This increases effective tax rate significantly.

### Key instruments that remain relevant

1. All standard deductions (80C, 80CCD(1B), 80D, 24(b)) in the old regime.
2. **Business income restructuring:** High earners often shift to a professional structure, allowing deduction of actual business expenses.
3. **Section 54 / 54F (capital gains exemption):** If you sell property or assets and reinvest in residential property, gains can be exempt.
4. **Charitable donations under 80G:** 100% or 50% deductions on donations to eligible institutions (not political parties).
5. **HUF creation:** Creating a separate HUF entity allows a second set of basic exemption limits and investments.

> [!TIP] Focus shifts at Rs. 50 lakh+
> The goal moves from "maximise deductions" to "income restructuring and deferral."

## Strategy for Rs. 1 Crore+ Income

At income above Rs. 1 crore, the **surcharge is 15%** under both regimes (for income between Rs. 1 crore and Rs. 2 crore). The effective tax rate can exceed 42%.

### Strategies relevant at this level

1. **Employer NPS under 80CCD(2)** — available even in the new regime. At 30% + 15% surcharge + cess, Rs. 10 lakh contributed by employer to NPS saves approximately Rs. 4.6 lakh in tax.
2. **Family income splitting** — paying salary to family members for genuine work in a business lowers overall tax by utilising their lower slabs.
3. **Gratuity, VRS, and leave encashment planning** — for those nearing retirement, these provide significant exempt payouts.
4. **LTCG exemption harvesting** — selling equity holdings strategically to realise LTCG within the Rs. 1.25 lakh annual exemption each year.
5. **Trusts and foundations** — ultra-HNIs may use charitable trusts to redirect income in a tax-efficient and socially impactful manner.

## Optimization Tips

1. **Determine your regime first, then invest.** Too many taxpayers invest in 80C instruments and then realise they should have been in the new regime. Run the comparison annually before making any commitment.
2. **As income rises, focus on tax-free income sources.** PPF interest, LTCG from equity up to Rs. 1.25 lakh, and maturity from life insurance (subject to conditions) are all tax-free.
3. **Employer NPS works at every income level in the new regime.** This is the one universal deduction available under the new regime.
4. **Do not ignore 80D at higher incomes.** Rs. 75,000 of health insurance premiums (self + senior citizen parents) saves Rs. 22,500 at 30% + surcharge. This is pure insurance you need anyway.
5. **Review tax strategy every year**, not just in March. Market conditions, salary changes, and regulation changes make an annual review essential.

## Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Defaulting to the old regime out of habit at lower income levels.** For incomes under Rs. 12–13 lakh with limited deductions, the new regime is often better.
2. **Treating all deductions as equally valuable.** A deduction worth Rs. 1 lakh saves Rs. 20,000 at 20% slab and Rs. 30,000 at 30% slab. Not all instruments are equally worth the lock-in.
3. **Ignoring surcharge thresholds.** At Rs. 50 lakh and Rs. 1 crore, surcharge kicks in abruptly. Sometimes earning slightly below the threshold or structuring income slightly differently can save a disproportionate amount of tax.
4. **Not using Section 87A rebate at lower incomes.** Under the new regime, if taxable income is up to Rs. 7 lakh, the rebate under 87A brings the tax to zero. This is an enormous benefit that many taxpayers under Rs. 7 lakh income miss by defaulting to the old regime unnecessarily.
5. **Waiting till February to plan.** Investments made in March create rushed decisions. The best tax planning begins in April at the start of the financial year.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. At what income level does the old tax regime clearly beat the new one?

Generally, if your total deductions (80C + 80D + 24(b) + HRA + NPS) exceed **Rs. 3.75 lakh**, the old regime becomes more beneficial. This threshold shifts depending on your specific income level and deduction mix.

### Q2. Can I switch between old and new tax regime every year?

Salaried individuals can switch every year. Self-employed individuals and business owners who opt for the new regime cannot switch back and forth freely; once they opt out of the new regime, they can only return under specific conditions.

### Q3. Does the surcharge on high incomes apply under both regimes?

Yes. Surcharge applies under both regimes based on total income. However, the **new regime caps the surcharge at 25% for incomes above Rs. 5 crore**, whereas the old regime levies 37% surcharge. This makes the new regime more attractive for very high earners.

### Q4. What is the most effective single tax-saving instrument across all income levels?

**NPS through employer contribution under Section 80CCD(2)** is the most universally effective, because it works under both regimes, has no ceiling relative to the employer's contribution capacity, and benefits retirement savings simultaneously.

### Q5. Are there any legal ways to reduce tax below zero (get a refund) through planning?

Tax refunds are possible if TDS deducted by your employer or others exceeds your actual tax liability. This can happen if you have significant deductions that were not accounted for during TDS. Planning your declarations to your employer accurately ensures correct TDS and avoids the need to wait for a refund.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Multiple Form 16: How to Handle TDS from Two or More Employers
articles.excerpt: Changed jobs mid-year? How to combine multiple Form 16s in a single ITR, prevent TDS under-deduction, and avoid Section 234B/234C interest — with a worked example.
articles.content:
## Purpose

Changing jobs during a financial year is increasingly common. When you move from one employer to another in the same financial year, you receive **separate Form 16 certificates from each employer**. Each employer knows only about the salary they paid you during their respective tenure. Neither employer automatically knows what the other paid.

This creates a specific tax challenge. If you do not disclose your previous employer's income to your new employer, the new employer calculates TDS only on their portion of salary, applying a full year's slab benefits to a partial year's income. The result is **under-deduction of TDS**, which leads to a tax shortfall that must be paid when you file your ITR.

## Why This Is a Common Problem

Each employer calculates TDS independently:

- **Previous employer:** Deducts TDS on salary paid for the period you worked there
- **New employer:** Deducts TDS on salary paid from date of joining onward

Unless you inform the new employer about your previous salary, the new employer applies the basic exemption and slab rates fresh from the start. Since the first few lakhs of income attract lower tax rates, both employers end up under-deducting TDS on your aggregate annual income.

> [!WARNING] The hidden tax shortfall
> At filing time, your actual total income pushes you into a higher slab, and the aggregate TDS falls short. You then owe tax at self-assessment, plus interest under **Section 234B and 234C** if the shortfall was significant.

## Step-by-Step Process

1. **Collect Form 16 from your previous employer** before you leave (or in June of the following year, whichever is sooner). Ask HR specifically for Part A and Part B.
2. **Join your new employer and immediately inform them of your previous salary.** Submit a letter or declaration mentioning:
    - Total salary received from the previous employer
    - TDS deducted by the previous employer
    - Investments already made or claimed earlier in the year
3. **Your new employer will factor in the previous salary** while calculating TDS for the remaining months. This prevents under-deduction.
4. **Collect Form 16 from the new employer** at year-end as well.
5. **While filing ITR, consolidate both Form 16s:**
    - Enter the total salary from both employers together in Schedule Salary
    - Claim HRA or other exemptions from each period separately
    - Enter both sets of TDS credits in Schedule TDS
6. **Cross-check the combined TDS against Form 26AS** to ensure all credits appear correctly.
7. **If there is a residual tax payable**, pay it as self-assessment tax before filing.

## Summary: How to Consolidate Multiple Form 16s

| Item | How to Handle |
| --- | --- |
| Gross salary | Add up salary from both Form 16 Part B figures |
| HRA exemption | Calculate separately for each employment period using respective rent details |
| Standard deduction | Only Rs. 50,000 in total for the year, **not** Rs. 50,000 per employer |
| Section 80C and other deductions | Claim once in total (not duplicated per employer) |
| TDS credits | Add Part A figures from both employers |
| Provident fund | EPF from both employers may appear separately in Form 26AS |

## Worked Example

Kiran worked for Company A from April 2024 to August 2024 and earned Rs. 6 lakh. She then joined Company B in September 2024 and earned Rs. 8 lakh by March 2025. **Total annual income: Rs. 14 lakh.**

- **Company A** deducted TDS assuming annual income of roughly Rs. 14.4 lakh (annualised), deducting around **Rs. 25,000**.
- **Company B** deducted TDS assuming annual income of Rs. 19.2 lakh (annualised) but since Kiran did not disclose Company A's salary, they treated the year fresh, deducting perhaps **Rs. 50,000**.

Total TDS by both employers: approx **Rs. 75,000**.

Actual tax on Rs. 14 lakh combined income (old regime): approximately **Rs. 1,60,000** after standard deduction and 80C.

> [!WARNING] Shortfall of ~Rs. 85,000
> Kiran must pay the balance as self-assessment tax when filing. Had she disclosed Company A's income to Company B, the new employer could have calibrated TDS correctly.

## Common Mistakes

1. **Not informing the new employer about previous salary.** This is the root cause of almost all multiple Form 16 tax shortfall situations.
2. **Claiming standard deduction twice** (once from each employer's computation). Only one standard deduction is allowed per taxpayer per year, regardless of how many employers.
3. **Not adding up both salaries and reporting only the new employer's amount in the ITR.** This is underreporting, which carries serious consequences including penalty and prosecution under Section 270A.
4. **Overlooking EPF contributions** from the old employer in Form 26AS. These may appear separately and need to be reconciled.
5. **Assuming both Form 16s can be filed as two separate ITRs.** There is only one ITR per taxpayer per year.

## Tips

1. **At the time of joining a new company**, proactively ask HR about the process for declaring previous salary. Many employers have a standard form or portal entry for this.
2. **Calculate your combined tax liability in April itself** if you know you will change jobs. Pay advance tax if there is likely to be a shortfall, to avoid Section 234B and 234C interest.
3. **Request a salary certificate from your previous employer at resignation itself.** Do not wait until June, as companies sometimes merge, shut down, or become difficult to contact.
4. **Use the Income Tax portal's prefilled data as a check.** It often captures TDS from both employers through Form 26AS integration.
5. **Keep both Form 16s organised** with the tax filing documents for that year. Income tax notices can come 6 years after filing.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. I joined a company in February and received a very small salary for two months. Do I still need to declare it to my main employer?

Yes. Even two months of salary from a previous or second employer must be declared. While the amount may be small, not declaring it is technically underreporting of income.

### Q2. I changed jobs twice in the same year. How do I handle three Form 16s?

The process is the same. You consolidate **all three in one ITR**. Add all salaries, take the standard deduction once, claim all TDS credits from all three, and ensure the aggregate tax is paid correctly.

### Q3. Can I use my new employer's Form 16 alone if I forgot to inform them about the old salary?

No. You must report total income. Using only the new employer's Form 16 and ignoring the old employer's income is **income suppression**. File using both, pay any shortfall tax, and add interest if applicable.

### Q4. What if there is excess TDS from both employers combined compared to my actual tax liability?

If total TDS deducted exceeds your actual tax liability, you will **receive a refund** after filing your ITR. This is common when two employers each apply slab benefits independently, leading to over-deduction.

### Q5. Both my employers offered different HRA structures. How do I calculate total HRA exemption?

Calculate HRA exemption **separately for each period of employment** using that employer's HRA and your rent payments during that period. The total exemption is the sum of the two separately calculated exempt amounts.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates
